Water drop impacting a superhydrophobic surface (SHS) rebounds completely with remarkable elasticity. For such an impact, the balance between the inertial and capillary forces ascertain the contact time. This is found to be fairly constant for any macroscopically flat SHS and for a given drop volume. Recently, various studies have shown that breaking the radial symmetry during the drop impact can significantly reduce the contact time below that of a flat SHS. One such study has been performed on a cylindrical SHS with a curvature comparable to the drop. The reduction in contact time has been attributed to the radially anisotropic flow imparted by the tangential component of momentum and the elliptical footprint of the drop during the crash. Here, we perform drop impact experiments on bathtub-like SHS and show that the radial anisotropy can be triggered even in the absence of both the criteria mentioned above. This is shown to be a consequence of the lamella deflection during the spreading of the drop. The reduction in contact time is quite clearly evident in this experimental regime.

The impact of a water drop on a superhydrophobic surface (SHS) results in a complete rebound characterized by significant elasticity. In such an instance, the interplay between inertial and capillary forces determines the contact time. This contact time has been observed to remain relatively constant for any macroscopically flat SHS and for a specific drop volume. Recent research has indicated that altering the radial symmetry during the drop impact can substantially decrease the contact time compared to that of a flat SHS. One investigation focused on a cylindrical SHS with a curvature similar to that of the drop. The observed reduction in contact time was attributed to the radially anisotropic flow generated by the tangential momentum and the elliptical shape of the drop upon impact. In the present study, we conduct drop impact experiments on bathtub-like SHS and demonstrate that radial anisotropy can occur even in the absence of the aforementioned conditions. This phenomenon is attributed to the deflection of the lamella during the spreading phase of the drop. The reduction in contact time is clearly observable in this experimental setup.

When a water drop hits a superhydrophobic surface, it bounces back with impressive springiness. The way the drop behaves is influenced by the balance between its weight and the surface tension, which determines how long it stays in contact with the surface. This contact time stays pretty steady for flat superhydrophobic surfaces and drops of the same size. Recently, researchers have discovered that changing the shape of the surface during the drop’s impact can lower the contact time compared to flat surfaces. One study looked at a cylindrical superhydrophobic surface, which had a curve similar to the drop itself. They found that the shorter contact time was due to the way the water moved in different directions and the shape the drop made when it landed. In our experiments on bathtub-like superhydrophobic surfaces, we found that this uneven flow can happen even without the two conditions mentioned before. We observed that this was because of the way the water spread out when it hit the surface. The shorter contact time was very clear in our tests.

The phenomenon of a water droplet's collision with a superhydrophobic surface (SHS) unfolds as a remarkable display of elastic rebound, wherein the equilibrium between inertial and capillary forces determines the duration of contact. This temporal parameter remains relatively invariant across macroscopically flat SHS for a specified droplet volume. Recent scholarly inquiries have illuminated the profound impact of disrupting radial symmetry during droplet impact, revealing a compelling capacity to diminish contact time to levels beneath those observed on flat SHS. Notably, one such investigation examined a cylindrical SHS possessing a curvature commensurate with that of the droplet, attributing the observed reduction in contact time to the emergence of radially anisotropic flow, instigated by the tangential momentum component and the elliptical imprint left by the droplet upon impact. In the present study, we embark upon a series of drop impact experiments on SHS resembling the contours of a bathtub, demonstrating that radial anisotropy can manifest even in the absence of the aforementioned conditions. This phenomenon is ascribed to the deflection of the lamella during the droplet's spreading, with a conspicuous reduction in contact time becoming manifest within this experimental framework.

When a water droplet kisses a superhydrophobic surface, it springs back with the kind of grace one might expect from a dancer on a stage, each bounce a testament to remarkable elasticity. In this delicate ballet, the dance of inertial and capillary forces choreographs the contact time, a duration that remains surprisingly consistent for any macroscopically flat SHS and a specific drop volume. But hold on—recent explorations reveal that shattering the radial symmetry during that impactful moment can slash the contact time down to a whisper compared to a flat SHS. A captivating study delved into a cylindrical SHS, its curvature akin to the droplet's own shape, unveiling how this alteration in symmetry sends ripples through the fluid, creating a whirlwind of radially anisotropic flow. The tangential momentum and the drop’s elliptical footprint collide, reducing contact time to mere seconds. Here, we plunge into drop impact experiments on a bathtub-like SHS, uncovering that radial anisotropy can emerge even without those earlier constraints. This revelation dances forth as the lamella deflects, spreading the drop like a painter's brush on canvas, and the evidence of reduced contact time shines brightly in this experimental showcase.

For probabilistic programs, it is usually not possible to automatically derive exact information about their properties, such as the distribution of states at a given program point. Instead, one can attempt to derive approximations, such as upper bounds on tail probabilities. Such bounds can be obtained via concentration inequalities, which rely on the moments of a distribution, such as the expectation (the first raw moment) or the variance (the second central moment). Tail bounds obtained using central moments are often tighter than the ones obtained using raw moments, but automatically analyzing central moments is more challenging. This paper presents an analysis for probabilistic programs that automatically derives symbolic upper and lower bounds on variances, as well as higher central moments, of cost accumulators. To overcome the challenges of higher-moment analysis, it generalizes analyses for expectations with an algebraic abstraction that simultaneously analyzes different moments, utilizing relations between them. A key innovation is the notion of moment-polymorphic recursion, and a practical derivation system that handles recursive functions. The analysis has been implemented using a template-based technique that reduces the inference of polynomial bounds to linear programming. Experiments with our prototype central-moment analyzer show that, despite the analyzer's upper/lower bounds on various quantities, it obtains tighter tail bounds than an existing system that uses only raw moments, such as expectations.

For probabilistic programs, it is generally infeasible to automatically compute exact information regarding their properties, such as the state distribution at specific program points. Instead, it is common to derive approximations, such as upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds can be established through concentration inequalities, which depend on distribution moments, including the expectation (the first raw moment) and the variance (the second central moment). Tail bounds based on central moments are typically more precise than those derived from raw moments; however, automated analysis of central moments presents greater complexity. This study introduces a method for probabilistic program analysis that automatically derives symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher central moments of cost accumulators. To address the difficulties inherent in higher-moment analysis, the approach extends expectation-based analyses through an algebraic abstraction that concurrently evaluates multiple moments, leveraging interrelations between them. A notable contribution is the concept of moment-polymorphic recursion, along with a practical system for deriving bounds in recursive functions. The implementation employs a template-based methodology that translates the task of inferring polynomial bounds into linear programming problems. Experimental evaluations using a prototype central-moment analyzer indicate that, although the analyzer computes upper and lower bounds for various quantities, it achieves tighter tail bounds compared to an existing system that relies solely on raw moments, such as expectations.

Within the domain of probabilistic programming, the aspiration to deduce precise and definitive characterizations of program properties—such as the state distributions manifesting at specific junctures—often confronts insurmountable computational barriers. Consequently, one must settle for approximative methodologies, such as establishing upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds are frequently derived through the application of concentration inequalities, mathematical constructs contingent upon distributional moments, notably the expectation (the inaugural raw moment) and the variance (the secondary central moment). While tail bounds leveraging central moments tend to exhibit greater precision compared to those predicated solely on raw moments, the automated extraction of central moments presents heightened analytical complexity.

This study introduces a systematic approach to probabilistic program analysis, capable of autonomously deriving symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher-order central moments associated with cost accumulators. To surmount the intrinsic difficulties of higher-moment computation, the method extends expectation-based analyses through an algebraic abstraction that concurrently interrogates multiple moments, exploiting the interdependencies among them. At the heart of this advancement lies the conceptual framework of moment-polymorphic recursion, alongside a practical derivation mechanism adept at addressing recursive functions. The implementation employs a template-oriented methodology wherein polynomial bound inference is rendered into a tractable linear programming problem.

Empirical evaluations of the prototype analyzer reveal that, despite its bounded approximations across diverse quantities, it achieves superior tail bounds relative to established systems reliant solely on raw moment analyses, such as expectations.

Probabilistic programs are slippery beasts, their properties often cloaked in uncertainty too tangled for exact deduction. Pinpointing the precise spread of states at any given juncture? Forget it. Instead, we chase shadows—approximations like upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds emerge from concentration inequalities, which are built on the sturdy shoulders of distribution moments: expectation, the first raw moment, and variance, the second central moment. Tail bounds founded on central moments grip tighter than those wrangled from raw moments, but oh, the labyrinthine task of analyzing central moments automatically. 

This paper strides into the fray with a fresh approach, unfurling an analysis that conjures symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher central moments of cost accumulators. To wrestle the unruly complexities of higher-moment analysis, it stretches the framework of expectation analysis into a broader algebraic abstraction—a mechanism that dances through the interconnected threads of different moments. At its heart thrums a spark of ingenuity: moment-polymorphic recursion, paired with a derivation system built to tackle the recursive beasts lurking in probabilistic programs. 

The implementation leans on a template-based technique, transforming the thorny inference of polynomial bounds into the solvable terrain of linear programming. And the prototype? A sharp blade in practice, carving tighter tail bounds than an existing system that clings solely to raw moments like expectation. Experiments bear witness: this analyzer doesn’t just walk the walk—it tightens the grip where others falter.

Within the domain of probabilistic programming, the aspiration to deduce precise and definitive characterizations of program properties—such as the state distributions manifesting at specific junctures—often confronts insurmountable computational barriers. Consequently, one must settle for approximative methodologies, such as establishing upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds are frequently derived through the application of concentration inequalities, mathematical constructs contingent upon distributional moments, notably the expectation (the inaugural raw moment) and the variance (the secondary central moment). While tail bounds leveraging central moments tend to exhibit greater precision compared to those predicated solely on raw moments, the automated extraction of central moments presents heightened analytical complexity.

This study introduces a systematic approach to probabilistic program analysis, capable of autonomously deriving symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher-order central moments associated with cost accumulators. To surmount the intrinsic difficulties of higher-moment computation, the method extends expectation-based analyses through an algebraic abstraction that concurrently interrogates multiple moments, exploiting the interdependencies among them. At the heart of this advancement lies the conceptual framework of moment-polymorphic recursion, alongside a practical derivation mechanism adept at addressing recursive functions. The implementation employs a template-oriented methodology wherein polynomial bound inference is rendered into a tractable linear programming problem.

Empirical evaluations of the prototype analyzer reveal that, despite its bounded approximations across diverse quantities, it achieves superior tail bounds relative to established systems reliant solely on raw moment analyses, such as expectations.

For programs that work with probabilities, it’s usually not possible to automatically figure out exact details about their behavior, like the distribution of states at a certain point in the program. Instead, people try to estimate things, such as upper limits on the chances of extreme outcomes (tail probabilities). These estimates can be made using concentration inequalities, which depend on certain characteristics of a distribution, like the average (the first raw moment) or how spread out it is (the second central moment, or variance). Tail estimates based on central moments are often more precise than those based on raw moments, but figuring out central moments automatically is harder.

This paper introduces a method to analyze probabilistic programs that automatically calculates symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher central moments related to program costs. To handle the difficulties of analyzing higher moments, the approach builds on methods used for averages and extends them using a mathematical framework that examines multiple moments at once, leveraging the connections between them. One key idea is “moment-polymorphic recursion,” along with a practical system that works with recursive functions. The method uses templates to simplify the process, turning the task of finding polynomial bounds into a linear programming problem. Tests with the prototype system show that it provides tighter tail estimates than an existing system that only uses raw moments like averages.

Probabilistic programs are slippery beasts, their properties often cloaked in uncertainty too tangled for exact deduction. Pinpointing the precise spread of states at any given juncture? Forget it. Instead, we chase shadows—approximations like upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds emerge from concentration inequalities, which are built on the sturdy shoulders of distribution moments: expectation, the first raw moment, and variance, the second central moment. Tail bounds founded on central moments grip tighter than those wrangled from raw moments, but oh, the labyrinthine task of analyzing central moments automatically. 

This paper strides into the fray with a fresh approach, unfurling an analysis that conjures symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances and higher central moments of cost accumulators. To wrestle the unruly complexities of higher-moment analysis, it stretches the framework of expectation analysis into a broader algebraic abstraction—a mechanism that dances through the interconnected threads of different moments. At its heart thrums a spark of ingenuity: moment-polymorphic recursion, paired with a derivation system built to tackle the recursive beasts lurking in probabilistic programs. 

The implementation leans on a template-based technique, transforming the thorny inference of polynomial bounds into the solvable terrain of linear programming. And the prototype? A sharp blade in practice, carving tighter tail bounds than an existing system that clings solely to raw moments like expectation. Experiments bear witness: this analyzer doesn’t just walk the walk—it tightens the grip where others falter.

For probabilistic programs, it is usually not possible to automatically derive exact information about their properties, such as the distribution of states at a given program point. Instead, one can attempt to derive approximations, such as upper bounds on tail probabilities. Such bounds can be obtained via concentration inequalities, which rely on the moments of a distribution, such as the expectation (the first raw moment) or the variance (the second central moment). Tail bounds obtained using central moments are often tighter than the ones obtained using raw moments, but automatically analyzing central moments is more challenging. This paper presents an analysis for probabilistic programs that automatically derives symbolic upper and lower bounds on variances, as well as higher central moments, of cost accumulators. To overcome the challenges of higher-moment analysis, it generalizes analyses for expectations with an algebraic abstraction that simultaneously analyzes different moments, utilizing relations between them. A key innovation is the notion of moment-polymorphic recursion, and a practical derivation system that handles recursive functions. The analysis has been implemented using a template-based technique that reduces the inference of polynomial bounds to linear programming. Experiments with our prototype central-moment analyzer show that, despite the analyzer's upper/lower bounds on various quantities, it obtains tighter tail bounds than an existing system that uses only raw moments, such as expectations.

For programs that deal with probabilities, it’s often hard to find exact details about their behavior, like what the possible outcomes look like at a certain point in the program. Instead of exact answers, we can try to get rough estimates, such as upper limits on certain probabilities. We can find these limits using concentration inequalities, which depend on moments of a distribution, like the average (the first moment) or the variance (the second moment). The limits we get from central moments tend to be more precise than those from raw moments, but analyzing central moments automatically is trickier.

This paper offers a way to analyze probabilistic programs that can automatically figure out symbolic upper and lower limits on variances and higher central moments related to cost accumulators. To tackle the difficulties of analyzing higher moments, it builds on methods for expectations using a math approach that looks at different moments together, taking advantage of their connections. A key idea here is moment-polymorphic recursion, along with a practical system to handle recursive functions. The analysis is put into practice with a template-based method that turns the task of finding polynomial limits into a simpler linear programming problem. Tests with our prototype central-moment analyzer show that, even though the analyzer provides upper and lower limits on various measures, it offers tighter tail bounds compared to another system that only uses raw moments like averages.

Empirical evaluations of the prototype central-moment analyzer reveal that, notwithstanding the provision of upper and lower bounds on various metrics, it achieves tighter tail bounds than existing systems reliant solely on raw moments, such as expectations.

In the domain of probabilistic programming, the quest for precise insights into the properties of such systems, particularly regarding state distributions at specific junctures, often proves elusive. The analytical landscape necessitates the pursuit of approximations—most notably, upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds emerge from the application of concentration inequalities, which fundamentally hinge on the moments of a distribution, encompassing the expectation as the first raw moment and the variance as the second central moment. It is noteworthy that tail bounds derived from central moments typically exhibit greater precision than their raw moment counterparts; however, the automatic analysis of central moments poses significant challenges.

This paper endeavors to navigate these complexities by presenting a framework for the analysis of probabilistic programs that autonomously generates symbolic upper and lower bounds for variances alongside higher central moments of cost accumulators. To surmount the intricacies associated with higher-moment evaluations, the study advances a generalization of expectation analyses through an algebraic abstraction that concurrently scrutinizes diverse moments, exploiting the interrelations that exist among them. Central to this innovation is the concept of moment-polymorphic recursion, accompanied by a practical derivation system adept at managing recursive functions. The analysis is operationalized via a template-based methodology, which transmutes the inference of polynomial bounds into a linear programming paradigm. Empirical evaluations of the prototype central-moment analyzer reveal that, notwithstanding the provision of upper and lower bounds on various metrics, it achieves tighter tail bounds than existing systems reliant solely on raw moments, such as expectations.

In the wild world of probabilistic programs, the quest for precise insights is often a mirage, shimmering just out of reach. We can’t just pluck exact distributions from the ether at any given program point; instead, we must settle for clever approximations, those elusive upper bounds on tail probabilities. These bounds waltz into existence through concentration inequalities, leaning heavily on the momentous moments of a distribution—think of expectation, that first raw moment, or variance, the second central moment, the heartbeat of uncertainty. 

While tail bounds derived from central moments often wrap around our calculations like a snug blanket, offering tighter fits than their raw moment counterparts, diving into the depths of central moment analysis can feel like wrestling an octopus. But fear not! This paper bursts forth with a vibrant analysis of probabilistic programs that conjures both symbolic upper and lower bounds on variances, along with those higher central moments, the unsung heroes of cost accumulators.

To navigate the treacherous waters of higher-moment analysis, we’ve crafted an algebraic abstraction, a dazzling tapestry that weaves together different moments, revealing the elegant relationships that bind them. Here lies a sparkling gem: the notion of moment-polymorphic recursion, paired with a practical derivation system that dances gracefully with recursive functions. Our analysis, a marvel of ingenuity, has been brought to life through a template-based technique, transforming the inference of polynomial bounds into a linear programming symphony. In our experimental playground, the prototype central-moment analyzer shines, revealing tighter tail bounds than its raw-moment-only predecessors, defying expectations and embracing the thrill of discovery!

CHAPTER XII. THE PASSING OF THE LITTLE PEOPLE

In this chapter, we explore the world of enchanting little beings. We have a list of illustrations, showing us various magical characters and scenes. 

First, we see the little river-neck of Sweden. Then, there’s a cheerful fairy from Naples saying, “God speed you, gentlemen!” We meet an elf-monarch who, quite humorously, has become a court fool. Next, we travel to the Isle of Rgen, where dwarfs delight in giving gifts to children. One dwarf borrows a lovely silk gown, while others, the Black Dwarfs of Rgen, plot some mischief. 

The Troll’s children add to the charm, and we catch a glimpse of a Coblynau, who brings a touch of whimsy. A Danish elle-maid makes an appearance, along with Bertha, the White Lady, and some playful Greek fairies. An elf-traveler shares tales of adventure. 

Brownies, who love to help with household chores, are known for their joy in doing domestic service. One Brownie particularly enjoys his bowl of cream. We learn about the demands of Pck, while an Irish Cluricaune adds a twist of fun. 

Japanese children play with Brownies, and we meet a little Fir-Darrig. The persistent Kobold of Kpenick shows his mischievous side, and we hear stories about mer-folk and an old Nix near Ghent. 

The Nickel is busy with his work, while Hob in Hobhole goes about his tasks. The Irish Pooka is a horse too, bringing more magic to our tales. We encounter the Will o'-the-Wisp, and Pisky, who chases farmers' cows. 

Red Comb is a tyrant, and the Welsh Puck brings joy as a merry night-wanderer. “By the moon we sport and play,” sing the elves with glowing eyes. 

There’s an Irish changeling, and a faint voice is heard singing softly under a leaf. “Ainsel” and Gitto Bach share their stories with the fairies. Kaguyahime, the moon-maid, and the little hunchback add to our collection of wonders. 

Finally, we reflect on the rich world of fairy stories and the capture of Skillywidden. With a warm goodbye, we cherish these little people who have filled our hearts with magic.

CHAPTER XII. THE WHISPER OF THE ELUSIVE ONES

In the enchanted realms of Sweden, where rivers twirl like ribbons of silver, a tapestry of wonder unfolds. _Frontis._ "Godspeed, noble souls!" they cry, as vibrant as the dawn. 

Behold! The Neapolitan fairy dances, her laughter a melody that flutters through the air like the first breeze of spring. And there, amid the shadows, the elf-monarch dons the motley garb of a court jester, spinning tales of whimsy and woe. 

On the Isle of Rgen, dwarfs with hearts as warm as freshly baked bread bestow treasures upon wide-eyed children, while one crafty dwarf, wrapped in a gown of silk, schemes and sways. The Black Dwarfs of Rgen, with mischief glinting in their eyes, plot their next grand escapade. 

Ah, the troll’s offspring, wild and free, frolic in the moonlit glades, while a Coblynau scurries, full of secrets and charm. "I can't linger here any longer!" the echoes of the past seem to whisper, as an elle-maid from Denmark flits through the pages of time. 

Meet Bertha, the ethereal White Lady, draped in whispers of mist, and a troupe of Greek fairies, twinkling like stars scattered across the night sky. An elf-traveler wanders, carving paths through realms unseen, while Brownie, the merry housekeeper, revels in the simple joy of a bowl of cream, a feast fit for kings. 

All that Pck asked for was but a fleeting moment of revelry. “Wag-at-the-Wa’” shouts an Irish Cluricaune, his laughter ringing like a bell, as Japanese children and Brownies share stories under the watchful gaze of the moon. 

A tiny Fir-Darrig, full of mischief, dances with the persistent Kobold of Kpenick, while the mer-folk sing their siren songs, echoing near Ghent. The Nickel toils away, unseen, as Hob in Hobhole dreams of adventures yet to come. 

The Irish Pooka, a majestic horse, gallops into legend, while Will o’-the-Wisp flickers like a lost thought, leading the way. Pisky chases the farmers’ cows, a playful spirit in the twilight, while Red Comb reigns as a tyrant, cloaked in shadows. 

The Welsh Puck, a merry night-wanderer, beckons, “By the moon, we sport and play!” Glowing eyes of elves twinkle with mischief, and an Irish changeling whispers secrets of yore. “The acorn before the oak have I witnessed,” a voice sings beneath the canopy of leaves, soft as a sigh. 

“Ainsel,” it murmurs, as Gitto Bach and the fairies weave dreams, and Kaguyahime, the moon-maid, drifts softly through the night. The little hunchback, a guardian of tales, stands sentinel as Taknakanx Kan’s legacy breathes life into the air.

THE ORDEAL OF RICHARD FEVEREL, a grand tapestry woven by the deft hands of George Meredith in 1905, unfolds in a symphony of chapters, each one a note in the melody of life’s tumultuous dance.

I. THE INMATES OF RAYNHAM ABBEY – a motley crew bound by secrets and shadows.  
II. FATES SELECTED THE FOURTEENTH BIRTHDAY TO TRY THE STRENGTH – a crucible where boyhood meets the fire of destiny.  
III. THE MAGIAN CONFLICT – a clash of wills, as ancient as time itself.  
IV. ARSON – flames lick the air, devouring innocence and igniting chaos.  
V. ADRIAN PLIES HIS HOOK – a fisherman of souls, casting lines into troubled waters.  
VI. JUVENILE STRATAGEMS – the cunning games of youth, a chessboard of dreams and deceptions.  
VII. DAPHNE'S BOWER – a sanctuary where whispers entwine like vines in the sun-dappled glade.  
VIII. THE BITTER CUP – a chalice filled with the dark brew of heartache.  
IX. A FINE DISTINCTION – the sharp edge of clarity amidst a fog of confusion.  
X. RICHARD PASSES THROUGH HIS PRELIMINARY ORDEAL – the gauntlet runs hot beneath his feet.  
XI. THE LAST ACT OF THE BAKEWELL COMEDY IS CLOSED IN A LETTER – ink and paper sealing fates.  
XII. THE BLOSSOMING SEASON – nature’s rebirth, a riot of color and promise.  
XIII. THE MAGNETIC AGE – an era pulling hearts like iron filings to a lodestone.  
XIV. AN ATTRACTION – gravity’s whisper, pulling two souls inexorably together.  
XV. FERDINAND AND MIRANDA – echoes of romance in the theater of life.  
XVI. UNMASKING OF MASTER RIPTON THOMPSON – the truth unveiled, a face stripped of guise.  
XVII. GOOD WINE AND GOOD BLOOD – the heady mix of heritage and celebration.  
XVIII. THE SYSTEM ENCOUNTERS THE WILD OATS SPECIAL PLEA – order meets chaos in a fierce embrace.  
XIX. A DIVERSION PLAYED ON A PENNY WHISTLE – the sweet sound of mischief in the air.  
XX. CELEBRATES THE TIME-HONOURED TREATMENT OF A DRAGON BY THE HERO – the age-old battle against the beast within.  
XXI. RICHARD IS SUMMONED TO TOWN TO HEAR A SERMON – the call of the city, a summons to awakening.  
XXII. INDICATES THE APPROACHES OF FEVER – the fevered pulse of change, quickening the heart.  
XXIII. CRISIS IN THE APPLE-DISEASE – rot and renewal intertwined in a bitter fruit.  
XXIV. OF THE SPRING PRIMROSE AND THE AUTUMNAL – cycles of life, a dance of seasons.  
XXV. IN WHICH THE HERO TAKES A STEP – the first faltering move toward destiny’s embrace.  
XXVI. RECORDS THE RAPID DEVELOPMENT OF THE HERO – a metamorphosis, a chrysalis bursting forth

"I don't want to fight! I'm glad it's not ladylike to fight, it scares me so!"  
"Here, baby darling, go to sister Rosie."  
Rosie gently lifted off his nightshirt and held the candle close.  
"Just because you kiss Janet McFadden, don’t think you can kiss any girl."  
Rosie looked at him with sad and serious eyes.  
She read it again by the light of the candle.  
It felt awkward to be the one Mrs. O'Brien turned to in this disappointment.  
Everyone looked at Rosie, who sat lost in her thoughts, not aware of them around her.

"Just because you kiss Janet McFadden does not imply that you can kiss any girl."  
Rosie gazed at him with very sad and serious eyes.  
She reread it by the light of the candle.  
To act as the confidant of Mrs. O'Brien regarding this specific disappointment was, at the very least, embarrassing.  
They all looked at Rosie, who sat, unaware of their presence, staring off into nothing.

LIST OF ILLUSTRATIONS  
A young woman seated on a low stool by a window, repairing a tear in her skirt (_Frontispiece_)  
"I carved those letters there fifteen years ago. Now I am to remove them."  
"A slight, dark figure emerges from the shadows and places a hand on the man bending forward."  
"‘Cormorants!’ he exclaimed. ‘They anticipate a feast of death, but they will be thwarted.’"  

[Illustration: Facsimile Page of Manuscript from THE LEAVENWORTH CASE]  
"Yes, sir,"  
Could someone have entered his room late at night, crossed through it, and stood near him without sufficiently disturbing him to cause him to turn his head?  
"Yes," she replied, her hands pressing together in visible tension.  
"Miss Leavenworth, the key to the library door is missing."  
She remained silent.  
"It has been stated that, prior to the discovery of the murder, you were observed at the library door upstairs. Was the key present in the lock at that time?"  
"It was not."  

Anna K. Green Rohlfs

Not Yet INVENTORY OF ILLUSTRATIONS A maiden seated upon a humble stool near the window, laboring to repair the tear in her garment (_Frontispiece_) "I carved those letters here a decade and a half ago. Now, the time has come to erase them." "A shadow-bound silhouette emerges and places a deliberate hand upon the stooped man’s shoulder." "Cormorants!" erupted from his lips. "They anticipate a feast of demise, yet their expectations shall be thwarted." [Illustration: Replica Page of Manuscript from THE LEAVENWORTH CASE "Indeed, sir," Could someone have intruded into his quarters under the veil of night, traversing its confines to stand at his side, without rousing him to the extent of turning his gaze? "Indeed," her hands clasped together in strained fervor. "Miss Leavenworth, the key to the library’s portal is absent." She offered no reply. "It has been asserted that prior to the revelation of the crime, you approached the library door above. Might you clarify whether the key resided within its lock at that time?" "It did not." Anna K. LIST OF ILLUSTRATIONS  
A young girl sits on a small stool near the window, stitching up a tear in her skirt (_Frontispiece_).  
"I carved these letters here fifteen years ago. Now I have to cut them out."  
"A slender, shadowy figure steps out from the darkness and places a hand on the bent man’s shoulder."  
"Vultures!" he muttered. "They expect death, but they'll leave disappointed."  

[Illustration: A Page from the Manuscript of THE LEAVENWORTH CASE]  
"Yes, sir."  
Could someone have entered his room late at night, walked through it, and stood beside him without him stirring or turning his head?  
"Yes," she said, her hands clenching tightly.  

"Miss Leavenworth, the key to the library door is missing."  
She didn’t reply.  
"It’s been said that before the murder was discovered, you went to the library door upstairs. Was the key still in the lock then?"  
"It wasn’t."  

Anna K. Green Rohlfs

A young woman seated on a low stool by a window, repairing a tear in her skirt (_Frontispiece_)  
"I carved those letters there fifteen years ago. Now I am to remove them."  
"A slight, dark figure emerges from the shadows and places a hand on the man bending forward."  
"‘Cormorants!’ he exclaimed. ‘They anticipate a feast of death, but they will be thwarted.’"  

[Illustration: Facsimile Page of Manuscript from THE LEAVENWORTH CASE]  
"Yes, sir,"  
Could someone have entered his room late at night, crossed through it, and stood near him without sufficiently disturbing him to cause him to turn his head?  
"Yes," she replied, her hands pressing together in visible tension.  
"Miss Leavenworth, the key to the library door is missing."  
She remained silent.  
"It has been stated that, prior to the discovery of the murder, you were observed at the library door upstairs. Was the key present in the lock at that time?"  
"It was not."  

Anna K. Green Rohlfs

A young girl, perched on a low stool by the window, threading her needle through the fabric’s wound (_Frontispiece_)  
“I carved those letters there fifteen years ago. Now I’m to carve them out.”  
A shadowy figure, slight and dark, glides from the gloom and rests a hand on the bent man’s shoulder.  
“Cormorants!” The word escapes like a hiss. “They come for death’s feast, but they’ll leave hungry.”  

[Illustration: Facsimile Page from THE LEAVENWORTH CASE Manuscript]  
“Yes, sir.” Might someone have slipped into his room under the veil of night, crossed the threshold, stood at his side—and still not roused him enough to turn his head?  
“Yes,” her hands clenched tight, white-knuckled.  
“Miss Leavenworth, the library key is missing.” She offered no reply.  
“Before the murder was revealed, you were seen at the library’s door. Was the key in the lock?”  
“It was not.”  

Anna K. Green Rohlfs

Mr. Ransom is waiting. He stands in a corridor and then moves into a room. The lawyer comes in. Rain falls outside. There’s a sense of elimination in the air. They talk about Hunter's Inn.

In the next part, called "The Call of the Waterfall," two doors appear. It's half-past one in the morning when the story unfolds. The term "Georgian" is mentioned. Where the mill stream runs the fiercest, a detective does his work. Anitra is there too, and the topic of "Love" comes up. Someone says, "I don't hear."

Now we move to the section about money. God's forest gives way to man's. In Mrs. Deo's room, something happens between the elderberry bushes. On the train, there is a suspicious test that leads to a startling decision. They talk about the Devil's Cauldron.

In the next part, titled "The Man of Mystery," we meet Death Eddy. Hazen is also introduced, and then she speaks. Fifteen minutes pass. Someone declares, "There is one way." But it's not yet time.

The illustrations tell their own stories. A young girl sits on a low stool by the window, mending a tear in her skirt. She recalls cutting letters fifteen years ago, and now she’s to cut them out again. A slight, dark figure slips from the shadows and lays a hand on a man's shoulder. He exclaims, "Cormorants!" They are looking for a feast of death but will be disappointed.

There’s a facsimile page from a manuscript of "The Leavenworth Case." A woman answers, "Yes, sir," and she wonders if someone might have entered his room at night without disturbing him. The tension rises when they mention that the key to the library door is missing. She doesn’t reply. They remind her that before the murder was discovered, she visited the library door. They ask if the key was in the lock. She responds, "It was not."

A young girl is seated on a low stool by the window, repairing a tear in her skirt (Frontispiece)  
"I cut those letters there fifteen years ago. Now I am to remove them."  
"A slight, dark figure emerges from the shadows and places a hand on the stooping man's shoulder."  
"Cormorants!" escaped his lips. "They seek a feast of death, but they will be disappointed."  
[Illustration: Facsimile Page of Manuscript from THE LEAVENWORTH CASE]  
"Yes, sir," it is possible that he may have entered his room late at night, crossed it, and stood beside him without causing sufficient disturbance to prompt him to turn his head? "Yes," her hands pressed painfully together. "Miss Leavenworth, the key to the library door is missing." She did not respond. "It has been testified that prior to the actual discovery of the murder, you visited the library door above. Will you inform us if the key to that door was in the lock?" "It was not."  
Anna K. Green Rohlfs

IV. Mr. Ransom, a man of patience, lingers like a shadow waiting for dawn’s first light. V. In the corridor, echoes dance, whispering secrets of the past, while in the room, silence wraps around like a heavy velvet curtain. VI. The Lawyer, a hawk-eyed predator, prowls the territory of legality, sharp and calculating. VII. Rain cascades, a symphony of droplets drumming on roofs and streets, washing away the day’s sins. VIII. Elimination, a cold, clinical act, looms like a specter over the choices we make, a razor's edge of consequence. IX. Hunter's Inn, a refuge for the weary, its walls steeped in tales of those who sought shelter from the storm.

PART II.--THE CALL OF THE WATERFALL X. Two Doors stand sentinel, one leading to the light of revelation, the other to the shadows of doubt. XI. Half-Past One in the Morning, the witching hour when truth slithers out from under the beds, cloaked in moonlight. XII. "Georgian," she whispers, a name woven with history, echoing through the corridors of her heart. XIII. Where the Mill Stream Runs Fiercest, wild and untamed, the water churns, mirroring the turbulence within. XIV. A Detective's Work, a labyrinth of clues and shadows, where every corner turned could unveil a hidden truth. XV. Anitra, a name that dances on the tongue, a melody of mystery and allure. XVI. "Love," that tempestuous force, igniting hearts and leaving ashes in its wake. XVII. "I Don't Hear," she murmurs, the weight of unspoken words hanging heavy in the air.

PART III.--MONEY XVIII. God's Forest, a cathedral of nature, then Man's, a kingdom built on greed and ambition. XIX. In Mrs. Deo's Room, secrets lie hidden like treasures buried beneath the floorboards. XX. Between the Elderberry Bushes, where whispers flicker like fireflies in the night. XXI. On the Cars, wheels clatter, carrying dreams and despair alike, a moving tapestry of lives intertwined. XXII. A Suspicious Test, the air thick with tension, as truth and deception waltz dangerously close. XXIII. A Startling Decision, like a lightning strike, illuminating the darkened path ahead. XXIV. The Devil's Cauldron bubbles with intrigue, a place where sin and salvation clash.

PART IV.--THE MAN OF MYSTERY XXV. Death Eddy, a whirlpool of fate, where lives are swallowed whole. XXVI. Hazen, a name that lingers like smoke, elusive and enigmatic. XXVII. She Speaks, her voice a melody that cuts through the night. XXVIII. Fifteen Minutes, a heartbeat in time, pregnant with possibilities. XXIX. "There is One Way," a whisper of fate, guiding the lost. XXX. Not Yet, a breath held in suspense, the moment before the storm breaks.

The Devil's Cauldron bubbles with intrigue, a place where sin and salvation clash.

PART IV.--THE MAN OF MYSTERY XXV. Death Eddy, a whirlpool of fate, where lives are swallowed whole. XXVI. Hazen, a name that lingers like smoke, elusive and enigmatic. XXVII. She Speaks, her voice a melody that cuts through the night. XXVIII. Fifteen Minutes, a heartbeat in time, pregnant with possibilities. XXIX. "There is One Way," a whisper of fate, guiding the lost. XXX. Not Yet, a breath held in suspense, the moment before the storm breaks.

LIST OF ILLUSTRATIONS A young girl, a figure of innocence, sits on a low stool by the window, mending a rent in her skirt, stitching together the fabric of her life (_Frontispiece_) "I cut them letters there fifteen years ago. Now I'm to cut 'em out," she reflects, a past entwined with the present. "A slight, dark form steals from the shadows and lays a hand on the stooping man's shoulder," a silent touch of fate. "Corm

VII. Captain Duff, at the helm of the nimble sloop "Fancy," VIII. a fate splintered by an unlucky smash. IX. "Chinks" and "dope"—the murky currency of Puget Sound smugglers. X. Shadows writhe on the waters, whispers of illicit trade. XI. A trial by fire, a crucible of chaos, a very trying experience. XII. Lessons learned in the dance of kedging, ropes taut like nerves. XIII. The chase begins—an eerie, flickering light beckons across the waves. XIV. Bonny’s invention hums to life, a contraption both clever and strange. XV. Captured! The jaws of a revenue-cutter snap shut, but XVI. the first mate and crew slip free, shadows fleeing into night. XVII. Salvation in the form of a Siwash kid, small hands, mighty deeds. XVIII. Life unfolds in Skookum John’s camp, rough edges and camaraderie. XIX. A treacherous Indian from Neah Bay, betrayal in every step. XX. Freedom chased like a specter in an exhilarating race for liberty. XXI. Mistaken identity—truth tangled and frayed. XXII. Two voyages, brief yet brimming with peril. XXIII. Alaric Todd’s darkest hour descends, a thundercloud of despair. XXIV. Phil Ryder stands tall, paying a debt with honor. XXV. Engaged to interpret for the French, words as bridges. XXVI. The climb begins, preparations for an ascent into the heavens. XXVII. Bonny takes charge, commanding chaos into order. XXVIII. On the edge of Paradise Valley, breath caught, hearts racing. XXIX. Mount Rainier beneath their boots, a god’s pedestal conquered. XXX. Blown from the crater’s rim, a tempest’s fury unleashed. XXXI. Desperation sharpens every moment, a desperate situation unfolds. XXXII. A song rises, fragile yet powerful—it saves Alaric’s very life. XXXIII. Laid low, the crew repairs and recovers, sinew and spirit mended. XXXIV. Madness on their heels, chased by a madman’s relentless fury. XXXV. A gang of loggers, rough hands and kinder hearts, offer refuge. XXXVI. Northwest logging camp—grit, sweat, and timber-scented camaraderie. XXXVII. What is a hump-durgin? The answer teases like a riddle. XXXVIII. Alaric and Bonny, fugitives again, take to flight once more. XXXIX. Bonny stumbles upon his old friend, the wandering tramp. XL. A flood of light—clarity, revelation, and the dawn of hope. ILLUSTRATIONS: Ice looms above Gibraltar, jagged and gleaming. Alaric makes his first decision, a step into destiny. "'Vell,

VII. Captain Duff, at the helm of the nimble sloop "Fancy," VIII. a fate splintered by an unlucky smash. IX. "Chinks" and "dope"—the murky currency of Puget Sound smugglers. X. Shadows writhe on the waters, whispers of illicit trade. XI. A trial by fire, a crucible of chaos, a very trying experience. XII. Lessons learned in the dance of kedging, ropes taut like nerves. XIII. The chase begins—an eerie, flickering light beckons across the waves. XIV. Bonny’s invention hums to life, a contraption both clever and strange. XV. Captured! The jaws of a revenue-cutter snap shut, but XVI. the first mate and crew slip free, shadows fleeing into night. XVII. Salvation in the form of a Siwash kid, small hands, mighty deeds. XVIII. Life unfolds in Skookum John’s camp, rough edges and camaraderie. XIX. A treacherous Indian from Neah Bay, betrayal in every step. XX. Freedom chased like a specter in an exhilarating race for liberty. XXI. Mistaken identity—truth tangled and frayed. XXII. Two voyages, brief yet brimming with peril. XXIII. Alaric Todd’s darkest hour descends, a thundercloud of despair. XXIV. Phil Ryder stands tall, paying a debt with honor. XXV. Engaged to interpret for the French, words as bridges. XXVI. The climb begins, preparations for an ascent into the heavens. XXVII. Bonny takes charge, commanding chaos into order. XXVIII. On the edge of Paradise Valley, breath caught, hearts racing. XXIX. Mount Rainier beneath their boots, a god’s pedestal conquered. XXX. Blown from the crater’s rim, a tempest’s fury unleashed. XXXI. Desperation sharpens every moment, a desperate situation unfolds. XXXII. A song rises, fragile yet powerful—it saves Alaric’s very life. XXXIII. Laid low, the crew repairs and recovers, sinew and spirit mended. XXXIV. Madness on their heels, chased by a madman’s relentless fury. XXXV. A gang of loggers, rough hands and kinder hearts, offer refuge. XXXVI. Northwest logging camp—grit, sweat, and timber-scented camaraderie. XXXVII. What is a hump-durgin? The answer teases like a riddle. XXXVIII. Alaric and Bonny, fugitives again, take to flight once more. XXXIX. Bonny stumbles upon his old friend, the wandering tramp. XL. A flood of light—clarity, revelation, and the dawn of hope. ILLUSTRATIONS: Ice looms above Gibraltar, jagged and gleaming. Alaric makes his first decision, a step into destiny. "'Vell,

Captain Duff was in charge of the sloop named "Fancy." There was an unfortunate crash that followed. The crew dealt with challenges, including some unsavory characters. They faced smugglers in Puget Sound. Life became quite difficult for them during this time. They learned important lessons about moving their boat using kedging. One night, they chased after a mysterious light. Bonny came up with an invention that proved useful. However, they were captured by a revenue cutter. The first mate and crew managed to escape. They were helped by a young Siwash boy. Life at Skookum John's camp was quite an adventure. They encountered a treacherous Indian from Neah Bay. An exciting race for freedom took place. There was a case of mistaken identity that added to the tension. They went on two short but thrilling voyages. Alaric Todd faced his darkest hour. Phil Ryder had to settle a debt. He found himself engaged to interpret for the French. Preparations were made for a big ascent. Bonny took charge of the situation. They found themselves on the edge of Paradise Valley. They stood at the foot of Mount Rainier. A strong wind blew them from the rim of a crater. They found themselves in a desperate situation. A song ended up saving Alaric’s life. They had to stop for repairs. Suddenly, they were chased by a madman. Fortunately, they encountered a group of friendly loggers. This led them to a logging camp in the Northwest. They learned what a hump-durgin was. Alaric and Bonny took to the skies once more. Bonny discovered his friend the tramp. Finally, a flood of light illuminated their path.

Captain Duff was in charge of the sloop named "Fancy." There was an unfortunate crash that followed. The crew dealt with challenges, including some unsavory characters. They faced smugglers in Puget Sound. Life became quite difficult for them during this time. They learned important lessons about moving their boat using kedging. One night, they chased after a mysterious light. Bonny came up with an invention that proved useful. However, they were captured by a revenue cutter. The first mate and crew managed to escape. They were helped by a young Siwash boy. Life at Skookum John's camp was quite an adventure. They encountered a treacherous Indian from Neah Bay. An exciting race for freedom took place. There was a case of mistaken identity that added to the tension. They went on two short but thrilling voyages. Alaric Todd faced his darkest hour. Phil Ryder had to settle a debt. He found himself engaged to interpret for the French. Preparations were made for a big ascent. Bonny took charge of the situation. They found themselves on the edge of Paradise Valley. They stood at the foot of Mount Rainier. A strong wind blew them from the rim of a crater. They found themselves in a desperate situation. A song ended up saving Alaric’s life. They had to stop for repairs. Suddenly, they were chased by a madman. Fortunately, they encountered a group of friendly loggers. This led them to a logging camp in the Northwest. They learned what a hump-durgin was. Alaric and Bonny took to the skies once more. Bonny discovered his friend the tramp. Finally, a flood of light illuminated their path.

VII. Bigot, the Intendant, with shadows that stretch long and schemes that twist tighter.  
VIII. Caroline de St. Castin, a jewel caught in the net of fate’s finery.  
IX. Pierre Philibert, steadfast as a river’s course, yet restless as its rapids.  
X. Amélie de Repentigny, where grace meets sorrow beneath an endless sky.  
XI. A soldier’s welcome, brimming with laughter and the clang of arms.  
XII. The Castle of St. Louis, fortress of pride, its walls holding secrets like cupped hands.  
XIII. The Chien d’Or, teeth bared in eternal defiance, carved into stone like a growl frozen in time.  
XIV. A council of war, sharp tongues clashing like swords drawn in anger.  
XV. Josephine, charming as sunlight, elusive as shadows at noon.  
XVI. Angélique des Meloises, a storm wrapped in silk, her smile a dagger’s edge.  
XVII. Splendide Mendax, truth and lies braided tight, a dance of dazzling deceit.  
XVIII. The Merovingian Princess, a relic of bloodlines that whisper of ancient glory.  
XIX. Put money in thy purse, a mantra that rings hollow yet drives the heart wild.  
XX. Belmont, where paths converge and destinies collide.  
XXI. Sic Itur Ad Astra, the stars calling with voices sharp as broken glass.  
XXII. So Glozed the Tempter, honeyed words dripping into poisoned wells.  
XXIII. Seals of Love, But Sealed in Vain, promises locked with trembling hands, only to shatter.  
XXIV. The Hurried Question of Despair, a cry torn from the depths of frantic hearts.  
XXV. Betwixt the Last Violet and the Earliest Rose, a season of aching beauty.  
XXVI. The Canadian Boat Song, oars slicing water, voices rising like prayers.  
XXVII. Cheerful Yesterdays and Confident Tomorrows, memory and hope arm in arm.  
XXVIII. A Day at the Manor House, where sunlight plays on polished wood and whispers linger in empty halls.  
XXIX. Felices Ter et Amplius, joy thrice-blessed and boundless.  
XXX. No Speech of Silk Will Serve Your Turn, a warning sharp as a blade’s edge.  
XXXI. The Ball at the Intendant’s Palace, gilded masks and fevered steps in a carnival of ambition.  
XXXII. On With the Dance, where laughter and intrigue spin together like a whirlpool.  
XXXIII. La Corriveau, a name that haunts, a shadow that lingers.  
XXXIV. Weird Sisters, weaving threads of fate with hands as steady as nightfall.  
XXXV. Flaskets of Drugs, Full to Their Wicked Lips, temptation sealed in glass, danger whispering from corks undone.  
XXXVI. The Broad, Black Gateway of a Lie, a threshold you cross with trembling steps, never to return unchanged.  
XXXVII. Arrival of Pierre Philibert, the air thick with expectation, destiny knocking at the door.  
XXXVIII. A Wild Night Indoors and Out, a tempest that rages both within

VII. Bigot, the Intendant, with shadows that stretch long and schemes that twist tighter.  
VIII. Caroline de St. Castin, a jewel caught in the net of fate’s finery.  
IX. Pierre Philibert, steadfast as a river’s course, yet restless as its rapids.  
X. Amélie de Repentigny, where grace meets sorrow beneath an endless sky.  
XI. A soldier’s welcome, brimming with laughter and the clang of arms.  
XII. The Castle of St. Louis, fortress of pride, its walls holding secrets like cupped hands.  
XIII. The Chien d’Or, teeth bared in eternal defiance, carved into stone like a growl frozen in time.  
XIV. A council of war, sharp tongues clashing like swords drawn in anger.  
XV. Josephine, charming as sunlight, elusive as shadows at noon.  
XVI. Angélique des Meloises, a storm wrapped in silk, her smile a dagger’s edge.  
XVII. Splendide Mendax, truth and lies braided tight, a dance of dazzling deceit.  
XVIII. The Merovingian Princess, a relic of bloodlines that whisper of ancient glory.  
XIX. Put money in thy purse, a mantra that rings hollow yet drives the heart wild.  
XX. Belmont, where paths converge and destinies collide.  
XXI. Sic Itur Ad Astra, the stars calling with voices sharp as broken glass.  
XXII. So Glozed the Tempter, honeyed words dripping into poisoned wells.  
XXIII. Seals of Love, But Sealed in Vain, promises locked with trembling hands, only to shatter.  
XXIV. The Hurried Question of Despair, a cry torn from the depths of frantic hearts.  
XXV. Betwixt the Last Violet and the Earliest Rose, a season of aching beauty.  
XXVI. The Canadian Boat Song, oars slicing water, voices rising like prayers.  
XXVII. Cheerful Yesterdays and Confident Tomorrows, memory and hope arm in arm.  
XXVIII. A Day at the Manor House, where sunlight plays on polished wood and whispers linger in empty halls.  
XXIX. Felices Ter et Amplius, joy thrice-blessed and boundless.  
XXX. No Speech of Silk Will Serve Your Turn, a warning sharp as a blade’s edge.  
XXXI. The Ball at the Intendant’s Palace, gilded masks and fevered steps in a carnival of ambition.  
XXXII. On With the Dance, where laughter and intrigue spin together like a whirlpool.  
XXXIII. La Corriveau, a name that haunts, a shadow that lingers.  
XXXIV. Weird Sisters, weaving threads of fate with hands as steady as nightfall.  
XXXV. Flaskets of Drugs, Full to Their Wicked Lips, temptation sealed in glass, danger whispering from corks undone.  
XXXVI. The Broad, Black Gateway of a Lie, a threshold you cross with trembling steps, never to return unchanged.  
XXXVII. Arrival of Pierre Philibert, the air thick with expectation, destiny knocking at the door.  
XXXVIII. A Wild Night Indoors and Out, a tempest that rages both within

Under the banner of the White Ensign, a tale unfurls—a naval odyssey amidst the clamor and chaos of the Great War, penned by the deft hand of Percy F. Westerman, whose ink has danced upon the pages of "Rounding Up the Raider" and "The Fight for Constantinople." 

Journey through the tempestuous seas, illustrated by the vivid strokes of E. S. Hodgson, as we plunge into a world of daring exploits and high-seas intrigue. 

Contents swirl like smoke on the horizon:

I. Laddie’s whispered warning, a harbinger of what lies ahead.  
II. Snared by the lurking shadow of a U-boat, lurking like a predator.  
III. The bomb nestled in the hold, a silent promise of chaos.  
IV. A night spent on neutral ground, where secrets dance like shadows.  
V. The encounter with a spy, a web of deception woven tight.  
VI. The dummy periscope, a trickery of the mind.  
VII. The ramming, a collision of fates.  
VIII. "In the ditch," a phrase filled with grit and determination.  
IX. A midnight expedition, where the heart races and danger beckons.  
X. How the landing party fared, tales of valor and defeat.  
XI. Osborne’s capture, a twist of fate.  
XII. The Turkish biplane, soaring through the stormy skies.  
XIII. The "Sunderbund's" lifeboat, a fragile vessel amidst the turmoil.  
XIV. Submarined, swallowed by the depths.  
XV. Castaways on a hostile shore, where survival becomes an art.  
XVI. 'Twixt U-boat and Arabs, a dance of peril.  
XVII. The whaler’s voyage, a journey through the unknown.  
XVIII. In the nick of time, when seconds stretch into eternity.  
XIX. Misunderstandings, whispers of confusion.  
XX. The desert wireless station, a beacon in the vastness.  
XXI. "A proper lash up," chaos unbound.  
XXII. The fouled propellers, a tangle of fate.  
XXIII. Driven to destruction, where hope flickers.  
XXIV. The chase of the felucca, a race against the tide.  
XXV. An unknown antagonist, lurking in the shadows.  
XXVI. Reunited, threads of destiny woven once more.  
XXVII. A daring operation, bold as the breaking dawn.  
XXVIII. Osborne’s reward, the sweet taste of triumph.

Under the aegis of the White Ensign, a narrative unfolds, chronicling the intricate machinations of naval warfare during the Great War, as envisioned by Percy F. Westerman—esteemed author of "Rounding Up the Raider" and "The Fight for Constantinople," among others. The tome, adorned with illustrations by E. S. Hodgson, emerges from the venerable publishing house of Blackie and Son, with branches in London, Glasgow, and Bombay.

The thematic architecture of this narrative is meticulously structured into chapters, each delineating a distinct episode in a saga rife with peril and intrigue. It commences with "Laddie's Warning," an ominous prelude that foreshadows the tumultuous odyssey to come. Subsequent chapters navigate the treacherous waters of U-boat encounters, the existential threat of a concealed bomb, and the moral quagmire of espionage. The text delves into the psychological landscape of the protagonist as he confronts the perils of a "Dummy Periscope" and the visceral chaos of being "Rammed."

As the plot thickens, the narrative transports us into the heart of conflict—an expedition under the cover of night, a capture by adversaries, and the audacious maneuvers that define the essence of maritime gallantry. Themes of misunderstanding and existential strife are woven through the fabric of the text, culminating in a series of daring operations and the ultimate reunion of characters against the backdrop of relentless adversity. Each chapter serves as a testament to the unwavering spirit that characterizes those who navigate the tumultuous seas of war.

Under the banner of the White Ensign, a tale unfurls—a naval odyssey amidst the clamor and chaos of the Great War, penned by the deft hand of Percy F. Westerman, whose ink has danced upon the pages of "Rounding Up the Raider" and "The Fight for Constantinople." 

Journey through the tempestuous seas, illustrated by the vivid strokes of E. S. Hodgson, as we plunge into a world of daring exploits and high-seas intrigue.

O masters, lords, and rulers in all lands, Is this the handiwork you give to God, This monstrous thing distorted and soul-quenched? How will you ever straighten up this shape-; Touch it again with immortality; Give back the upward looking and the light; Rebuild in it the music and the dream; Make right the immemorial infamies, Perfidious wrongs, immedicable woes? O masters, lords, and rulers in all lands, How will the future reckon with this man? How answer his brute question in that hour When whirlwinds of rebellion shake the world? How will it be with kingdoms and with kings-- With those who shaped him to the thing he is-- When this dumb terror shall reply to God, After the silence of the centuries? Edwin Markham CONTENTS I. On the Upper Deck II. On the Lower Deck III. Legends IV. Cabesang Tales V. A Cochero's Christmas Eve VI. Basilio VII. Simoun VIII. Merry Christmas IX. Pilates X. Wealth and Want XI. Los Baos XII. Placido Penitente XIII. The Class in Physics XIV. In the House of the Students XV. Seor Pasta XVI. The Tribulations of a Chinese XVII. The Quiapo Pair XVIII. Legerdemain XIX. The Fuse XX. The Arbiter XXI. Manila Types XXII. The Performance XXIII. A Corpse XXIV. Dreams XXV. Smiles and Tears XXVI. Pasquinades XXVII. The Friar and the Filipino XXVIII. Tatakut XXIX. Exit Capitan Tiago XXX. Juli XXXI. The High Official XXXII. Effect of the Pasquinades XXXIII. La Ultima Razn XXXIV. The Wedding XXXV. The Fiesta XXXVI. Ben-Zayb's Afflictions XXXVII. The Mystery XXXVIII. Fatality XXXIX. Conclusion

O stewards of dominion, wielders of power across the earth, is this the offering you lay before the Infinite? This grotesque artifact, warped and bereft of spirit, stands as your testament. By what alchemy do you propose to rectify its misshapen form; to reawaken within it the eternal spark; to restore its gaze toward the heavens and its communion with the luminous? Can you rethread into its essence the cadence of harmony and the reverie of aspiration? Will you undo the ancient stains of disgrace, the treacherous injustices, the agonies beyond redress?

O stewards of dominion, wielders of power across the earth, what reckoning awaits you when the ledger of posterity is opened to this being? How shall you respond to his mute indictment in that climactic moment when the gales of insurrection surge against the edifice of order? What fate then shall befall the thrones and the crowned? What judgment shall be meted to those who forged this man into his present state of estrangement? When this inarticulate dread at last directs its unrelenting gaze heavenward, breaking the millennia-long silence, what account shall it render to the Eternal?

O stewards of dominion, wielders of power across the earth, is this the offering you lay before the Infinite? This grotesque artifact, warped and bereft of spirit, stands as your testament. By what alchemy do you propose to rectify its misshapen form; to reawaken within it the eternal spark; to restore its gaze toward the heavens and its communion with the luminous? Can you rethread into its essence the cadence of harmony and the reverie of aspiration? Will you undo the ancient stains of disgrace, the treacherous injustices, the agonies beyond redress?

O stewards of dominion, wielders of power across the earth, what reckoning awaits you when the ledger of posterity is opened to this being? How shall you respond to his mute indictment in that climactic moment when the gales of insurrection surge against the edifice of order? What fate then shall befall the thrones and the crowned? What judgment shall be meted to those who forged this man into his present state of estrangement? When this inarticulate dread at last directs its unrelenting gaze heavenward, breaking the millennia-long silence, what account shall it render to the Eternal?

O mighty masters, lords adorned with power, rulers whose shadows stretch across the lands—what offering, what twisted craft, do you lay before the heavens? Is this the shape you mold for God’s eyes, this hulking aberration, bent and broken, its soul snuffed out like a guttering flame? How will you ever pull this warped figure upright, breathe eternity back into its hollow chest? Can you lift its gaze to the heavens once more, flood its darkness with light, or rekindle the music and dreams that once danced within? Tell me, how will you undo the ancient scars, the treacheries etched deep, the wounds that no balm can soothe? O masters, lords, and rulers, answer me this: when history’s tides crash upon your feet, when rebellion rages like a hurricane tearing through the world, how will you face this man? How will you reply to the brute question he flings into that hour, his silence heavy with centuries of unshed cries? What will become of kingdoms and kings, of those who forged his misery, who shaped him into this voiceless terror? And when that long-muted dread rises to speak—no, roars—before God, what will your answer be?

Contents unfurl like chapters of a shadowed tale: the upper deck, the lower deck, myths spun in whispers, Cabesang Tales, a cochero’s bleak Christmas Eve. Basilio walks, Simoun schemes. Merry Christmas rings hollow. Pilates presides, Wealth meets Want. Steam rises in Los Baños; Placido Penitente rebels. In the student’s house, laughter strains; Señor Pasta debates. A Chinese man’s tribulations, the Quiapo pair’s illusions. Legerdemain sparks, the fuse burns. The arbiter judges; Manila’s faces blur. A performance unfolds, a corpse lies still. Dreams scatter like leaves; smiles and tears mingle. Pasquinades sting, the friar meets the Filipino. Fear takes root—Tatakut. Capitan Tiago exits, Juli mourns. The high official looms. Pasquinades ripple outward. La Última Razón echoes. A wedding blooms, a fiesta roars. Ben-Zayb’s afflictions deepen. Mystery coils, fatality strikes. The tale concludes.

O masters, lords, and rulers in all lands, Is this the handiwork you give to God, This monstrous thing distorted and soul-quenched? How will you ever straighten up this shape-; Touch it again with immortality; Give back the upward looking and the light; Rebuild in it the music and the dream; Make right the immemorial infamies, Perfidious wrongs, immedicable woes? O masters, lords, and rulers in all lands, How will the future reckon with this man? How answer his brute question in that hour When whirlwinds of rebellion shake the world? How will it be with kingdoms and with kings-- With those who shaped him to the thing he is-- When this dumb terror shall reply to God, After the silence of the centuries? Edwin Markham CONTENTS I. O sovereigns, potentates, and arbiters across the expanse of existence, is this the creation you offer unto the divine? This grotesque entity, contorted and devoid of essence? How shall you ever reinstate the integrity of this form? Infuse it anew with the spark of eternity; restore its gaze toward transcendence and illumination; reconstruct within its confines the symphony of aspiration and the reverie of potential; rectify the age-old indignities, the treacherous injustices, the unhealable afflictions? O sovereigns, potentates, and arbiters across the expanse of existence, how will posterity account for this individual? How shall we respond to his primal inquiry in that fateful moment when tempests of insurrection ravage the fabric of society? What fate awaits empires and their rulers—those architects who molded him into his present state—when this mute dread stands poised to address the divine, after the prolonged silence of epochs? 

Edwin Markham

Oh, leaders and rulers everywhere, is this what you offer to God? This terrible creation, broken and drained of spirit? How will you ever fix this form? Can you touch it again with the gift of life? Can you bring back hope and brightness? Can you rebuild its song and its dreams? Can you right the wrongs of the past, the deep hurts, and the endless suffering? Oh, leaders and rulers everywhere, how will the future deal with this man? How will you answer his raw question when storms of rebellion shake the world? What will happen to nations and their kings—those who made him into what he is—when this silent fear speaks to God after centuries of quiet?

Edwin Markham

XXXIX. Conclusion

Oh, leaders and rulers everywhere, is this what you offer to God? This terrible creation, broken and drained of spirit? How will you ever fix this form? Can you touch it again with the gift of life? Can you bring back hope and brightness? Can you rebuild its song and its dreams? Can you right the wrongs of the past, the deep hurts, and the endless suffering? Oh, leaders and rulers everywhere, how will the future deal with this man? How will you answer his raw question when storms of rebellion shake the world? What will happen to nations and their kings—those who made him into what he is—when this silent fear speaks to God after centuries of quiet?

Edwin Markham

XXXIX. Conclusion

O sovereigns, potentates, and arbiters across the expanse of existence, is this the creation you offer unto the divine? This grotesque entity, contorted and devoid of essence? How shall you ever reinstate the integrity of this form? Infuse it anew with the spark of eternity; restore its gaze toward transcendence and illumination; reconstruct within its confines the symphony of aspiration and the reverie of potential; rectify the age-old indignities, the treacherous injustices, the unhealable afflictions? O sovereigns, potentates, and arbiters across the expanse of existence, how will posterity account for this individual? How shall we respond to his primal inquiry in that fateful moment when tempests of insurrection ravage the fabric of society? What fate awaits empires and their rulers—those architects who molded him into his present state—when this mute dread stands poised to address the divine, after the prolonged silence of epochs? 

Edwin Markham

XXXIX. Conclusion

O you, the masters, the lords, the rulers, reigning over all lands, is this grotesque creation your offering to God? This distorted abomination, drained of spirit and life? How, pray tell, will you ever untangle this twisted form? Will you dare to touch it anew with the spark of immortality? Will you breathe back into it the upward gaze, the illuminating light? Will you resurrect the symphony, the dreams once woven in its fibers? Can you mend the ancient, festering injustices, the treacherous wounds, the agonies that refuse to heal? O you, the masters, the lords, the rulers, what fate awaits this man in the annals of the future? How will you respond to his savage query when the tempests of rebellion roar through the world? What will become of empires, of kings—those who sculpted him into this wretched being? When this voiceless dread stands before God, after centuries of hushed despair, how will you face the judgment? 

The future looms like a storm, swirling and wild, and your hands, heavy with the weight of history, tremble at the thought. The echoes of your actions will resonate in the corridors of time, as the silent cries of the oppressed rise from the ashes. Will you confront the specter of your creation, this man forged in the fires of suffering, when the world demands answers, when the scales of justice finally tip? What will you say when the winds of change howl through your castles, rattling your crowns, and the truth, like a phoenix, rises from the depths of despair?

CHAPTERS THAT SWELL LIKE A DARK TIDE:  
I. **At the Eleventh Hour** — The clock’s last gasp, the edge of destiny, 1  
II. **At War with Society** — A rebellion smolders, a world turned enemy, 8  
III. **A Friendly Chat** — Words like a bridge, or perhaps a trap, 16  
IV. **The House on Clapham Common** — A sanctuary shrouded in whispers, 23  
V. **The Inner Circle** — Where secrets spiral inward and trust tightens, 30  
VI. **New Friends** — Strangers become comrades, strangers become fate, 37  
VII. **The Daughter of Natas** — A name heavy as prophecy, a daughter born of storms, 46  
VIII. **Learning the Part** — The stage is vast, the role is treacherous, 54  
IX. **The Beginning of Sorrows** — Grief’s first tremor, a prelude to ruin, 63  
X. **The "Ariel"** — Wings of ambition, iron against the sky, 70  
XI. **First Blood** — The crimson seal of conflict, 78  
XII. **In the Master's Name** — Authority commands, shadows obey, 85  
XIII. **For Life or Death** — A wager with mortality, 91  
XIV. **The Psychological Moment** — When time collapses into choice, 98  
XV. **A Voyage of Discovery** — Horizons unravel, truths emerge, 103  
XVI. **A Wooing in Mid-Air** — Love dances where clouds gather, 110  
XVII. **Aeria Felix** — Joy in the heights, serenity kissed by wind, 119  
XVIII. **A Navy of the Future** — War reimagined, destruction refined, 127  
XIX. **The Eve of Battle** — Silence thick with foreboding, 135  
XX. **Between Two Lives** — The fork in the road, the pull of two worlds, 141  
XXI. **Just in Time** — A breath spared, catastrophe averted, 153  
XXII. **Armed Neutrality** — Poised on the knife’s edge, 162  
XXIII. **A Battle in the Night** — Darkness erupts, stars bear witness, 169  
XXIV. **The New Warfare** — Innovation’s grim face, 179  
XXV. **The Heralds of Disaster** — Ominous echoes, destruction’s emissaries, 188  
XXVI. **An Interlude** — The eye of the storm, fleeting peace, 193  
XXVII. **On the Track of Treason** — Betrayal leaves footprints, 201  
XXVIII. **A Skirmish in the Clouds** — Chaos dances among the heavens, 208  
XXIX. **An Embassy from the Sky** — Diplomacy draped in thunder, 216  
XXX. **At Close Quarters** — Conflict pressed to the bone, 225  
XXXI. **A Russian Raid** — A northern tempest breaks, 233  
XXXII. **The End of the Chase** — Pursuit collapses, finality surges, 241  
XXXIII. **The Breaking of the Charm** — Illusions shatter, truths bare themselves, 247  
XXXIV. **The Path of Conquest** — Footsteps through fire

BOOK THE THIRD.

But the real historic strength of England, physical and moral, has never had anything to do with this athletic specialism; it has been rather hindered by it. Somebody said that the Battle of Waterloo was won on Eton playing-fields. It was a particularly unfortunate remark, for the English contribution to the victory of Waterloo depended very much more than is common in victories upon the steadiness of the rank and file in an almost desperate situation. The Battle of Waterloo was won by the stubbornness of the common soldier—that is to say, it was won by the man who had never been to Eton. It was absurd to say that Waterloo was won on Eton cricket-fields. But it might have been fairly said that Waterloo was won on the village green, where clumsy boys played a very clumsy cricket. In a word, it was the average of the nation that was strong, and athletic glories do not indicate much about the average of a nation. Waterloo was not won by good cricket-players. But Waterloo was won by bad cricket-players, by a mass of men who had some minimum of athletic instincts and habits. It is a good sign in a nation when such things are done badly. It shows that all the people are doing them. And it is a bad sign in a nation when such things are done very well, for it shows that only a few experts and eccentrics are doing them, and that the nation is merely looking on. Suppose that whenever we heard of walking in England it always meant walking forty-five miles a day without fatigue. We should be perfectly certain that only a few men were walking at all, and that all the other British subjects were being wheeled about in Bath-chairs. But if when we hear of walking it means slow walking, painful walking, and frequent fatigue, then we know that the mass of the nation still is walking. We know that England is still literally on its feet.

The enduring historical strength of England, both physical and moral, has not been associated with a focus on athletic specialization; in fact, it has likely been impeded by such a focus. A certain remark, often quoted, claims that the Battle of Waterloo was won on the playing fields of Eton. This statement is particularly misleading, as the English contribution to the victory at Waterloo was far more dependent than usual on the resilience and composure of the ordinary soldiers in an extremely precarious situation. The outcome at Waterloo owed its success to the tenacity of the common soldier—individuals who had not attended Eton. To suggest that the victory was achieved on Eton’s cricket fields is plainly incorrect. However, it could be more accurately argued that the triumph was rooted in the village greens, where unpolished young men engaged in equally unpolished games of cricket. In essence, the strength of England derived from the general population, and the achievements of elite athletes reveal little about this broader national character.

Waterloo was not won by individuals excelling at cricket, but rather by those who played it poorly—a group of men who nonetheless possessed a basic level of physical aptitude and familiarity with athletic practices. When such activities are performed inadequately by a wide range of people, it is indicative of a healthy national culture, as it shows broad participation. Conversely, when such activities are executed with exceptional skill by a select few, it reflects a troubling dynamic in which only a small group of specialists and enthusiasts are active, while the majority merely observes.

To illustrate this point, imagine that reports of walking in England exclusively referred to individuals capable of walking forty-five miles per day without difficulty. Such a scenario would suggest that only a small fraction of the population was engaging in walking, while the remainder relied on mobility aids. In contrast, if walking is described as slow, laborious, and accompanied by frequent fatigue, this demonstrates that the general population remains engaged in the activity. In such a case, it can be confidently stated that England, in a literal sense, remains on its feet.
