The outcome at Waterloo owed its success to the tenacity of the common soldier—individuals who had not attended Eton. To suggest that the victory was achieved on Eton’s cricket fields is plainly incorrect. However, it could be more accurately argued that the triumph was rooted in the village greens, where unpolished young men engaged in equally unpolished games of cricket. In essence, the strength of England derived from the general population, and the achievements of elite athletes reveal little about this broader national character.

Waterloo was not won by individuals excelling at cricket, but rather by those who played it poorly—a group of men who nonetheless possessed a basic level of physical aptitude and familiarity with athletic practices. When such activities are performed inadequately by a wide range of people, it is indicative of a healthy national culture, as it shows broad participation. Conversely, when such activities are executed with exceptional skill by a select few, it reflects a troubling dynamic in which only a small group of specialists and enthusiasts are active, while the majority merely observes.

To illustrate this point, imagine that reports of walking in England exclusively referred to individuals capable of walking forty-five miles per day without difficulty. Such a scenario would suggest that only a small fraction of the population was engaging in walking, while the remainder relied on mobility aids. In contrast, if walking is described as slow, laborious, and accompanied by frequent fatigue, this demonstrates that the general population remains engaged in the activity. In such a case, it can be confidently stated that England, in a literal sense, remains on its feet.

The enduring vitality of England, both in its corporeal resilience and its moral fortitude, has never owed its essence to the narrow precincts of athletic elitism; indeed, it has often found such specialization more an impediment than an aid. The oft-quoted assertion that the Battle of Waterloo was won upon the playing fields of Eton is a rhetorical misstep of notable magnitude, for the decisive English contribution to that momentous clash rested upon the unyielding constancy of the ordinary infantryman, navigating the precipice of catastrophe with remarkable composure. Thus, Waterloo was not secured by the privileged denizens of Eton but by the tenacity of those who had never set foot within its hallowed grounds.

To attribute the victory to the cricketing finesse of Eton’s manicured pitches is patently absurd. A more defensible claim might place the laurels upon the rough-hewn village greens, where ungainly lads engaged in the imperfect rituals of an even clumsier cricket. In essence, it was the unassuming robustness of the nation’s average citizen that proved decisive—a robustness unheralded by the spectacle of athletic triumphs, which illuminate only the apex and not the base of the societal pyramid. Waterloo, then, was not the conquest of consummate sportsmen, but of imperfect ones—a legion of individuals whose athletic endeavors were marked not by excellence but by sufficiency. This mediocrity is paradoxically a sign of national health, for it implies that participation is widespread and democratic, rather than confined to an insular cadre of virtuosos.

Conversely, when excellence in a physical activity becomes the exclusive domain of a select few, the nation itself is relegated to the passive role of spectatorship, its vitality diminished by its reliance on specialists. Imagine a hypothetical England where the very mention of walking conjured feats of covering forty-five miles a day without weariness. Such a scenario would not herald a nation of walkers but rather one in which walking had been monopolized by an elite minority, while the rest of the populace languished in sedentary dependence, carted about in Bath-chairs. By contrast, an England wherein walking is slow, laborious, and marked by frequent fatigue is an England still collectively ambulatory—still, in the most literal sense, standing on its own feet.

England’s real strength, both in its spirit and its physical endurance, has never been tied to a focus on athletic excellence. In fact, this obsession with sports might have held it back in some ways. There’s a famous saying that the Battle of Waterloo was won on Eton’s playing fields, but it’s not a very accurate statement. The English success at Waterloo actually came down to the grit and resilience of ordinary soldiers, standing firm in an almost hopeless situation. These soldiers weren’t Eton boys. They were everyday men who likely never set foot on an elite cricket field. It’s silly to say Waterloo was won by polished cricketers from Eton. If anything, it would make more sense to say it was won on the village green, where rough-and-tumble boys played awkward, scrappy games of cricket. 

The real strength of the country lies in its average citizens. Athletic greatness in a few doesn’t say much about the whole nation. Waterloo wasn’t won by skilled athletes—it was won by men who might have been lousy at cricket but had just enough of an athletic streak to persevere. This sort of widespread, imperfect participation in sports and physical activity is actually a good sign. It shows that these things aren’t just the domain of specialists; they’re part of everyday life for regular people. On the other hand, when sports are done exceptionally well by only a few experts, it suggests that the rest of the nation has become spectators rather than participants.

Imagine if walking in England always meant someone walking forty-five miles a day effortlessly. That would tell us most people weren’t walking at all; they’d probably be riding around in wheelchairs or carriages. But if walking in England means slow, clumsy walking, with plenty of rest stops, that’s actually encouraging. It means the majority of people are still on their feet, moving under their own power. And as long as that’s true, England remains strong, grounded, and alive.

England’s true muscle—its grit and moral marrow—has never sprung from the sleek sinews of athletic prowess. In truth, such polished sporting specialization may have done more to hobble its essence than bolster it. That famous quip about Waterloo being won on Eton’s playing-fields? A remark as ill-fitting as a silk hat on a scarecrow. For the English triumph at Waterloo owed its salvation not to elite finesse or aristocratic swagger, but to the dogged, mud-streaked determination of common men holding the line in a near-hopeless fray. It was the rank and file, steadfast and unyielding, who carved victory from the jaws of chaos—the very men who had never set foot on Eton’s manicured lawns.

To say the battle was won on the cricket fields of Eton is laughable. But to whisper that it was won on the scruffy village green, where gawky lads swung at clumsy balls in awkward arcs? That comes closer to truth. Waterloo was not the triumph of lithe athletes and practiced precision. No, it was the triumph of fumblers and triers, of men who could muster just enough athletic instinct to keep their bodies moving forward when the world buckled around them. And here lies a curious truth: a nation’s strength blooms not in its moments of sporting perfection, but in the hearty imperfection of its masses.

Imagine an England where every mention of walking conjured visions of marathon strides—forty-five miles a day without breaking a sweat. Wouldn’t that reveal a land where only a select few bothered to walk at all, while the rest lounged in wheeled chairs, coddled and inert? No, the pulse of the nation beats strongest when walking means trudging, faltering, feeling the ache of effort in every joint. When the slow walkers and weary climbers are many, England still stands firmly on its feet. A nation of experts dazzles, but a nation of participants endures.

Somebody said that the Battle of Waterloo was won on Eton playing-fields. We should be perfectly certain that only a few men were walking at all, and that all the other British subjects were being wheeled about in Bath-chairs. We know that England is still literally on its feet.

Conversely, it is a negative indicator when such activities are executed exceptionally well, since this implies that only a small group of experts or eccentrics are involved, while the general population remains passive. If discussions about walking in England were to imply that it involved consistently walking forty-five miles a day without fatigue, we would conclude that only a select few individuals were walking, while the majority of British citizens were being transported in chairs. However, if walking refers to slow, laborious walking that often results in fatigue, we can infer that the majority of the population is still engaged in walking. This indicates that England remains literally on its feet.

However, if walking is described as slow, tiring, and challenging, we can be assured that most of the nation is still on its feet, still participating. This means that England is still literally standing strong.

Ah, the true soul of England—both muscle and spirit—has never danced in the limelight of athletic prowess; in fact, it has often stumbled over it. Someone once quipped that the Battle of Waterloo was won on the hallowed grounds of Eton's playing fields. What a woefully misguided remark! The English triumph at Waterloo was, in truth, a testament to the unwavering grit of the rank and file, those steadfast souls caught in a maelstrom of desperation. It was the stubborn resolve of the common soldier—the chap who never set foot on Eton’s manicured lawns—that turned the tide. To claim that victory belonged to the cricket fields of Eton is as absurd as saying the sun rises in the West! 

No, the real theatre of triumph unfolded on the village green, where gangly boys swung at an unwieldy cricket ball with all the finesse of a lumbering bear. The heart of the nation pulsed strongest in the average, the everyday. Athletic triumphs, glittering and grand, reveal little about the pulse of a populace. Waterloo was not won by the polished strokes of expert cricketers; it was won by the clumsy, the unrefined—by a sea of men who, despite their lack of finesse, possessed the barest flicker of athletic instinct. 

What a glorious sign for a nation when such endeavors are performed poorly! It means that the many are engaged, that the spirit of the land is alive and kicking. Conversely, when these same pursuits are executed with dazzling precision, it whispers of a disheartening truth—that only a select few, the quirky virtuosos, are partaking, while the rest of the nation merely spectates from the sidelines. Imagine if our notion of walking in England were always tethered to tales of men striding forty-five miles a day with effortless grace. That would scream of a populace too languid to even lift a foot, relegated to the comforts of Bath chairs. But when we hear of walking, let it be the slow shuffle, the aching footfalls, the weariness that tells us the great mass is still on its feet, still striding across the landscape of life. England, in all its splendid imperfection, remains grounded, ever so literally on its feet.

Neither make too much of flaws and occasional overstatements. Some persons seem to think that absolute truth, in the form of rigidly stated propositions, is all that conversation admits. This is precisely as if a musician should insist on having nothing but perfect chords and simple melodies,—no diminished fifths, no flat sevenths, no flourishes, on any account. Now it is fair to say, that, just as music must have all these, so conversation must have its partial truths, its embellished truths, its exaggerated truths. It is in its higher forms an artistic product, and admits the ideal element as much as pictures or statues. One man who is a little too literal can spoil the talk of a whole tableful of men of *esprit*.—"Yes," you say, "but who wants to hear fanciful people's nonsense? Put the facts to it, and then see where it is!"—Certainly, if a man is too fond of paradox,—if he is flighty and empty,—if, instead of striking those fifths and sevenths, those harmonious discords, often so much better than the twinned octaves, in the music of thought,—if, instead of striking these, he jangles the chords, stick a fact into him like a stiletto. But remember that talking is one of the fine arts,—the noblest, the most important, and the most difficult,—and that its fluent harmonies may be spoiled by the intrusion of a single harsh note. Therefore conversation which is suggestive rather than argumentative, which lets out the most of each talker's results of thought, is commonly the pleasantest and the most profitable. It is not easy, at the best, for two persons talking together to make the most of each other's thoughts, there are so many of them.

Do not elevate the imperfections of discourse to undue prominence, nor fastidiously condemn its occasional hyperboles. There exists a peculiar breed of interlocutors who appear to believe that the sole admissible currency in conversation is the austere coinage of immutable truths, minted in the form of rigorously precise propositions. This fixation is as misguided as a musician's insistence on confining themselves to unblemished harmonies and unadorned melodies—eschewing diminished fifths, flattened sevenths, and all ornamental flourishes, as though these were blasphemies against the tonal sanctity of music. Yet, just as the art of music is enriched by such complexities, so too does conversation thrive on its partial truths, its artfully augmented truths, and even its deliberate exaggerations. At its apex, conversation transcends mere utilitarian exchange and ascends to the status of an art form, imbued with an idealistic dimension no less vital than that animating great paintings or sculptures.

The presence of one excessively literal mind, however, can disrupt the delicate equilibrium of an otherwise sparkling dialogue, reducing a symphony of *esprit* to a discordant monotony. “Yes,” one might object, “but who has patience for whimsical absurdities? Ground it in facts, and then see what remains!” Certainly, if a conversationalist becomes enamored of empty paradoxes or flits about thoughtlessly, jangling conceptual chords rather than producing resonant harmonies—those enriching dissonances, like fifths and sevenths, which often surpass the sterile perfection of octaves—then, by all means, pierce their folly with the stiletto of fact. Yet one must also remember that conversation, far from a mechanical exchange of data, is a consummate art—arguably the most exalted, consequential, and intricate of all. Its delicate symmetries can be shattered by the intrusion of even a single graceless note.

Thus, the most fruitful dialogues are those suffused with suggestion rather than contention, those which elicit the fullest distillation of thought from each participant. For even in the most favorable conditions, it remains a formidable challenge for two minds to interweave their reflections harmoniously—so abundant are the strands of thought that clamor for attention.

Do not elevate the imperfections of discourse to undue prominence, nor fastidiously condemn its occasional hyperboles. There exists a peculiar breed of interlocutors who appear to believe that the sole admissible currency in conversation is the austere coinage of immutable truths, minted in the form of rigorously precise propositions. This fixation is as misguided as a musician's insistence on confining themselves to unblemished harmonies and unadorned melodies—eschewing diminished fifths, flattened sevenths, and all ornamental flourishes, as though these were blasphemies against the tonal sanctity of music. Yet, just as the art of music is enriched by such complexities, so too does conversation thrive on its partial truths, its artfully augmented truths, and even its deliberate exaggerations. It is unwise to place excessive emphasis on minor imperfections or occasional exaggerations in conversation. Some individuals seem to believe that discussions must adhere exclusively to absolute truths expressed through strictly defined statements. This approach is comparable to a musician insisting on limiting compositions to flawless chords and straightforward melodies, disallowing diminished fifths, flat sevenths, or ornamental flourishes under any circumstances. However, just as music derives richness and variety from such elements, conversation also benefits from partial truths, embellished truths, and even exaggerated truths. In its more refined forms, conversation is an art form that accommodates idealized elements, much like painting or sculpture.

A person who is overly literal can disrupt the dynamic of an entire group of intellectually engaging individuals. You might argue, "But who wants to listen to imaginative nonsense? Subject it to factual scrutiny and see how it holds up!" Admittedly, if someone indulges excessively in paradox, appears frivolous, or lacks depth—if, instead of producing those thought-provoking harmonies akin to the fifths and sevenths in music, they create dissonance—then it is appropriate to confront them with facts to restore balance. Yet, one must also recognize that conversation is a sophisticated art form, arguably the most significant and challenging. It thrives on fluid exchanges of ideas, which can be disrupted by the introduction of a single discordant note.

For this reason, conversations that are suggestive rather than overtly argumentative, and that encourage participants to share the fullest extent of their thoughts, are generally the most engaging and rewarding. Even under ideal circumstances, it is inherently difficult for two individuals to fully explore and benefit from each other's ideas, given the sheer abundance and complexity of those ideas.

Don’t let flaws or the occasional overreach loom larger than life. Some folks seem to think that absolute truth, pinned down in rigid little boxes of statements, is the only thing conversation can bear. That’s like a musician demanding nothing but clean, polished chords and simple, obedient melodies—no daring diminished fifths, no wild flat sevenths, no extravagant flourishes allowed. But let’s be honest: just as music needs those offbeat notes to breathe, conversation thrives on its own unorthodox rhythms—partial truths, embroidered truths, truths stretched like taffy. In its finest forms, it’s a work of art, no less shaped by imagination than a painted canvas or a sculpted figure.

A single too-literal soul can suck the air out of a whole room full of quick-witted minds. “Yes,” you might say, “but who wants to listen to the nonsense of fanciful people? Pin it down with facts and see what survives!” Fair enough—if someone is too tangled up in paradoxes, floating untethered and hollow, jangling thoughts instead of striking those rich fifths and brave sevenths, those discordant harmonies that often outshine the neat octaves, then by all means, jab a fact into him like a blade. But don’t forget: talking is a high art—the grandest, the deepest, the trickiest of them all—and its flowing symphony can be wrecked by just one sour note. The best conversations are suggestive rather than combative, opening the floodgates to let each participant pour out the fruits of their thinking, raw and ripe.

Even so, two minds sharing the same space rarely make the most of each other’s thoughts. There’s simply too much to grasp at once, too many ideas spinning in the air like leaves in a storm.

Don’t focus too much on small mistakes or occasional exaggerations. Some people seem to believe that every conversation must stick to rigid truths, like rules set in stone. But that’s like saying music should only have perfect chords and simple tunes—no playful flourishes, no unexpected notes. The truth is, just as music needs variety, so does conversation. It thrives on partial truths, creative twists, and even a bit of exaggeration. At its best, talking is a kind of art, like painting or sculpting, with room for imagination.

You might think, "But who wants to listen to someone rambling nonsense? Bring in the facts and straighten it out!" And sure, if someone gets carried away with wild ideas and empty chatter, it can be frustrating. If they’re making more noise than sense, it’s fine to call them out and bring things back to reality. But keep in mind that good conversation is like music—it needs balance. Too much harshness can ruin the flow, just like one wrong note can throw off a melody.

The best conversations aren’t about winning arguments. They’re about sharing thoughts and ideas in a way that invites more. When people talk freely, bringing their unique insights to the table, the result is often more enjoyable and worthwhile for everyone. It’s not easy, even in the best of times, for two people to fully connect and make the most of their ideas together. There’s just so much going on in each person’s mind. But when it works, when the rhythm feels right, conversation becomes something special—something you remember and cherish.

Neither make too much of flaws and occasional overstatements. Some persons seem to think that absolute truth, in the form of rigidly stated propositions, is all that conversation admits. This is precisely as if a musician should insist on having nothing but perfect chords and simple melodies,—no diminished fifths, no flat sevenths, no flourishes, on any account. Now it is fair to say, that, just as music must have all these, so conversation must have its partial truths, its embellished truths, its exaggerated truths. It is in its higher forms an artistic product, and admits the ideal element as much as pictures or statues. One man who is a little too literal can spoil the talk of a whole tableful of men of *esprit*.—"Yes," you say, "but who wants to hear fanciful people's nonsense? Put the facts to it, and then see where it is!"—Certainly, if a man is too fond of paradox,—if he is flighty and empty,—if, instead of striking those fifths and sevenths, those harmonious discords, often so much better than the twinned octaves, in the music of thought,—if, instead of striking these, he jangles the chords, stick a fact into him like a stiletto. But remember that talking is one of the fine arts,—the noblest, the most important, and the most difficult,—and that its fluent harmonies may be spoiled by the intrusion of a single harsh note. Therefore conversation which is suggestive rather than argumentative, which lets out the most of each talker's results of thought, is commonly the pleasantest and the most profitable. It is not easy, at the best, for two persons talking together to make the most of each other's thoughts, there are so many of them.

One ought not to overly fixate on imperfections or the occasional hyperbole that may surface in discourse. There exists a contingent of individuals who seem to posit that conversation can only accommodate absolute truth, manifesting as inflexible assertions devoid of nuance. This perspective is akin to a musician who demands an exclusive repertoire of flawless chords and simplistic melodies—eschewing diminished fifths, flat sevenths, or any embellishments whatsoever. Just as the realm of music necessitates the inclusion of these complexities, so too must conversation embrace its partial truths, embellished assertions, and hyperbolic expressions. At its zenith, dialogue transcends mere exchange; it becomes an artistic endeavor, replete with an idealistic dimension akin to the creation of visual art. 

A solitary individual, excessively literal in their approach, can indeed obfuscate the intellectual vibrancy of a gathering of spirited interlocutors. One might interject, “But who desires to engage with fanciful nonsense? Let us confront the verifiable facts directly!” This is a valid contention. If a participant leans too heavily into paradox, displaying a flightiness devoid of substance—if, instead of crafting those harmonious discords that enrich the symphony of thought, they merely produce cacophony—then it may be judicious to introduce a fact with surgical precision. Yet, one must remain cognizant of the fact that conversation is among the fine arts—arguably the most noble, significant, and challenging of human endeavors. Its fluid harmonies are susceptible to disruption by a solitary discordant note. Thus, dialogue that is suggestive rather than merely argumentative, that fosters the fullest expression of each participant's contemplations, tends to yield the most enriching and enjoyable exchanges. Given the inherent complexity of thought, it is no trivial task for two individuals to truly amplify one another’s insights.

One ought not to overly fixate on imperfections or the occasional hyperbole that may surface in discourse. There exists a contingent of individuals who seem to posit that conversation can only accommodate absolute truth, manifesting as inflexible assertions devoid of nuance. This perspective is akin to a musician who demands an exclusive repertoire of flawless chords and simplistic melodies—eschewing diminished fifths, flat sevenths, or any embellishments whatsoever. Just as the realm of music necessitates the inclusion of these complexities, so too must conversation embrace its partial truths, embellished assertions, and hyperbolic expressions. At its zenith, dialogue transcends mere exchange; it becomes an artistic endeavor, replete with an idealistic dimension akin to the creation of visual art.

A single person who adopts an excessively literal approach can disrupt the dialogue among a group of more imaginative individuals. One might argue that fanciful statements are undesirable and that one should focus on the facts to clarify the discussion. Consequently, conversations that emphasize suggestion rather than strict argumentation, allowing each participant to express the full extent of their thoughts, tend to be the most enjoyable and beneficial. Let not the flaws and the occasional exuberance of claims loom too large in your mind. Some souls wander through conversation clutching the belief that only absolute truths—rigidly etched propositions—are worthy of our exchange. Imagine a musician, adamant that only the pristine chords and uncomplicated melodies shall grace his ears—no diminished fifths, no flat sevenths, and, heavens, no flourishes! Yet music, in all its resplendent glory, thrives on the rich tapestry of these very complexities; and so too must our conversations dance with partial truths, adorned truths, and yes, even those grandiose exaggerations. It’s a vibrant art form, as much a canvas as a statue, where ideals take flight.

Picture this: one overly literal soul can drain the color from a table bustling with lively spirits. “But who longs to hear the fanciful babble?” you might retort. “Lay bare the facts, and let’s see where we stand!” Certainly, if one clings too tightly to paradox, flitting aimlessly with empty words, then by all means, thrust a fact into him like a dagger of clarity! Yet, keep in mind, conversation is one of the fine arts—noble, essential, and exquisitely challenging. A single discordant note can shatter its fluid harmonies. 

Thus, conversation that ignites the imagination instead of locking horns, that releases the full bloom of each speaker’s thoughts, is often the most delightful and enriching. At the best of times, it’s no small feat for two souls to fully capture the essence of each other's musings, for there exists an ocean of thoughts to navigate together.

Don't worry too much about flaws or the occasional exaggeration in conversation. Some people believe that only strict, absolute truths matter when we talk. It's like a musician who only wants perfect chords and simple tunes, avoiding anything more complex. Just like music needs all kinds of notes and styles, conversation also requires its own mix of partial truths, embellished ideas, and even some exaggeration. At its best, conversation is an art form, just like painting or sculpture. If one person takes everything too literally, it can ruin the lively exchange of ideas at the table.

You might say, "But who wants to hear nonsense from fanciful people? Let’s stick to the facts." And yes, if someone is too obsessed with clever twists or seems empty-headed, it can be frustrating. If they disrupt the flow of conversation with clashing ideas instead of creating harmony, then it’s definitely time to bring in some solid facts. But keep in mind that talking is a fine art—it's one of the most noble, important, and challenging skills we have. A single harsh note can disrupt the smooth flow of a conversation.

That's why conversations that are suggestive rather than just argumentative, that allow everyone to share their thoughts, tend to be the most enjoyable and beneficial. It's not easy for two people to fully grasp each other's ideas since there are so many to consider.

Then there are the sentimentalists, who are the largest class and who make the easiest work of social questions. In the study of the individual organism we know that normal physiology presents the greatest difficulties and is the essential basis for a correct study of diseases and remedies. We also know that popular knowledge of physiology is meager in the extreme, while popular notions attach almost entirely to diseases and to remedies. The same is true of society. The study of the structure and functions of the organs of society is long and difficult, and we have, as yet, accomplished very little towards it. We can hope to accomplish much only by a long study of history and a careful examination of institutions. I venture to say that no study except the highest mathematics has ever yet made such demands on the human mind as are made by sociology. We cannot make an experiment in sociology because we cannot dispose of the time, that is, of the lives of a body of men and women. We have to carry in mind a great number of variables, to weigh their value, and to deduce their resultant, although for many of them we can find no unit of measurement or comparison, and although we have no notation to help us. I think that we shall have to adopt some of these methods of the other sciences sooner or later, but at present I see no means of advancing sociology save by the cultivation of a trained judgment through the careful study of sociological phenomena and sequences.

The group of sentimentalists constitutes the largest category and approaches social questions with relative ease. In the context of studying individual organisms, it is well understood that normal physiology poses significant challenges and serves as the crucial foundation for accurately analyzing diseases and their treatments. However, it is equally evident that the general public possesses only a very limited understanding of physiology, with most of their attention directed toward diseases and remedies. The same dynamic applies to society. Investigating the structure and functions of societal institutions is a complex and protracted endeavor, and progress in this area has thus far been minimal. Substantial advancements can only be expected through the sustained study of historical developments and the meticulous analysis of institutions.

It is reasonable to assert that, apart from advanced mathematics, no discipline has demanded as much from the human intellect as sociology does. Unlike other sciences, sociology cannot rely on experimental methods because experiments would require control over extensive periods, essentially encompassing the lifetimes of groups of individuals. The discipline necessitates the consideration of numerous variables, the evaluation of their significance, and the derivation of their combined effects. This is made more challenging by the absence of standardized units for measurement or comparison in many cases, as well as the lack of a formal system of notation to aid the process.

Eventually, sociology may need to adopt certain methodologies from other sciences. For now, however, its progress appears contingent on fostering well-informed judgment through the rigorous study of sociological patterns and causal relationships.

Among the manifold approaches to social inquiry, the sentimentalists form the most populous faction, distinguished by their proclivity for simplifying the intricate. Their facile engagement with societal complexities mirrors the superficial grasp that popular thought often exhibits in relation to physiology. Within the domain of individual biology, we recognize that the rigorous study of normal physiological processes poses profound challenges and constitutes the indispensable groundwork for the proper comprehension of pathology and therapeutic interventions. Yet, common understanding remains narrowly preoccupied with maladies and their remedies, neglecting the foundational principles. An analogous intellectual myopia afflicts the study of society: the anatomy and dynamics of its institutional organs elude cursory observation and demand a protracted, labor-intensive investigation—one that we, as yet, have barely begun.

The promise of significant progress lies only through the arduous examination of historical trajectories and the meticulous dissection of social institutions. I submit that, in terms of intellectual rigor, few disciplines rival sociology, save perhaps the most abstract reaches of pure mathematics. Unlike the experimental sciences, sociology is constrained by the impossibility of orchestrating controlled trials; human lives, bound by the irreversible flow of time, cannot be arbitrarily manipulated in the service of theoretical validation. Here, the scholar must grapple with an unwieldy constellation of variables, assessing their interplay and synthesizing their outcomes, all in the absence of standardized metrics or a formal symbolic apparatus to scaffold reasoning.

While I foresee the eventual necessity of importing methodological tools from other scientific disciplines, the present state of sociology admits no shortcut. Advancement will hinge upon the cultivation of a disciplined and discerning judgment, honed through the systematic exploration of sociological patterns and the causal chains that animate the social fabric.

There’s a group of people, the sentimentalists, who make up the largest number and tend to see social issues in the simplest terms. When we study the human body, we know that understanding how it works in a healthy state is the hardest part, but it’s also the key to figuring out illnesses and treatments. Most people, though, don’t know much about how the body works. Their focus is mostly on diseases and cures. The same idea applies to society. Learning how society is structured and how it functions is a long and challenging process. So far, we’ve made very little progress in this area. To get anywhere, we need to spend a lot of time studying history and closely examining how institutions work.

In fact, I’d argue that sociology—the study of society—demands as much from the human mind as advanced math does. Unlike other sciences, we can’t run experiments in sociology because that would mean using up people’s lives and time. Instead, we have to keep track of many different factors, figure out how much they matter, and try to understand how they all come together. The tricky part is that we don’t have clear ways to measure or compare many of these factors, and we don’t even have a proper system to write it all down. I believe that someday we’ll need to borrow methods from other sciences, but for now, the only way forward is to train ourselves to think carefully and study social patterns and events with great attention.

Among the manifold approaches to social inquiry, the sentimentalists form the most populous faction, distinguished by their proclivity for simplifying the intricate. Their facile engagement with societal complexities mirrors the superficial grasp that popular thought often exhibits in relation to physiology. Within the domain of individual biology, we recognize that the rigorous study of normal physiological processes poses profound challenges and constitutes the indispensable groundwork for the proper comprehension of pathology and therapeutic interventions. Yet, common understanding remains narrowly preoccupied with maladies and their remedies, neglecting the foundational principles. An analogous intellectual myopia afflicts the study of society: the anatomy and dynamics of its institutional organs elude cursory observation and demand a protracted, labor-intensive investigation—one that we, as yet, have barely begun.

The promise of significant progress lies only through the arduous examination of historical trajectories and the meticulous dissection of social institutions. I submit that, in terms of intellectual rigor, few disciplines rival sociology, save perhaps the most abstract reaches of pure mathematics. Unlike the experimental sciences, sociology is constrained by the impossibility of orchestrating controlled trials; human lives, bound by the irreversible flow of time, cannot be arbitrarily manipulated in the service of theoretical validation. Here, the scholar must grapple with an unwieldy constellation of variables, assessing their interplay and synthesizing their outcomes, all in the absence of standardized metrics or a formal symbolic apparatus to scaffold reasoning.

While I foresee the eventual necessity of importing methodological tools from other scientific disciplines, the present state of sociology admits no shortcut. Advancement will hinge upon the cultivation of a disciplined and discerning judgment, honed through the systematic exploration of sociological patterns and the causal chains that animate the social fabric.

The group of sentimentalists constitutes the largest category and approaches social questions with relative ease. In the context of studying individual organisms, it is well understood that normal physiology poses significant challenges and serves as the crucial foundation for accurately analyzing diseases and their treatments. However, it is equally evident that the general public possesses only a very limited understanding of physiology, with most of their attention directed toward diseases and remedies. The same dynamic applies to society. Investigating the structure and functions of societal institutions is a complex and protracted endeavor, and progress in this area has thus far been minimal. Substantial advancements can only be expected through the sustained study of historical developments and the meticulous analysis of institutions.

It is reasonable to assert that, apart from advanced mathematics, no discipline has demanded as much from the human intellect as sociology does. Unlike other sciences, sociology cannot rely on experimental methods because experiments would require control over extensive periods, essentially encompassing the lifetimes of groups of individuals. The discipline necessitates the consideration of numerous variables, the evaluation of their significance, and the derivation of their combined effects. This is made more challenging by the absence of standardized units for measurement or comparison in many cases, as well as the lack of a formal system of notation to aid the process.

Eventually, sociology may need to adopt certain methodologies from other sciences. For now, however, its progress appears contingent on fostering well-informed judgment through the rigorous study of sociological patterns and causal relationships.

Ah, the sentimentalists—those vast-hearted dreamers who glide over the thorny terrain of social questions as if it were a meadow in spring. Their ranks are legion, their task deceptively simple: to pluck the prettiest petals from a bouquet of human complexity. But here’s the rub—look closer, and the roots of society, like the sinews of the body, demand a far graver labor. In the study of the human organism, we know that normal physiology is the labyrinth we must first navigate; it is the bedrock upon which we build any understanding of maladies or cures. Yet popular wisdom, ever impatient, skips straight to the drama of disease, the dazzling promise of remedies, leaving the quiet, stubborn machinery of life largely ignored. So too with society.

The organs and sinews of civilization—the intricate gears and pulleys that keep the great beast moving—are shadowed by centuries of trial and error. To grasp them, to truly comprehend their purpose and interplay, one must wade deep into history’s rivers and sift through the sediment of institutions. And let me say this plainly: sociology, that inscrutable art of human patterns, makes demands on the mind as towering as those of mathematics. We cannot toy with lives in some grand experiment; time, that merciless landlord, offers no laboratory. Instead, we are left to juggle countless variables, each elusive, each unmeasured, each slipping through our fingers like smoke. And without a notation—without the neat scaffolding other sciences enjoy—our task grows heavier still.

Yet hope remains, glimmering in the cultivation of judgment—judgment honed and sharpened by patient study, by tracing the veins of cause and effect until the shape of society’s heart emerges. Perhaps someday we’ll borrow tools from the sciences that have charted their own wilderness. For now, the work must be slow, deliberate, and unyielding.

Then there are the sentimentalists, who are the largest class and who make the easiest work of social questions. In the study of the individual organism we know that normal physiology presents the greatest difficulties and is the essential basis for a correct study of diseases and remedies. We also know that popular knowledge of physiology is meager in the extreme, while popular notions attach almost entirely to diseases and to remedies. The same is true of society. The study of the structure and functions of the organs of society is long and difficult, and we have, as yet, accomplished very little towards it. We can hope to accomplish much only by a long study of history and a careful examination of institutions. I venture to say that no study except the highest mathematics has ever yet made such demands on the human mind as are made by sociology. We cannot make an experiment in sociology because we cannot dispose of the time, that is, of the lives of a body of men and women. We have to carry in mind a great number of variables, to weigh their value, and to deduce their resultant, although for many of them we can find no unit of measurement or comparison, and although we have no notation to help us. I think that we shall have to adopt some of these methods of the other sciences sooner or later, but at present I see no means of advancing sociology save by the cultivation of a trained judgment through the careful study of sociological phenomena and sequences.

Sentimentalists represent the largest group and tend to simplify social questions. In the examination of the individual organism, it is evident that understanding normal physiology poses significant challenges and serves as a fundamental basis for accurately studying diseases and treatments. Furthermore, it is recognized that the general public's understanding of physiology is extremely limited, with prevalent notions focusing predominantly on diseases and remedies. This situation is similarly applicable to the study of society. Investigating the structure and functions of societal institutions is a lengthy and complex process, and we have made minimal progress in this area. Substantial advancement can only be anticipated through extensive historical study and a meticulous evaluation of institutions. I assert that no field, with the exception of advanced mathematics, has imposed such rigorous demands on cognitive faculties as sociology does. Experimental methods in sociology are infeasible due to the inability to manipulate the lifespan of a collective group of individuals. We must consider numerous variables, assess their significance, and derive their collective effects, despite the fact that many of these variables lack measurable units or comparable standards, and we currently lack a formal notation to assist us. It appears that we will eventually need to incorporate methodologies from other scientific disciplines; however, at this moment, the only viable approach to advancing sociology is through the development of a trained judgment informed by the thorough study of sociological phenomena and their interrelations.

In the landscape of societal discourse, the sentimentalists emerge as the predominant faction, often reducing complex social questions to simplistic conclusions. Reflecting upon the individual organism, we recognize that the realm of normal physiology poses formidable challenges, serving as the foundational substrate for an accurate understanding of maladies and their remedies. Similarly, it is evident that the collective grasp of physiological principles among the populace is woefully inadequate, with prevailing notions predominantly fixating on ailments and their corresponding cures. This phenomenon mirrors the intricate tapestry of society itself; the exploration of its structural and functional components is an arduous endeavor, and our achievements in this domain remain scant. Our aspirations for significant advancement hinge upon an extensive inquiry into historical trajectories and a meticulous scrutiny of societal institutions.

I would assert that no intellectual pursuit, save perhaps the most abstruse branches of mathematics, demands as much from the cognitive faculties as does sociology. The experimental method, so often a linchpin in scientific inquiry, eludes us in this discipline; the ephemeral nature of human lives precludes the possibility of controlled sociological experiments. We are tasked with managing a multitude of variables, evaluating their respective weights, and extracting their cumulative effects, all while grappling with the absence of standardized metrics or comparative frameworks. It seems inevitable that we will need to assimilate methodologies from other scientific fields as we progress. Yet, at this juncture, the only viable path forward in sociology appears to be the cultivation of a discerning judgment, honed through the diligent examination of sociological phenomena and their interrelations.

Some people are sentimentalists. They make it easy to talk about social issues. When we look at how a person’s body works, we see that understanding normal health can be tough. This knowledge is key for figuring out diseases and treatments. Most people don’t know much about how the body works. Instead, they focus on sickness and cures. The same goes for society. Understanding how society is built and how it functions is a long and hard journey, and we still have a lot to learn. To truly make progress, we need to study history and closely examine social institutions.

I believe that sociology demands more from our minds than almost any other field, except maybe higher math. We can't run experiments in sociology like we can in other sciences. We cannot just take a group of people and observe them for a short time. Instead, we have to think about many different factors, figure out how they relate to each other, and draw conclusions. This is hard because we often can’t measure these factors or compare them easily, and we lack a clear way to write this down.

I think we will eventually need to borrow some methods from other sciences. But for now, the best way to move forward in sociology is to develop a trained judgment. This comes from carefully studying social events and patterns over time.

I believe that sociology demands more from our minds than almost any other field, except maybe higher math. We can't run experiments in sociology like we can in other sciences. We cannot just take a group of people and observe them for a short time. Instead, we have to think about many different factors, figure out how they relate to each other, and draw conclusions. This is hard because we often can’t measure these factors or compare them easily, and we lack a clear way to write this down.

I think we will eventually need to borrow some methods from other sciences. But for now, the best way to move forward in sociology is to develop a trained judgment. This comes from carefully studying social events and patterns over time.

Some people are sentimentalists. They make it easy to talk about social issues. When we look at how a person’s body works, we see that understanding normal health can be tough. This knowledge is key for figuring out diseases and treatments. Most people don’t know much about how the body works. Instead, they focus on sickness and cures. The same goes for society. Understanding how society is built and how it functions is a long and hard journey, and we still have a lot to learn. To truly make progress, we need to study history and closely examine social institutions.

In the landscape of societal discourse, the sentimentalists emerge as the predominant faction, often reducing complex social questions to simplistic conclusions. Reflecting upon the individual organism, we recognize that the realm of normal physiology poses formidable challenges, serving as the foundational substrate for an accurate understanding of maladies and their remedies. Similarly, it is evident that the collective grasp of physiological principles among the populace is woefully inadequate, with prevailing notions predominantly fixating on ailments and their corresponding cures. This phenomenon mirrors the intricate tapestry of society itself; the exploration of its structural and functional components is an arduous endeavor, and our achievements in this domain remain scant. Our aspirations for significant advancement hinge upon an extensive inquiry into historical trajectories and a meticulous scrutiny of societal institutions.

I would assert that no intellectual pursuit, save perhaps the most abstruse branches of mathematics, demands as much from the cognitive faculties as does sociology. The experimental method, so often a linchpin in scientific inquiry, eludes us in this discipline; the ephemeral nature of human lives precludes the possibility of controlled sociological experiments. We are tasked with managing a multitude of variables, evaluating their respective weights, and extracting their cumulative effects, all while grappling with the absence of standardized metrics or comparative frameworks. It seems inevitable that we will need to assimilate methodologies from other scientific fields as we progress. Yet, at this juncture, the only viable path forward in sociology appears to be the cultivation of a discerning judgment, honed through the diligent examination of sociological phenomena and their interrelations.

Ah, the sentimentalists! The grand majority, the easy dreamers who dance lightly over the rugged terrain of social questions, painting rosy pictures while the foundations tremble below. Just as in the intricate machinery of the human body, where the dance of normal physiology presents a labyrinth of challenges, so too does the heart of society pulse with complexity. We grasp at the tendrils of knowledge, but oh, how scanty it is! Most folks cling to the shadows of diseases and remedies, like moths to a flickering flame, while the true essence of society remains shrouded in mist.

Peering into the architecture and pulse of societal organs is a daunting expedition, akin to scaling a craggy mountain, and we’ve barely grazed the surface of this vast landscape. Our hopes of uncovering the hidden treasures buried within can only be realized through a meticulous pilgrimage through history’s winding corridors and a careful dissection of our institutions. I dare say, no discipline—save the lofty realm of mathematics—has ever laid such a heavy burden on the human intellect as sociology does.

We cannot conduct experiments in this sprawling field, for we cannot toy with the sands of time, nor can we play with the lives of men and women. We juggle a dizzying array of variables, trying to weigh their worth, seeking to untangle their interwoven fates. Yet, many of these elusive threads defy measurement, slipping through our fingers like smoke, leaving us with no notation to ground our thoughts. Perhaps, one day, we’ll borrow the tools of other sciences, but for now, our only path forward lies in honing our judgment through the patient observation of sociological phenomena and their intricate sequences.

President Lincoln in a note to Mayor Ramsey once wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." A college officer in my position at the opening of the college year would not be inclined to agree with Mr. Lincoln, for half the correspondence which comes to my desk during the summer months has to do with men who either want to work, or who say they do, in order that they may defray their college expenses. There are so many of them that their correspondence becomes almost depressing at times, for I realize the disappointments and the difficulties which very many of these boys will encounter after they reach college, and their unfitness to do any definite work well. "I have been out of high school three years," one young fellow writes, "and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This man, who has given all his time to work for three years and who has done nothing more than live, expects easily to carry a college course which in itself requires most of a man's time to do justice to, and at the same time to earn his living on the side. I get many such letters from those who feel that earning one's living and going to college are in no way incompatible. So much has been written about the fellows who have started to college without a cent and who have later been valedictorians of their classes and ultimately President of the United States, or at least a member of his cabinet, that the average high school boy has little conception of what sacrifice and deprivation such a procedure involves; if he did understand he would nat so often undertake it, or he would do so after more careful deliberation.

President Lincoln once wrote a note to Mayor Ramsey saying, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." While Lincoln saw eagerness to work as a rare and admirable quality, someone in my position, managing college matters as a new school year begins, might not entirely share his sentiment. Over the summer, I receive piles of letters from young men who either want jobs or claim they do, hoping to cover their college costs. There are so many of these requests that they can feel overwhelming, especially because I know how many of these boys will face struggles and disappointments once they arrive on campus. Many simply aren’t prepared to do the work they’re asking for.

One young man wrote to me, “I’ve been out of high school for three years and haven’t been able to save any money. But I really want to go to college. Can you help me find a job where I can earn my board, room, and enough for my small expenses?” This fellow, having spent three years working just to get by, now hopes to manage both a full college course load—which demands most of a person’s time—and a job to cover his living expenses. He’s not alone, either. I get plenty of letters from boys who believe working while attending college is a simple and practical plan.

Stories of students who’ve started with nothing, worked hard, and become valedictorians—or even presidents—have inspired many high school boys. But the truth behind those tales is often glossed over. The sacrifice and hardship involved are enormous, more than most realize. If these boys understood the reality of what they’re taking on, they might hesitate before committing, or at least take more time to weigh their decision carefully.

President Lincoln, in a note addressed to Mayor Ramsey, wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." However, a college administrator beginning the academic year might find it difficult to align with Lincoln’s sentiment. A significant portion of the correspondence arriving at my desk during the summer pertains to individuals seeking employment—or claiming to—in order to cover their college expenses. The sheer volume of such inquiries often feels overwhelming, as I am acutely aware of the challenges and setbacks many of these young men will face once enrolled. Furthermore, their lack of preparation for performing any specific type of work effectively adds to the concern.

One applicant, for instance, writes, "I have been out of high school for three years and have not managed to save any money. Nonetheless, I have a strong desire to attend college. Could you help me find a job where I can earn enough to cover my board, room, and any additional expenses I may incur?" This young man, who has spent three years working without accumulating savings, anticipates being able to handle the demands of a college education—an endeavor that requires most of an individual’s time and focus—while simultaneously earning enough to support himself. Letters like this are common, and they reflect a widespread belief among prospective students that earning a living and pursuing higher education are entirely compatible undertakings.

This misconception likely stems from numerous accounts extolling individuals who began college with no financial resources, later achieved academic distinction as valedictorians, and ultimately rose to prominent positions such as President of the United States or members of the Cabinet. Such narratives tend to obscure the significant sacrifices and hardships inherent in such paths. If high school students fully grasped the extent of the difficulties involved, they might either reconsider attempting this dual pursuit or approach it with greater forethought and preparation.

Can you help me find a job where I can earn my board, room, and enough for my small expenses?” This fellow, having spent three years working just to get by, now hopes to manage both a full college course load—which demands most of a person’s time—and a job to cover his living expenses. He’s not alone, either. I get plenty of letters from boys who believe working while attending college is a simple and practical plan.

Stories of students who’ve started with nothing, worked hard, and become valedictorians—or even presidents—have inspired many high school boys. But the truth behind those tales is often glossed over. The sacrifice and hardship involved are enormous, more than most realize. If these boys understood the reality of what they’re taking on, they might hesitate before committing, or at least take more time to weigh their decision carefully.

In a brief note to Mayor Ramsey, President Lincoln once penned these words: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." Yet, as I sit in my capacity as a college officer at the threshold of a new academic year, I must confess that my experience renders me somewhat less sanguine than Mr. Lincoln in this regard. Indeed, the summer months inundate my desk with letters from young men professing—or, at least, purporting—a desire to work, their aim being to finance their collegiate ambitions. Their missives, multitudinous as they are, seldom inspire unmitigated hope; rather, they are tinged with a certain pathos, for they presage the manifold trials and disillusionments that await many of these aspirants upon their arrival at college. Most are ill-prepared to perform any specific task with competence, a deficiency that compounds their already precarious circumstances.

One correspondent, for instance, articulates his predicament thus: "I have been out of high school three years and have not managed to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room, along with the additional funds required for my other minor expenses?" Here we encounter a young man who has devoted three full years to labor merely to subsist, yet he imagines that he can simultaneously undertake the rigorous intellectual demands of a college curriculum and support himself financially on the margins of his time. Such letters abound, each predicated on the naïve assumption that the dual endeavor of sustaining oneself materially while excelling academically is a harmonious enterprise, rather than an arduous balancing act fraught with compromises.

The mythology surrounding those who have ascended from penury to prominence—valedictorians who began their collegiate journey penniless, only to emerge as Presidents or cabinet members—has perpetuated a delusion among the average high school graduate. He envisions such triumphs without fully apprehending the profound sacrifices and relentless privations they entail. Were the magnitude of these costs more widely understood, fewer would embark on such a path so cavalierly, or they would do so only after a more sober and calculated reckoning.

Abraham Lincoln, with his plainspoken genius, once scribbled a note to Mayor Ramsey: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." Honest Abe’s sentiment might warm the heart on first reading, but as a college officer staring down the chaos of a new academic year, I find myself less inclined to second his optimism. My summer desk becomes a battlefield of letters, half of them from young men who either desperately want to work or claim they do—penned, no doubt, with dreams of conquering their tuition bills. Their appeals pile up until they form a small mountain of hope and heartbreak, and I can’t help but feel the weight of their future struggles pressing back against me. There are so many of them. Too many.

Take, for instance, the earnest plea of one young man: "I’ve been out of high school three years and haven’t managed to save a cent. But I want, more than anything, to go to college. Can you help me find a job where I can earn my board, my room, and a bit extra for the other small expenses I’ll have along the way?" There it is—a simple, aching ambition. But here, too, is the crack in the dream. This boy, who has spent three long years working just to scrape by, imagines he can now throw himself into a full college course load—a beast that demands nearly all of a man’s time and focus—and still somehow earn his keep on the side. And it’s not just him. Letter after letter arrives from others who believe, with stubborn innocence, that making a living and making it through college are tasks that can be neatly threaded together without fraying at the seams.

Why? Why do so many believe this? Maybe it’s the old stories, the ones we’ve spun and polished to gleaming. Tales of penniless farm boys who started college with lint in their pockets and left as valedictorians, or who climbed their way to the Oval Office or at least a seat in the President’s cabinet. But what those stories forget to whisper is the truth: the hunger, the exhaustion, the bone-deep sacrifices such journeys demand. If these boys understood just how sharp the trade-offs, how steep the climb, perhaps they’d hesitate. Perhaps they’d think longer, harder, before leaping.

President Lincoln in a note to Mayor Ramsey once wrote: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." A college officer in my position at the opening of the college year would not be inclined to agree with Mr. Lincoln, for half the correspondence which comes to my desk during the summer months has to do with men who either want to work, or who say they do, in order that they may defray their college expenses. There are so many of them that their correspondence becomes almost depressing at times, for I realize the disappointments and the difficulties which very many of these boys will encounter after they reach college, and their unfitness to do any definite work well. "I have been out of high school three years," one young fellow writes, "and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This man, who has given all his time to work for three years and who has done nothing more than live, expects easily to carry a college course which in itself requires most of a man's time to do justice to, and at the same time to earn his living on the side. I get many such letters from those who feel that earning one's living and going to college are in no way incompatible. So much has been written about the fellows who have started to college without a cent and who have later been valedictorians of their classes and ultimately President of the United States, or at least a member of his cabinet, that the average high school boy has little conception of what sacrifice and deprivation such a procedure involves; if he did understand he would nat so often undertake it, or he would do so after more careful deliberation.

President Lincoln once wrote a note to Mayor Ramsey saying, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." As a college officer, I find it hard to agree with Mr. Lincoln at the start of the college year. Most of the letters I receive during the summer are from young men who either want to work or claim they do, hoping to cover their college costs. There are so many letters that it can feel overwhelming. I can't help but think about the challenges these young men will face when they get to college and how many are not really ready for the work ahead.

One young man wrote to me, "I have been out of high school three years, and I have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This young man has spent three years working just to get by, yet he thinks he can easily manage a college course that takes a lot of time and effort, all while trying to earn a living on the side. I receive many letters like this from those who believe that working to support themselves and going to college can easily go hand in hand.

There are countless stories about students who started college with nothing and went on to become valedictorians or even rise to high offices in government. Because of these tales, many high school boys don’t really grasp what it takes to achieve such goals. If they truly understood the sacrifices and hardships involved, they might think twice before jumping into college life or at least think it through more carefully.

President Lincoln, in a correspondence addressed to Mayor Ramsey, stated, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." As an official at a college at the beginning of the academic year, I find it difficult to concur with Mr. Lincoln's assertion. A significant portion of the correspondence I receive during the summer pertains to individuals seeking employment, or claiming to seek it, in order to cover their college expenses. The volume of such inquiries is substantial, leading to a sense of discouragement as I contemplate the disappointments and challenges many of these students will face upon their arrival at college, as well as their inadequate preparation for fulfilling work responsibilities.

One young man wrote, "I have been out of high school three years and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This individual, who has devoted three years entirely to work without accumulating savings, expects to manage a college curriculum that demands a significant investment of time, while simultaneously earning a living. I receive numerous letters from individuals who maintain the belief that earning a living and pursuing a college education are not mutually exclusive endeavors.

A considerable amount has been documented regarding individuals who commenced their college education without financial resources, subsequently achieving the status of valedictorian or even ascending to the presidency of the United States, or at least holding a position in the presidential cabinet. Consequently, the average high school student lacks a comprehensive understanding of the sacrifices and hardships that such a journey entails. If he were fully aware of these challenges, he would likely hesitate to embark on this path or would proceed with greater caution and consideration.

There are countless stories about students who started college with nothing and went on to become valedictorians or even rise to high offices in government. Because of these tales, many high school boys don’t really grasp what it takes to achieve such goals. If they truly understood the sacrifices and hardships involved, they might think twice before jumping into college life or at least think it through more carefully.

President Lincoln once wrote a note to Mayor Ramsey saying, "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." As a college officer, I find it hard to agree with Mr. Lincoln at the start of the college year. Most of the letters I receive during the summer are from young men who either want to work or claim they do, hoping to cover their college costs. There are so many letters that it can feel overwhelming. I can't help but think about the challenges these young men will face when they get to college and how many are not really ready for the work ahead.

One young man wrote to me, "I have been out of high school three years, and I have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This young man has spent three years working just to get by, yet he thinks he can easily manage a college course that takes a lot of time and effort, all while trying to earn a living on the side. I receive many letters like this from those who believe that working to support themselves and going to college can easily go hand in hand.

There are countless stories about students who started college with nothing and went on to become valedictorians or even rise to high offices in government. Because of these tales, many high school boys don’t really grasp what it takes to achieve such goals. If they truly understood the sacrifices and hardships involved, they might think twice before jumping into college life or at least think it through more carefully.

In a correspondence addressed to Mayor Ramsey, President Lincoln articulated a poignant observation: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." However, as an academic administrator confronting the onset of a new academic year, I find myself at odds with Lincoln's optimism. The bulk of my summer correspondence is dominated by the entreaties of individuals who profess a desire for employment, ostensibly to alleviate their financial burdens associated with higher education. The sheer volume of these missives, each echoing a narrative of aspiration intertwined with desperation, becomes a source of profound disillusionment. I am acutely aware of the myriad obstacles and profound discontent that many of these young aspirants will inevitably face upon their arrival at the collegiate realm, compounded by their often inadequate preparation for any substantive labor.

One particular correspondent, having spent three years adrift since his departure from high school, implores: "I have been out of high school three years and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" This individual, having devoted his entire existence to mere survival, harbors the expectation that he can seamlessly navigate the rigorous demands of a college curriculum—an enterprise that itself demands a near-total allocation of one’s temporal resources—while concurrently striving to earn a subsistence. 

Such letters proliferate from those who naively conflate the dual pursuits of self-sustenance and academic engagement, oblivious to the complex interplay between these two endeavors. The narrative of the underprivileged student who, with scant resources, ascends to the pinnacle of academic achievement—becoming a valedictorian or even a member of the presidential cabinet—has been romanticized to such an extent that it obscures the profound sacrifices and hardships that this trajectory entails. Were these aspirants to truly comprehend the depth of commitment and deprivation required, they might approach their ambitions with a more judicious and circumspect deliberation.

In a heartfelt note to Mayor Ramsey, President Lincoln penned: "The lady bearer of this note says she has two sons who want to work. Set them at it if possible. Wanting work is so rare a want that it should be encouraged." Ah, but here I sit, an officer of the college, as the crisp autumn air whispers the dawn of a new academic year, and I find myself at odds with Mr. Lincoln’s rosy idealism. My desk, a battleground for the summer’s correspondence, is piled high with letters from eager souls, young men yearning for toil, or at least claiming to, all in the noble pursuit of funding their education. 

Each missive carries the weight of ambition, yet the sheer volume of them can be a melancholic symphony—a cacophony of dreams colliding with the hard ground of reality. I can almost hear the echoes of disappointment in the air, as I recognize the hardships these bright-eyed boys will face once they step foot on campus, grappling with the stark truth of their unpreparedness for the rigors of serious work. One young man, his words dripping with hope yet tinged with desperation, writes, "I have been out of high school three years, and have not been able to save any money. I want, however, very much to go to college. Can you secure a place for me to work where I can earn my board and room and such extra money as I shall need for my other small expenses?" 

Here is a fellow who has poured his sweat into labor for three long years, merely to scrape by. And yet, like a tightrope walker strung between the lofty ambitions of academia and the grind of daily survival, he believes he can juggle both—a college course, a beast that demands every ounce of one’s soul, while also conjuring enough coin to sustain his very life. 

How many letters echo this refrain, these hopeful hearts convinced that earning a living and pursuing their studies can dance together in a harmonious waltz? So many tales have been spun—of those who, with nary a dollar to their name, have risen to become valedictorians, perhaps even Presidents. Yet, the average high school lad, with dreams in his eyes and stars in his heart, remains blissfully unaware of the sacrifice, the deprivation that such a path entails. If only they grasped the weight of that leap, perhaps their ambitions would be tempered with a touch of cautious wisdom.
