But, although palæontology is a comparatively youthful scientific speciality, the mass of materials with which it has to deal is already prodigious. In the last fifty years the number of known fossil remains of invertebrated animals has been trebled or quadrupled. The work of interpretation of vertebrate fossils, the foundations of which were so solidly laid by Cuvier, was carried on, with wonderful vigour and success, by Agassiz in Switzerland, by Von Meyer in Germany, and last, but not least, by Owen in this country, while, in later years, a multitude of workers have laboured in the same field. In many groups of the animal kingdom the number of fossil forms already known is as great as that of the existing species. In some cases it is much greater; and there are entire orders of animals of the existence of which we should know nothing except for the evidence afforded by fossil remains. With all this it may be safely assumed that, at the present moment, we are not acquainted with a tithe of the fossils which will sooner or later be discovered. If we may judge by the profusion yielded within the last few years by the Tertiary formations of North America, there seems to be no limit to the multitude of Mammalian remains to be expected from that continent; and analogy leads us to expect similar riches in Eastern Asia, whenever the Tertiary formations of that region are as carefully explored. Again, we have as yet almost everything to learn respecting the terrestrial population of the Mesozoic epoch—and it seems as if the Western territories of the United States were about to prove as instructive in regard to this point as they have in respect of tertiary life. My friend Professor Marsh informs me that, within two years, remains of more than 160 distinct individuals of mammals, belonging to twenty species and nine genera, have been found in a space not larger than the floor of a good-sized room; while beds of the same age have yielded 300 reptiles, varying in size from a length of 60 feet or 80 feet to the dimensions of a rabbit.

Although paleontology is a relatively recent scientific discipline, the volume of material it addresses has already become substantial. Over the past fifty years, the catalog of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has expanded three- to fourfold. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, a field firmly established through the foundational work of Cuvier, was advanced with remarkable energy and success by Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in England. In more recent years, numerous researchers have continued to contribute to this area. In many groups of the animal kingdom, the number of identified fossil species rivals that of extant species. In certain cases, the fossil record even surpasses the number of currently living species. Entire orders of animals, whose existence would otherwise remain unknown, have been identified solely through fossil evidence.

Despite these achievements, it is reasonable to conclude that only a small fraction of all potential fossil discoveries has been made to date. The abundance of specimens recently unearthed from North America's Tertiary formations suggests that the continent holds an immense reservoir of mammalian fossils yet to be uncovered. By analogy, similar discoveries might be anticipated in Eastern Asia once its Tertiary formations are subjected to equally rigorous investigation. Furthermore, our understanding of the terrestrial organisms from the Mesozoic era remains minimal. Evidence suggests that the western regions of the United States may offer insights into this era comparable to those they have provided regarding Tertiary life. 

Professor Marsh has reported that, within a span of two years, fossils representing more than 160 distinct mammalian individuals, distributed across twenty species and nine genera, were recovered from an area no larger than a medium-sized room. In addition, Mesozoic-age deposits from the same region have yielded the remains of 300 reptiles, ranging in size from approximately 60 to 80 feet in length to those comparable in dimensions to a rabbit. These findings underscore the potential for continued discovery and the vast scope of paleontological research yet to be undertaken.

Though paleontology strides through the halls of science as a relatively youthful discipline, the sheer heap of relics it handles is already staggering, a treasure trove that seems to expand with each passing day. Over the last half-century, the catalog of fossilized invertebrates has ballooned—doubled, tripled, perhaps quadrupled—until it teems like an ancient tidepool frozen in time. The vertebrates, too, have not been left behind. Cuvier, that titan of foundational insight, laid the bedrock with precision; then came Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen on British soil, each wielding their genius like a chisel against stone. And now, in their wake, an army of scholars sifts the dust, piecing together tales from the bones of the long-dead.

In some corners of the animal kingdom, the fossil record rivals, even eclipses, the variety of living species. Entire orders of creatures, once lost to oblivion, now step into the light solely because their stony remnants were unearthed. And still, what we know today is but a sliver of what lies waiting beneath the surface. Imagine: if the last few decades of excavation in North America’s Tertiary formations have unveiled such a dizzying bounty of mammalian remains, who can fathom the riches buried under Asia’s unexplored Tertiary layers? The analogy almost demands it—a mirrored abundance, hidden in Eastern earth.

And then there’s the Mesozoic, that shadowed age of thunder and fangs. Our understanding of its terrestrial life is little more than a sketch, a whisper, yet the Western expanse of the United States beckons with promise. Its territories, already a crucible of Tertiary wonders, now hint at secrets locked even deeper in time. My friend, Professor Marsh, shares a tale so vivid it feels like myth: in the space of two short years, he and his team uncovered the remains of more than 160 individual mammals—twenty species spread across nine genera—all confined to a patch of ground no larger than a good-sized room. And that’s not all. From beds of the same age sprang forth 300 reptiles, some stretching sixty or eighty feet, others no larger than a rabbit. A veritable carnival of scales and bones, waiting patiently for eons to be found.

Though the discipline of paleontology may trace its lineage to relatively recent scientific origins, the sheer amplitude of its evidentiary corpus has already grown to astonishing proportions. Over the past half-century, the catalog of fossilized vestiges of invertebrate life forms has expanded threefold or even fourfold. The systematic deciphering of vertebrate paleontological records—initially architected with unparalleled precision by Cuvier—found its continuance in the indefatigable labors of Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in Britain. Their endeavors were succeeded, in more recent decades, by an ever-expanding cadre of investigators contributing to this burgeoning tapestry of discovery. Indeed, within numerous taxonomic strata of the animal kingdom, the inventory of fossilized species rivals—and in certain instances surpasses—the enumeration of extant taxa. Entire orders, whose ontological reality would have remained veiled in perpetual obscurity, owe their evidentiary affirmation solely to the mute testimony of fossilized remnants.

Yet, despite the seemingly boundless accumulation of paleontological artifacts, it is a reasonable conjecture that the current state of knowledge encompasses but a meager fraction—a mere tithe—of the fossils that await future revelation. The recent profusion of mammalian relics unearthed from the Tertiary strata of North America offers a tantalizing glimpse into the potential fecundity of the geological record. This abundance invites the inference that analogous treasures lie hidden beneath the Tertiary deposits of Eastern Asia, awaiting the meticulous excavation and scrutiny that will inevitably bring them to light. Moreover, the terrestrial biota of the Mesozoic epoch remains a largely uncharted frontier. The western expanses of the United States, already an abundant source of Tertiary insights, appear poised to yield similarly transformative revelations concerning Mesozoic life.

My colleague, Professor Marsh, recounts a staggering density of paleontological yield: within a mere two years, a confined area no larger than the floor of a commodious room has disclosed the fossilized remains of over 160 individual mammals, representing twenty distinct species across nine genera. Concurrently, stratigraphic layers of comparable antiquity have yielded an astonishing array of reptilian specimens—numbering 300 individuals and ranging in scale from rabbit-sized diminutiveness to leviathans stretching 60 or even 80 feet in length. In such discoveries, the latent immensity of Earth's deep biological history gradually unfurls before us, promising revelations yet untold.

Although paleontology is a relatively recent scientific discipline, the volume of material it addresses has already become substantial. Over the past fifty years, the catalog of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has expanded three- to fourfold. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, a field firmly established through the foundational work of Cuvier, was advanced with remarkable energy and success by Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in England. In more recent years, numerous researchers have continued to contribute to this area. In many groups of the animal kingdom, the number of identified fossil species rivals that of extant species. In certain cases, the fossil record even surpasses the number of currently living species. Entire orders of animals, whose existence would otherwise remain unknown, have been identified solely through fossil evidence.

Despite these achievements, it is reasonable to conclude that only a small fraction of all potential fossil discoveries has been made to date. The abundance of specimens recently unearthed from North America's Tertiary formations suggests that the continent holds an immense reservoir of mammalian fossils yet to be uncovered. By analogy, similar discoveries might be anticipated in Eastern Asia once its Tertiary formations are subjected to equally rigorous investigation. Furthermore, our understanding of the terrestrial organisms from the Mesozoic era remains minimal. Evidence suggests that the western regions of the United States may offer insights into this era comparable to those they have provided regarding Tertiary life.

Professor Marsh has reported that, within a span of two years, fossils representing more than 160 distinct mammalian individuals, distributed across twenty species and nine genera, were recovered from an area no larger than a medium-sized room. In addition, Mesozoic-age deposits from the same region have yielded the remains of 300 reptiles, ranging in size from approximately 60 to 80 feet in length to those comparable in dimensions to a rabbit. These findings underscore the potential for continued discovery and the vast scope of paleontological research yet to be undertaken.

Paleontology is still a relatively young branch of science, but the amount of material it deals with is already enormous. Over the past fifty years, the number of known fossils from invertebrate animals has grown three or four times larger. The study of vertebrate fossils, which was given such a solid start by Cuvier, was carried forward with great energy and success by Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in England. In more recent times, many others have joined in this work. In some groups of animals, the number of fossil species we know about is as large as—or even larger than—the number of species still living today. There are even entire orders of animals we wouldn’t know existed if not for the fossils they left behind. 

Despite all this progress, it’s safe to say we’ve only just begun to uncover the full range of fossils out there. Judging by the incredible finds from North America’s Tertiary formations in recent years, there seems to be no end to the number of mammal fossils that continent could yield. By the same reasoning, we can expect similar discoveries in Eastern Asia, once its Tertiary formations are thoroughly explored. 

There’s still so much to learn about the animals that lived on land during the Mesozoic era. The western United States might turn out to be as rich a source of fossils from that time as it has been for Tertiary fossils. In the same beds, they’ve uncovered 300 reptiles, ranging from enormous creatures 60 to 80 feet long to ones no bigger than a rabbit. The potential for new discoveries seems boundless.

Though the discipline of paleontology may trace its lineage to relatively recent scientific origins, the sheer amplitude of its evidentiary corpus has already grown to astonishing proportions. Over the past half-century, the catalog of fossilized vestiges of invertebrate life forms has expanded threefold or even fourfold. The systematic deciphering of vertebrate paleontological records—initially architected with unparalleled precision by Cuvier—found its continuance in the indefatigable labors of Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in Britain. Their endeavors were succeeded, in more recent decades, by an ever-expanding cadre of investigators contributing to this burgeoning tapestry of discovery. Indeed, within numerous taxonomic strata of the animal kingdom, the inventory of fossilized species rivals—and in certain instances surpasses—the enumeration of extant taxa. Entire orders, whose ontological reality would have remained veiled in perpetual obscurity, owe their evidentiary affirmation solely to the mute testimony of fossilized remnants.

Yet, despite the seemingly boundless accumulation of paleontological artifacts, it is a reasonable conjecture that the current state of knowledge encompasses but a meager fraction—a mere tithe—of the fossils that await future revelation. The recent profusion of mammalian relics unearthed from the Tertiary strata of North America offers a tantalizing glimpse into the potential fecundity of the geological record. This abundance invites the inference that analogous treasures lie hidden beneath the Tertiary deposits of Eastern Asia, awaiting the meticulous excavation and scrutiny that will inevitably bring them to light. Moreover, the terrestrial biota of the Mesozoic epoch remains a largely uncharted frontier. The western expanses of the United States, already an abundant source of Tertiary insights, appear poised to yield similarly transformative revelations concerning Mesozoic life.

My colleague, Professor Marsh, recounts a staggering density of paleontological yield: within a mere two years, a confined area no larger than the floor of a commodious room has disclosed the fossilized remains of over 160 individual mammals, representing twenty distinct species across nine genera. Concurrently, stratigraphic layers of comparable antiquity have yielded an astonishing array of reptilian specimens—numbering 300 individuals and ranging in scale from rabbit-sized diminutiveness to leviathans stretching 60 or even 80 feet in length. In such discoveries, the latent immensity of Earth's deep biological history gradually unfurls before us, promising revelations yet untold.

Though paleontology strides through the halls of science as a relatively youthful discipline, the sheer heap of relics it handles is already staggering, a treasure trove that seems to expand with each passing day. Over the last half-century, the catalog of fossilized invertebrates has ballooned—doubled, tripled, perhaps quadrupled—until it teems like an ancient tidepool frozen in time. The vertebrates, too, have not been left behind. Cuvier, that titan of foundational insight, laid the bedrock with precision; then came Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen on British soil, each wielding their genius like a chisel against stone. And now, in their wake, an army of scholars sifts the dust, piecing together tales from the bones of the long-dead.

In some corners of the animal kingdom, the fossil record rivals, even eclipses, the variety of living species. Entire orders of creatures, once lost to oblivion, now step into the light solely because their stony remnants were unearthed. And still, what we know today is but a sliver of what lies waiting beneath the surface. Imagine: if the last few decades of excavation in North America’s Tertiary formations have unveiled such a dizzying bounty of mammalian remains, who can fathom the riches buried under Asia’s unexplored Tertiary layers? The analogy almost demands it—a mirrored abundance, hidden in Eastern earth.

And then there’s the Mesozoic, that shadowed age of thunder and fangs. Our understanding of its terrestrial life is little more than a sketch, a whisper, yet the Western expanse of the United States beckons with promise. Its territories, already a crucible of Tertiary wonders, now hint at secrets locked even deeper in time. My friend, Professor Marsh, shares a tale so vivid it feels like myth: in the space of two short years, he and his team uncovered the remains of more than 160 individual mammals—twenty species spread across nine genera—all confined to a patch of ground no larger than a good-sized room. And that’s not all. From beds of the same age sprang forth 300 reptiles, some stretching sixty or eighty feet, others no larger than a rabbit. A veritable carnival of scales and bones, waiting patiently for eons to be found.

But, although palæontology is a comparatively youthful scientific speciality, the mass of materials with which it has to deal is already prodigious. In the last fifty years the number of known fossil remains of invertebrated animals has been trebled or quadrupled. The work of interpretation of vertebrate fossils, the foundations of which were so solidly laid by Cuvier, was carried on, with wonderful vigour and success, by Agassiz in Switzerland, by Von Meyer in Germany, and last, but not least, by Owen in this country, while, in later years, a multitude of workers have laboured in the same field. In many groups of the animal kingdom the number of fossil forms already known is as great as that of the existing species. In some cases it is much greater; and there are entire orders of animals of the existence of which we should know nothing except for the evidence afforded by fossil remains. With all this it may be safely assumed that, at the present moment, we are not acquainted with a tithe of the fossils which will sooner or later be discovered. If we may judge by the profusion yielded within the last few years by the Tertiary formations of North America, there seems to be no limit to the multitude of Mammalian remains to be expected from that continent; and analogy leads us to expect similar riches in Eastern Asia, whenever the Tertiary formations of that region are as carefully explored. Again, we have as yet almost everything to learn respecting the terrestrial population of the Mesozoic epoch—and it seems as if the Western territories of the United States were about to prove as instructive in regard to this point as they have in respect of tertiary life. My friend Professor Marsh informs me that, within two years, remains of more than 160 distinct individuals of mammals, belonging to twenty species and nine genera, have been found in a space not larger than the floor of a good-sized room; while beds of the same age have yielded 300 reptiles, varying in size from a length of 60 feet or 80 feet to the dimensions of a rabbit.

Paleontology, although a relatively recent scientific discipline, has already amassed a substantial collection of materials. Over the past fifty years, the number of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has increased two to four times. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, which was initially established by Georges Cuvier, has been vigorously advanced by notable scientists such as Louis Agassiz in Switzerland, Hermann von Meyer in Germany, and Richard Owen in the United Kingdom. In more recent years, numerous researchers have continued to contribute to this area of study. In various taxonomic groups, the number of known fossil forms is comparable to that of extant species. In certain instances, the number of fossil forms exceeds that of living species, and there are entire orders of animals whose existence is known only through fossil evidence.

It is reasonable to conclude that we are currently only aware of a small fraction of the fossils that will eventually be discovered. Evidence from recent findings in the Tertiary formations of North America suggests there is no limit to the number of mammalian remains that may be uncovered on that continent. Furthermore, analogous expectations arise regarding Eastern Asia, where similar fossil richness is anticipated once the Tertiary formations of that area are thoroughly examined.

Additionally, our understanding of the terrestrial fauna of the Mesozoic era remains limited. The western regions of the United States appear poised to provide significant insights into this aspect, similar to what has been revealed about Tertiary life. Concurrently, deposits of the same geological age have yielded 300 reptiles, with sizes ranging from 60 to 80 feet in length down to the dimensions of a rabbit.

Ah, the vibrant tapestry of palæontology, a sprightly young beast of a science, bursting at the seams with an astonishing trove of treasures from the past! In just half a century, the fossilized whispers of invertebrates have multiplied like wildflowers after a spring rain, tripling and quadrupling in number. The grand edifice of vertebrate fossil interpretation, so steadfastly erected by the genius of Cuvier, has been carried forward with a dazzling zeal by the likes of Agassiz in the crystalline Swiss alps, Von Meyer in the meticulous heart of Germany, and the incomparable Owen here on our own shores. And oh, how the ranks of eager discoverers have swelled in recent years, a veritable army of enthusiasts plowing through the rich soil of prehistoric life!

Dive into the depths of the animal kingdom’s annals, and you’ll find that the fossilized forms unearthed rival the living species in sheer abundance. In some cases, they outnumber their modern kin by a staggering margin! Entire orders of creatures flicker back to life through the ghostly remnants of their bones, whispering tales of their existence, tales we would be utterly blind to without their fossilized fingerprints. Yet, despite this wondrous bounty, we are but scratching the surface—one could safely wager that we’re acquainted with only a fraction, a mere whisper, of the fossils waiting to be revealed.

Take a glance at North America’s Tertiary formations, an overflowing cornucopia of Mammalian remains, a treasure trove that promises to keep yielding its secrets. And if we dare to dream, Eastern Asia beckons with its untapped wealth, waiting for curious minds to sift through its layers as carefully as a jeweler inspecting a precious gem. 

And then there’s the Mesozoic epoch, a tantalizing mystery still shrouded in the mists of time! The Western territories of the United States, my friend, are on the brink of unveiling a narrative as rich and intricate as that of Tertiary life. Professor Marsh shares the astonishing news—within a mere two years, more than 160 distinct mammals, spanning twenty species and nine genera, have emerged from a space no larger than your living room floor! And let’s not forget the 300 reptiles that have paraded forth from the same ancient beds, some stretching an awe-inspiring 60 to 80 feet, while others could comfortably fit in your lap like a rabbit. Oh, the wonders yet to come!

According to Professor Marsh, within a span of two years, more than 160 distinct mammalian individuals from twenty species and nine genera have been discovered in an area no larger than the floor of a typical room. Concurrently, deposits of the same geological age have yielded 300 reptiles, with sizes ranging from 60 to 80 feet in length down to the dimensions of a rabbit.

Paleontology, although a relatively recent scientific discipline, has already amassed a substantial collection of materials. Over the past fifty years, the number of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has increased two to four times. The interpretation of vertebrate fossils, which was initially established by Georges Cuvier, has been vigorously advanced by notable scientists such as Louis Agassiz in Switzerland, Hermann von Meyer in Germany, and Richard Owen in the United Kingdom. In more recent years, numerous researchers have continued to contribute to this area of study. In various taxonomic groups, the number of known fossil forms is comparable to that of extant species. In certain instances, the number of fossil forms exceeds that of living species, and there are entire orders of animals whose existence is known only through fossil evidence.

It is reasonable to conclude that we are currently only aware of a small fraction of the fossils that will eventually be discovered. Evidence from recent findings in the Tertiary formations of North America suggests there is no limit to the number of mammalian remains that may be uncovered on that continent. Furthermore, analogous expectations arise regarding Eastern Asia, where similar fossil richness is anticipated once the Tertiary formations of that area are thoroughly examined.

Paleontology is a relatively new field of science, but it has already gathered an impressive amount of information. In the last fifty years, the number of known fossil remains of invertebrate animals has tripled or even quadrupled. Pioneers like Cuvier laid a strong foundation for interpreting vertebrate fossils, and this work was carried on vigorously by scientists such as Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and Owen in England. Many others have also joined this effort in recent years. For many groups of animals, the number of known fossil forms is now as high as the number of living species. In some cases, it’s even higher. There are entire groups of animals that we would know nothing about if it weren’t for fossil evidence. 

At this point, we can be sure that we have only discovered a small fraction of the fossils that are still out there waiting to be found. For example, the Tertiary formations in North America have revealed a vast number of mammal remains recently, suggesting that many more will be uncovered in the future. We can expect similar discoveries in Eastern Asia, as long as those areas are explored thoroughly. 

Moreover, we still have a lot to learn about the land animals from the Mesozoic era. It looks like the western regions of the United States might be just as revealing about this time as they have been for Tertiary life. My friend Professor Marsh shared with me that in just two years, over 160 distinct individuals of mammals, belonging to twenty species and nine genera, have been found in an area no larger than a good-sized room. Additionally, the same age of rock layers has yielded 300 reptiles, ranging from sizes as large as 60 or 80 feet long down to the size of a rabbit.

Additionally, the same age of rock layers has yielded 300 reptiles, ranging from sizes as large as 60 or 80 feet long down to the size of a rabbit.

Despite its relative novelty as a scientific domain, paleontology grapples with an immense corpus of materials that is already staggering in its magnitude. Over the past half-century, the inventory of known fossil remnants pertaining to invertebrate organisms has experienced a remarkable expansion, with numbers multiplying by three or four times. The interpretative endeavors concerning vertebrate fossils, whose foundational principles were meticulously established by Cuvier, have been vigorously advanced by luminaries such as Agassiz in Switzerland, Von Meyer in Germany, and the illustrious Owen within the confines of this nation. In more contemporary epochs, a veritable multitude of researchers has engaged in this pursuit, contributing to a burgeoning repository of knowledge.

In numerous taxa within the animal kingdom, the aggregate of fossil forms already cataloged rivals, and in some instances exceeds, the diversity of extant species. Entire orders of organisms remain veiled in obscurity, their existence illuminated solely by the vestiges of fossilized remains. Given this context, it is reasonable to posit that our current acquaintance with the fossil record constitutes merely a fraction—perhaps a mere tithe—of what awaits discovery. The profusion of findings emanating from the Tertiary strata of North America in recent years suggests an inexhaustible reservoir of mammalian remains poised to emerge from that continent; analogously, we are led to anticipate similar bounties in Eastern Asia, contingent upon meticulous exploration of its Tertiary formations.

Moreover, our understanding of the terrestrial biota from the Mesozoic era remains in its infancy. The western territories of the United States appear poised to yield insights as profound concerning this epoch as they have regarding Tertiary life. My esteemed colleague, Professor Marsh, elucidates that within a mere biennium, remains from over 160 distinct mammalian individuals—comprising twenty species and nine genera—have been unearthed within an area no larger than a spacious room. Concurrently, contemporaneous deposits have revealed a staggering assemblage of 300 reptilian fossils, their sizes varying dramatically from lengths of 60 to 80 feet down to the diminutive scale of a rabbit.

Ah, the vibrant tapestry of palæontology, a sprightly young beast of a science, bursting at the seams with an astonishing trove of treasures from the past! In just half a century, the fossilized whispers of invertebrates have multiplied like wildflowers after a spring rain, tripling and quadrupling in number. The grand edifice of vertebrate fossil interpretation, so steadfastly erected by the genius of Cuvier, has been carried forward with a dazzling zeal by the likes of Agassiz in the crystalline Swiss alps, Von Meyer in the meticulous heart of Germany, and the incomparable Owen here on our own shores. And oh, how the ranks of eager discoverers have swelled in recent years, a veritable army of enthusiasts plowing through the rich soil of prehistoric life!

Dive into the depths of the animal kingdom’s annals, and you’ll find that the fossilized forms unearthed rival the living species in sheer abundance. In some cases, they outnumber their modern kin by a staggering margin! Entire orders of creatures flicker back to life through the ghostly remnants of their bones, whispering tales of their existence, tales we would be utterly blind to without their fossilized fingerprints. Yet, despite this wondrous bounty, we are but scratching the surface—one could safely wager that we’re acquainted with only a fraction, a mere whisper, of the fossils waiting to be revealed.

Take a glance at North America’s Tertiary formations, an overflowing cornucopia of Mammalian remains, a treasure trove that promises to keep yielding its secrets. And if we dare to dream, Eastern Asia beckons with its untapped wealth, waiting for curious minds to sift through its layers as carefully as a jeweler inspecting a precious gem. 

And then there’s the Mesozoic epoch, a tantalizing mystery still shrouded in the mists of time! The Western territories of the United States, my friend, are on the brink of unveiling a narrative as rich and intricate as that of Tertiary life. Professor Marsh shares the astonishing news—within a mere two years, more than 160 distinct mammals, spanning twenty species and nine genera, have emerged from a space no larger than your living room floor! And let’s not forget the 300 reptiles that have paraded forth from the same ancient beds, some stretching an awe-inspiring 60 to 80 feet, while others could comfortably fit in your lap like a rabbit. Oh, the wonders yet to come!

Cross-examined.—I am an officer in the Grenadier Guards. I have had some experience of stage-plays. I believe that nothing is easier than to write a good stage-play. I have written one myself. I found it extremely easy. Mounting guard was an intellectual exercise not to be mentioned in the same breath with it. My play has not been produced—not yet. I have shown it to several managers—they hesitate to produce it, on the ground that it is too intellectual. I had no objection to bring it down to the comprehension of the audience; but I did not see any way of making it less intellectual than it was—I have no objection to state its name—it is a burlesque called *Tom Tiddler.*

Interrogated.—I hold the rank of officer within the Grenadier Guards, and my acquaintance with the realm of stagecraft is not insubstantial. My conviction is firm: the composition of a commendable stage-play is a task of negligible difficulty. Indeed, I have authored one myself, encountering scarcely any resistance in the endeavor. In comparison, the mental discipline required for guard duty pales into triviality. Yet, my play remains unperformed, its theatrical debut deferred. Several managers have reviewed the work, yet their reluctance persists, citing its excessive intellectualism as a deterrent to production. While I am not averse to rendering it accessible to the audience's interpretive faculties, I discern no viable method of diminishing its intellectual density without compromising its essence. I freely disclose its title—a burlesque named *Tom Tiddler.*

I’m an officer in the Grenadier Guards and have some experience with stage plays. Honestly, I think writing a good play is one of the easiest things a person can do. I’ve written one myself, and it wasn’t hard at all. Compared to standing guard, which takes some mental effort, writing the play felt like a breeze. My play hasn’t been performed yet, though. I’ve shared it with a few theater managers, but they’re hesitant to put it on. They say it’s too intellectual for audiences. I was open to simplifying it so people could follow it better, but I couldn’t figure out how to make it less intellectual without losing what it was. I don’t mind telling you the title—it’s a burlesque called *Tom Tiddler.*

Interrogated.—Yes, I serve as an officer in the Grenadier Guards. And yes, I’ve dabbled in the world of stage-plays. Let me tell you, crafting a good one is child’s play—an idle amusement, a breeze through the trees. I’ve penned one myself, and the ease of it was almost laughable. Compared to standing sentry, a task that gnaws at the mind with its dull teeth, writing that play was a frolic, a lark. Has it graced the boards yet? Not quite. I’ve pitched it to managers, but they hem and haw, claiming it’s too intellectual for the masses. I wouldn’t mind simplifying it, dragging it down to the common grasp, but truth be told, I see no way to dull its edge. Curious about the name? It’s a burlesque—*Tom Tiddler.*

I’ve pitched it to managers, but they hem and haw, claiming it’s too intellectual for the masses. I wouldn’t mind simplifying it, dragging it down to the common grasp, but truth be told, I see no way to dull its edge. Curious about the name? It’s a burlesque—*Tom Tiddler.*

Cross-examined.—I am an officer in the Grenadier Guards. I have had some experience of stage-plays. I believe that nothing is easier than to write a good stage-play. I have written one myself. I found it extremely easy. Mounting guard was an intellectual exercise not to be mentioned in the same breath with it. My play has not been produced—not yet. I have shown it to several managers—they hesitate to produce it, on the ground that it is too intellectual. I had no objection to bring it down to the comprehension of the audience; but I did not see any way of making it less intellectual than it was—I have no objection to state its name—it is a burlesque called *Tom Tiddler.*

In the arena of scrutiny, I find myself positioned as an officer within the esteemed Grenadier Guards, a role not devoid of its own theatricality. My encounters with the realm of stage-plays have led me to the audacious conviction that the composition of a commendable theatrical work is a task marked by remarkable ease. Indeed, I have ventured into this creative territory and crafted a play, which I discovered to be a pursuit that pales in complexity compared to the cerebral discipline of guard duty. Regrettably, this play remains unproduced, despite my presentations to various managers who, while intrigued, falter at the prospect of its intellectual rigor. I remain unperturbed by the prospect of simplifying its essence for the audience; however, I grapple with the challenge of diminishing its inherent intellectuality. The title, if it must be disclosed, is a burlesque entitled *Tom Tiddler.*

I am an officer in the Grenadier Guards and I have some experience with stage plays. I think writing a good play is quite easy. In fact, I’ve written one myself and found it very simple. Guard duty is nothing compared to that. My play hasn’t been shown to audiences yet. I’ve shared it with a few theater managers, but they hesitate to put it on stage because they feel it’s too intellectual. I would be happy to simplify it for the audience, but I just don’t know how to make it less intellectual than it already is. I’m fine with sharing its title—it’s a burlesque called *Tom Tiddler.*

Under the bright glare of scrutiny, I stand—an officer in the Grenadier Guards, a sentinel of both duty and creativity. Ah, the world of stage-plays! A realm where the ink flows like a river and the words dance like fireflies. I assure you, crafting a good stage-play is a breeze, a delightful waltz in the moonlight. I’ve penned one myself, a labor of love that felt lighter than the weight of my musket. Yet, alas! My masterpiece remains a hidden gem, shrouded in the shadows of doubt. Managers, those gatekeepers of the limelight, hesitate, whispering that it’s too cerebral. I’m willing to dumb it down for the masses, but how to strip away its essence? Its name? A cheeky burlesque, *Tom Tiddler*, waiting for its moment to shine.

Whatever was once the custom, in these days, at least, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts and to do so promptly. The young man with the buoyancy and optimism of Mr. Skimpole, in *[Bleak House](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bleak_House),* who held that "the sordid word money should never be mentioned in the relations of friendship" is having little vogue at the present time. The enthusiastic life insurance agent, if you will admit his logic, can prove to you that if you will pay your premiums long enough they will cost you nothing, but every sensible man knows that the thing that is worth anything costs something, and the fraternity is no exception. Fraternity men in the past have too frequently taken their obligations lightly, and so in a financial way have brought fraternities into disrepute. Fraternity officers have begun strongly to emphasize the importance of financial matters in the fraternity and are insisting upon the introduction of accounting systems into all chapters which will make it possible for fraternity accounts to be kept accurately and which will impress upon the fraternity man from the outset his obligation to meet his bills when they are due. When a boy goes into a fraternity, neither he nor his father should be deceived as to the actual cost of membership, nor allowed to believe that payment may be evaded or deferred. It is ordinarily as unwise to go into a fraternity with the idea that the cost of living will thus be reduced as it is to enter matrimony with the thought that it will cost no more to support a family than an individual. With us at the University of Illinois the fraternity man is likely to be called upon to expend twenty per cent more than if he had remained outside of the organization, and if he develops popularity and contracts the joining habit, it may cost him considerably more than this.

In contemporary practice, fraternity members are expected to fulfill their financial obligations in a timely manner. Unlike the character Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, who optimistically asserted that "the sordid word money should never be mentioned in the relations of friendship," such attitudes are no longer widely accepted. While a life insurance agent may argue that consistent payment of premiums can eventually negate their cost, it is generally understood that anything of value requires financial expenditure, and fraternities are no exception. Historically, fraternity members have often failed to take their financial responsibilities seriously, which has led to reputational harm for these organizations. 

To address this issue, fraternity leadership has increasingly emphasized the importance of financial accountability. Officers are advocating for the implementation of structured accounting systems within chapters, ensuring that records are maintained accurately. This approach aims to instill a sense of responsibility in fraternity members, making clear their obligation to settle bills promptly. When a student joins a fraternity, both he and his family should be fully informed about the associated costs and should not harbor misconceptions about deferring or avoiding payment. 

Entering a fraternity with the expectation of reduced living expenses is generally impractical, much like assuming that marriage will not increase financial obligations compared to living as an individual. At the University of Illinois, it is common for fraternity members to incur expenses approximately twenty percent higher than those faced by non-members. Furthermore, individuals who achieve social popularity and develop a tendency to join multiple organizations may find their costs significantly higher than this estimate.

These days, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts on time. The carefree attitude of Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, who believed money should never come up between friends, doesn’t have much place now. Sure, a life insurance agent might tell you that paying premiums long enough makes them practically free, but everyone knows that anything worthwhile comes with a cost, and fraternities are no exception. In the past, fraternity members often didn’t take their financial responsibilities seriously, which hurt the reputation of fraternities as a whole. Because of this, fraternity leaders now put a strong focus on finances. They’re making sure chapters use proper accounting systems so bills are kept straight and members learn early on to pay what they owe, when they owe it.

When a young man joins a fraternity, neither he nor his family should be misled about the actual costs involved. It’s important they understand that membership comes with financial obligations that can’t be avoided or put off. Joining a fraternity isn’t likely to save money—it’s about as unrealistic as thinking marriage won’t cost more than living alone. At the University of Illinois, for instance, fraternity members typically spend about 20% more than they would if they stayed outside the organization. And if a member becomes popular or develops a habit of joining multiple groups, those costs could climb even higher. 

Fraternities offer much to their members, but it’s important to go into them with clear expectations about the financial commitment. A fraternity isn’t free—it’s an investment, and everyone involved should treat it that way.

In the contemporary landscape of collegiate life, whatever nostalgic indulgences one might entertain regarding bygone traditions, the ethical expectation placed upon members of fraternities is unequivocal: financial obligations must be met, and met without delay. The airy romanticism of Dickens’s Mr. Skimpole, who flitted through *Bleak House* espousing the quaint notion that the vulgar specter of money ought never intrude upon the sanctity of friendship, finds little purchase in our present epoch. Indeed, even the most persuasive evangelists of speculative optimism—such as the life insurance agent with his paradoxical promise that enduring premiums will ultimately cost nothing—cannot obscure the inexorable truth: anything of worth exacts a price, and fraternity membership constitutes no exception to this axiom.

Historically, the financial ethos within fraternities has been tarnished by a certain levity with which obligations were shouldered—an institutional Achilles’ heel that has, over time, eroded the reputational capital of such organizations. To counteract this legacy of fiscal negligence, fraternity leadership has taken deliberate steps to foreground the primacy of economic accountability. Central to these reforms is the introduction of robust accounting mechanisms within all chapters, systems meticulously designed not merely for administrative precision but also to instill in members a durable consciousness of their financial responsibilities. The era of laissez-faire fraternal finances is, it seems, in terminal decline.

When a young man contemplates the rite of passage that is fraternity induction, neither he nor his progenitor should labor under illusions regarding the pecuniary realities therein. Neither the costs themselves nor the obligation to discharge them punctually should be obfuscated. Any notion that fraternity life might serve as a vehicle for economizing is as misguided as the belief that matrimony entails no greater financial strain than bachelorhood. At the University of Illinois, for instance, membership in a fraternity typically inflates a student’s expenditures by approximately twenty percent relative to their unaffiliated peers. And for those whose social ambitions extend to serial affiliations or general popularity, the fiscal outlay may well exceed this baseline, underscoring the gravity of financial foresight in such decisions.

At the University of Illinois, for instance, fraternity members typically spend about 20% more than they would if they stayed outside the organization. And if a member becomes popular or develops a habit of joining multiple groups, those costs could climb even higher. 

Fraternities offer much to their members, but it’s important to go into them with clear expectations about the financial commitment. A fraternity isn’t free—it’s an investment, and everyone involved should treat it that way.

These days, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts on time. The carefree attitude of Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, who believed money should never come up between friends, doesn’t have much place now. Sure, a life insurance agent might tell you that paying premiums long enough makes them practically free, but everyone knows that anything worthwhile comes with a cost, and fraternities are no exception. In the past, fraternity members often didn’t take their financial responsibilities seriously, which hurt the reputation of fraternities as a whole. Because of this, fraternity leaders now put a strong focus on finances. They’re making sure chapters use proper accounting systems so bills are kept straight and members learn early on to pay what they owe, when they owe it.

When a young man joins a fraternity, neither he nor his family should be misled about the actual costs involved. It’s important they understand that membership comes with financial obligations that can’t be avoided or put off. Joining a fraternity isn’t likely to save money—it’s about as unrealistic as thinking marriage won’t cost more than living alone. At the University of Illinois, for instance, fraternity members typically spend about 20% more than they would if they stayed outside the organization. And if a member becomes popular or develops a habit of joining multiple groups, those costs could climb even higher. 

Fraternities offer much to their members, but it’s important to go into them with clear expectations about the financial commitment. A fraternity isn’t free—it’s an investment, and everyone involved should treat it that way.

In the contemporary landscape of collegiate life, whatever nostalgic indulgences one might entertain regarding bygone traditions, the ethical expectation placed upon members of fraternities is unequivocal: financial obligations must be met, and met without delay. The airy romanticism of Dickens’s Mr. Skimpole, who flitted through *Bleak House* espousing the quaint notion that the vulgar specter of money ought never intrude upon the sanctity of friendship, finds little purchase in our present epoch. Indeed, even the most persuasive evangelists of speculative optimism—such as the life insurance agent with his paradoxical promise that enduring premiums will ultimately cost nothing—cannot obscure the inexorable truth: anything of worth exacts a price, and fraternity membership constitutes no exception to this axiom.

Historically, the financial ethos within fraternities has been tarnished by a certain levity with which obligations were shouldered—an institutional Achilles’ heel that has, over time, eroded the reputational capital of such organizations. To counteract this legacy of fiscal negligence, fraternity leadership has taken deliberate steps to foreground the primacy of economic accountability. Central to these reforms is the introduction of robust accounting mechanisms within all chapters, systems meticulously designed not merely for administrative precision but also to instill in members a durable consciousness of their financial responsibilities. The era of laissez-faire fraternal finances is, it seems, in terminal decline.

When a young man contemplates the rite of passage that is fraternity induction, neither he nor his progenitor should labor under illusions regarding the pecuniary realities therein. Neither the costs themselves nor the obligation to discharge them punctually should be obfuscated. Any notion that fraternity life might serve as a vehicle for economizing is as misguided as the belief that matrimony entails no greater financial strain than bachelorhood. At the University of Illinois, for instance, membership in a fraternity typically inflates a student’s expenditures by approximately twenty percent relative to their unaffiliated peers. And for those whose social ambitions extend to serial affiliations or general popularity, the fiscal outlay may well exceed this baseline, underscoring the gravity of financial foresight in such decisions.

Once upon a time, perhaps, the rules of fraternity life might have bent and swayed like reeds in the wind, but these days, the expectation is as solid as stone: fraternity men must pay their debts, and pay them swiftly. Gone is the airy, whimsical spirit of Mr. Skimpole from *Bleak House,* that chirping optimist who deemed "money" too vulgar a term to sully the purity of friendship. Such notions, quaint as they are, find no purchase in the modern day. Now, even the slick-tongued life insurance agent can weave you a tale of premiums so persistent they vanish into thin air, but let’s be real—every man worth his salt knows the truth: anything of value demands its price. Fraternities, those gilded brotherhoods, are no exception.

Once, fraternity men might have skated lightly over their financial commitments, leaving behind a trail of unpaid dues and ill repute. But that era is fading like twilight over the quad. Today, fraternity officers are wielding their ledgers and calculators like knights with swords, determined to bring order to the chaos. Accounting systems, polished and precise, are being ushered into chapter halls, a steel spine for fraternity finances. The message rings loud and clear: pay your bills on time, or face the consequences. No more illusions for the young lad or his father, no whispered promises of low costs or slippery evasions. Joining a fraternity has a price tag, and it’s not written in disappearing ink.

To imagine fraternity life as a cost-cutter’s dream is as misguided as believing marriage is a budget-friendly upgrade. At the University of Illinois, the fraternity man will find his expenses swelling by a hefty twenty percent over what the lone wolf might spend outside the brotherhood. And if he’s blessed—or cursed—with charm and a penchant for joining every club in sight, that twenty percent may balloon into something far more formidable. The fraternity path, paved with camaraderie and tradition, is no stroll through the park; it’s a road where every step echoes with the sound of coins leaving your pocket.

Whatever was once the custom, in these days, at least, fraternity men are expected to pay their debts and to do so promptly. The young man with the buoyancy and optimism of Mr. Skimpole, in *[Bleak House](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bleak_House),* who held that "the sordid word money should never be mentioned in the relations of friendship" is having little vogue at the present time. The enthusiastic life insurance agent, if you will admit his logic, can prove to you that if you will pay your premiums long enough they will cost you nothing, but every sensible man knows that the thing that is worth anything costs something, and the fraternity is no exception. Fraternity men in the past have too frequently taken their obligations lightly, and so in a financial way have brought fraternities into disrepute. Fraternity officers have begun strongly to emphasize the importance of financial matters in the fraternity and are insisting upon the introduction of accounting systems into all chapters which will make it possible for fraternity accounts to be kept accurately and which will impress upon the fraternity man from the outset his obligation to meet his bills when they are due. When a boy goes into a fraternity, neither he nor his father should be deceived as to the actual cost of membership, nor allowed to believe that payment may be evaded or deferred. It is ordinarily as unwise to go into a fraternity with the idea that the cost of living will thus be reduced as it is to enter matrimony with the thought that it will cost no more to support a family than an individual. With us at the University of Illinois the fraternity man is likely to be called upon to expend twenty per cent more than if he had remained outside of the organization, and if he develops popularity and contracts the joining habit, it may cost him considerably more than this.

Currently, fraternity members are expected to fulfill their financial obligations promptly. The notion, exemplified by Mr. Skimpole in *Bleak House*, that financial matters should be excluded from friendships is not widely accepted today. The argument made by enthusiastic life insurance agents, which suggests that paying premiums will eventually yield a net cost of zero, is flawed. A rational individual understands that valuable resources necessitate a financial commitment, and fraternities are no exception to this principle. Historically, fraternity members have often treated their financial responsibilities casually, leading to a negative perception of fraternities as a whole. 

In response, fraternity leadership has increasingly emphasized the importance of financial accountability and is advocating for the implementation of accounting systems across all chapters. These systems aim to ensure accurate financial tracking and instill a sense of responsibility regarding timely payment of dues among fraternity members from the outset. 

When an individual joins a fraternity, it is imperative that neither he nor his family is misled about the true costs associated with membership, nor should they be led to believe that payments can be postponed or neglected. Entering a fraternity with the expectation that it will lower living expenses is as misguided as marrying with the assumption that supporting a family will not incur additional costs. At the University of Illinois, for instance, a fraternity member can expect to spend approximately twenty percent more than he would if he remained unaffiliated. Furthermore, if he gains popularity and becomes accustomed to the fraternity lifestyle, his expenses may increase significantly beyond this initial percentage.

In the past, many fraternity members have taken their financial responsibilities too lightly, which has hurt the reputation of fraternities. That’s why fraternity leaders are now putting a strong focus on financial matters. They want to set up accounting systems in every chapter, so that all fraternity accounts are accurate. This will help remind each member of their duty to pay their bills when they are due.

At the University of Illinois, a fraternity man might actually spend about twenty percent more than if he had chosen to stay outside the fraternity. If he becomes popular and keeps joining more groups, it could end up costing him even more than that.

In contemporary society, the conventions surrounding fraternity membership have evolved, mandating that individuals within these societies fulfill their financial obligations with punctuality. The idealistic youth, reminiscent of Mr. Skimpole from *Bleak House*, who espouses the notion that monetary discourse should remain extraneous to the fabric of friendship, finds himself increasingly out of favor. The exuberant life insurance salesman, appealing to an ostensibly sound reasoning, may assert that prolonged premium payments ultimately render themselves cost-free; however, the discerning individual recognizes the inherent truth that value necessitates sacrifice, a principle that extends unequivocally to the fraternity experience.

Historically, fraternity members have often approached their commitments with an alarming levity, thereby tarnishing the collective reputation of their organizations through financial irresponsibility. In response, fraternity leadership has instituted a vigorous campaign to underscore the significance of fiscal responsibility within these communities. They advocate for the implementation of rigorous accounting frameworks across all chapters, designed not only to ensure meticulous financial stewardship but also to instill in each fraternity member an acute awareness of their duty to honor financial commitments as they arise.

As a young man embarks upon his journey into fraternity life, it is imperative that neither he nor his progenitor falls prey to the fallacy that the true cost of membership is obscured or that financial obligations may be postponed indefinitely. It is as imprudent to enter a fraternity under the illusion that it will alleviate the financial burdens of living as it is to marry with the expectation that familial support will require no greater expenditure than that of solitary existence. Within the context of the University of Illinois, the fraternity initiate is poised to incur expenses that may exceed his previous financial commitments by as much as twenty percent, and should he cultivate a social network or develop a propensity for membership, the fiscal implications could ascend to even more significant heights.

As a young man embarks upon his journey into fraternity life, it is imperative that neither he nor his progenitor falls prey to the fallacy that the true cost of membership is obscured or that financial obligations may be postponed indefinitely. It is as imprudent to enter a fraternity under the illusion that it will alleviate the financial burdens of living as it is to marry with the expectation that familial support will require no greater expenditure than that of solitary existence. Within the context of the University of Illinois, the fraternity initiate is poised to incur expenses that may exceed his previous financial commitments by as much as twenty percent, and should he cultivate a social network or develop a propensity for membership, the fiscal implications could ascend to even more significant heights.

In contemporary society, the conventions surrounding fraternity membership have evolved, mandating that individuals within these societies fulfill their financial obligations with punctuality. The idealistic youth, reminiscent of Mr. Skimpole from *Bleak House*, who espouses the notion that monetary discourse should remain extraneous to the fabric of friendship, finds himself increasingly out of favor. The exuberant life insurance salesman, appealing to an ostensibly sound reasoning, may assert that prolonged premium payments ultimately render themselves cost-free; however, the discerning individual recognizes the inherent truth that value necessitates sacrifice, a principle that extends unequivocally to the fraternity experience.

Historically, fraternity members have often approached their commitments with an alarming levity, thereby tarnishing the collective reputation of their organizations through financial irresponsibility. In response, fraternity leadership has instituted a vigorous campaign to underscore the significance of fiscal responsibility within these communities. They advocate for the implementation of rigorous accounting frameworks across all chapters, designed not only to ensure meticulous financial stewardship but also to instill in each fraternity member an acute awareness of their duty to honor financial commitments as they arise.

Within the context of the University of Illinois, the fraternity initiate is poised to incur expenses that may exceed his previous financial commitments by as much as twenty percent, and should he cultivate a social network or develop a propensity for membership, the fiscal implications could ascend to even more significant heights.

In the whirlwind of modern camaraderie, the brotherhood of fraternity men has shed the cloak of delinquency; these days, they are expected to honor their debts with the punctuality of a clock that never skips a beat. Gone are the days of Mr. Skimpole from *Bleak House,* that buoyant dreamer who naively suggested that money—oh, that sordid little word—should never intrude upon the sacred bonds of friendship. Such whimsies have lost their charm, drowned beneath the weight of reality. 

Picture the relentless life insurance agent, armed with logic sharper than a surgeon’s scalpel, eager to convince you that if you just keep paying those premiums, they’ll eventually cost you nothing at all. But every wise man knows that value demands a price, and the fraternity is no exception to this rule. Once upon a time, fraternity men may have danced lightly around their obligations, like carefree leaves caught in the breeze, but that carefree approach has tarnished the fraternity’s name and left it in the murky waters of disrepute. 

Now, the officers of these brotherhoods are wielding the sword of fiscal responsibility, brandishing the importance of financial prudence like a banner in the wind. They are demanding the implementation of meticulous accounting systems within every chapter, systems that will keep the fraternity’s coffers in perfect order and etch into the hearts of its members an unwavering commitment to meet their dues when the clock strikes. 

When a young man steps into the grand arena of fraternity life, neither he nor his father should be lulled by illusions about the true cost of this membership; no one should believe that bills can be whisked away into the shadows. It’s as foolish to enter a fraternity with dreams of reduced living costs as it is to embark on the sacred journey of matrimony under the delusion that supporting a family will be a breeze. At the University of Illinois, for instance, a fraternity man might find himself shelling out twenty percent more than if he had chosen the solitary path, and should he become the life of the party, that cost could swell even further, like a tide rising to meet the moon.

Merited and timely as such criticism may appear to a transient observer of the State university, it is recognized as superficial and essentially false by all those who have felt the inner throb and glow of the enterprise. Idealist the institution is not, if idealism means a sterile yearning for the unattainable. Materialist it is not, if materialism is identified with satisfaction in the welfare of the senses. The State university to-day is at the same time intensely visionary and intensely practical: its driving power is the creative artist's desire to externalize and eternize his dream. With eyes fixed upon that end, it does not shrink from the coarse tasks of mixing pigments, quarrying stone, or melting bronze. It would honor every truth by use, and it holds that the triumph of the spiritual is the subjugation of the material. The financial support which it solicits is the means to the realization of a vision embracing almost the whole of life, and the wealth which it helps to create is but the first fruits of its contemplated harvest.

However justified such reproaches may seem to the casual onlooker skimming the surface of the State university’s vast endeavor, those who have attuned themselves to its deeper pulsations, who have felt the fervent vitality of its purpose, discern their superficiality and fundamental inaccuracy. The institution is no haven for the barren aspirations of an idealism that flutters ineffectually toward the unattainable. Nor does it sink into the inert satisfactions of materialism, if by that term we mean the complacent celebration of sensory contentment. Rather, the State university occupies a liminal space where the fervor of the visionary and the rigor of the pragmatic coalesce. Its animating force resembles the artist’s compulsion to incarnate and immortalize an imagined world; yet, in pursuit of this higher aim, it engages unflinchingly in the earthy labor of grinding pigments, hewing stone, and casting bronze.

The institution’s ethos seeks not to transcend or deny materiality but to subdue it, channeling its recalcitrant energies toward the service of enduring truths. In this, it affirms that the apotheosis of the spiritual lies in the disciplined mastery of the tangible. Financial resources, far from being an end in themselves, serve as instruments for the enactment of a vision that aspires to encompass the full breadth of human existence. The wealth it generates is merely the preliminary yield of a far more expansive and profound harvest.

Criticism, no matter how fitting or well-timed it may seem to the casual passerby sizing up the State university, crumbles as shallow and fundamentally untrue in the eyes of those who have felt the pulse, the heat, the living heartbeat of its purpose. This institution is no aloof dreamer, pining after the unreachable with limp hands and vacant sighs. Nor is it some brutish hedonist, gorging on earthly comforts and calling it a day. No, the State university stands as both a visionary and a workhorse, its soul fired by the artist’s hunger to shape the intangible into something lasting, something real. Fixated on this grand ambition, it does not flinch before the grit of its labor—mixing paints, chiseling stone, pouring bronze with hands steady and unashamed. It exalts all truths through their use, believing that the highest triumph of the spirit lies in bending the material world to its will. The money it seeks? Not an end, but a tool—a way to breathe life into a vision vast enough to cradle nearly all of human existence. And the wealth it generates? Merely the first glimmer of the bountiful harvest it dreams to sow.

Criticism of the State university might seem fair to someone just passing through, but those who truly know it understand that such views miss the mark. The university isn’t chasing impossible dreams, nor is it simply focused on material comfort or physical needs. Instead, it’s both deeply imaginative and firmly grounded. It works like an artist, driven to bring a vision to life and make it last. 

With that goal in mind, it doesn’t shy away from hard, gritty work—whether that’s mixing paint, carving stone, or casting metal. The university believes that every truth should have a purpose, and that true spiritual success comes from mastering and using the material world. When it asks for funding, it’s not just about money. That support fuels a vision that touches nearly every part of life. The wealth it helps create is only the beginning of the bigger impact it hopes to achieve.

The university does not embody idealism, should idealism be construed as a barren longing for the unattainable, nor does it represent materialism if that term signifies mere gratification of sensory desires. Rather, the State university exists in a duality that is simultaneously intensely visionary and pragmatically grounded; its propulsive force derives from the creative artist's fervent aspiration to manifest and immortalize their vision. With its sights steadfastly set upon this purpose, it does not recoil from the laborious tasks of blending pigments, extracting stone, or forging bronze. It seeks to honor every truth through application, maintaining that the conquest of the spiritual realm necessitates the subjugation of the material. The financial support it pursues serves as a conduit to actualize a vision that encompasses the breadth of existence, while the wealth it cultivates represents merely the initial yield of its envisaged bounty.

Ah, the fleeting eye of the casual observer may cast a shadow on the vibrant tapestry of the State university, dismissing its essence as mere surface drapery—yet, for those who have danced with its pulse, who have basked in its incandescent glow, such judgments are as shallow as puddles after a rainstorm. Idealism? Not here, if it means languishing in a sterile dream, forever out of reach. And materialism? Far from it, if it merely revels in the carnal comforts of the senses. This grand institution, a paradox of vision and pragmatism, throbs with the artist's fervent wish to breathe life into dreams, to carve them into the very fabric of reality. With steadfast gaze fixed on that shimmering horizon, it dives headfirst into the grit of creation—mixing pigments, hewing stone, melting bronze. Every truth deserves its place in the grand orchestra of existence, for the spiritual ascends only when it tames the material. The funding it seeks is the golden key to unlock a vision that encompasses the vast expanse of life itself, while the wealth it fosters is merely the first blush of a rich, bountiful harvest yet to come.

Some people may think the criticism of the State university is fair and timely, but those who have truly experienced its spirit see it differently. The university is not just dreaming of impossible ideals. It is not only focused on material pleasures either. Instead, the State university today is both visionary and practical. It has the creative drive of an artist who wants to bring dreams to life and make them last. With this goal in mind, it willingly takes on tough tasks like mixing paints, cutting stone, or melting metal. It believes that every truth deserves to be used, and it sees the victory of the spirit as conquering the material world. The funding it seeks is a way to achieve a vision that touches nearly every part of life. The wealth it helps create is just the beginning of what it hopes to accomplish.

The fame of our greatest comic dramatist is founded wholly or mainly on but three of his five plays . His first comedy was little more than a brilliant study after such models as were eclipsed by this earliest effort of their imitator ; and tragedy under his hands appears rouged and wrinkled , in the patches and powder of Lady Wishfort . But his three great comedies are more than enough to sustain a reputation as durable as our language . Were it not for these we should have no samples to show of comedy in its purest and highest form . Of Congreve's immediate predecessors , whose aim had been to raise on French foundations a new English fabric of simple and unmixed comedy , Wycherley was of too base metal and Etherege was of metal too light to be weighed against him ; and besides theirs no other or finer coin was current than the crude British ore of Shadwell's brutal and burly talent . The enduring renown of our foremost comic dramatist rests almost entirely upon but three of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy, though undeniably brilliant, amounted to little more than a luminous exercise in mimicry, fashioned after models that his own nascent genius would soon outshine. Under his pen, tragedy appears as an aging coquette, her faded features obscured by the artificial embellishments of rouge and powder, much like the painted absurdities of Lady Wishfort. Yet these deficiencies are amply redeemed by his trio of masterful comedies, which alone suffice to anchor his legacy in perpetuity, as steadfast as the English tongue itself. Without these exemplars, we would be bereft of comedy in its most unalloyed and exalted form.

Ben Jonson, that lone crusader striving to impose a purer order upon the dramaturgical chaos of his era—when tragedy and comedy were as hopelessly entangled on the stage as they are in the tangled skein of human affairs—fell short in imbuing his intricate and monumental creations with the requisite fluidity and the essential vivacity that define the comic spirit. Turning to Congreve’s immediate precursors, we find that their collective efforts to erect an authentically English edifice of unadulterated comedy upon French foundations were marked by intrinsic limitations: Wycherley’s craftsmanship was alloyed with baser substances, and Etherege’s, though more refined, proved insubstantial—a coinage too light to carry weight against Congreve’s more substantial minting. Beyond these, the comedic landscape offered little but the unpolished ore of Shadwell’s coarse and muscular talent.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, the creative anatomy of Molière contains more than sufficient material to have fashioned a Congreve, while the equivalent proportion from Congreve’s corpus would, in turn, have sufficed to sculpt a Sheridan. Meanwhile, the robust and expansive humor that animates the comedies of Vanbrugh secures for him a seat—if not at the head, then not far removed—within the pantheon of comedic masters. Thus, within this lineage of theatrical endeavor, the contours of Congreve's achievement emerge as singularly luminous, a pinnacle unmatched by predecessors or successors alike.

The enduring reputation of our most esteemed comic dramatist rests almost entirely on three of his five plays. His initial comedy amounted to little more than an impressive exercise in imitation, modeled on earlier works that his own surpassed even at this nascent stage. His attempts in tragedy, however, appear artificial and unconvincing, resembling the overly adorned and exaggerated features of Lady Wishfort. Nonetheless, his three principal comedies alone suffice to guarantee a legacy as enduring as the English language itself. Without these, we would lack exemplars of comedy at its most refined and elevated.

Ben Jonson, who uniquely sought to reform the dramatic conventions of his era—when comedy and tragedy were as interwoven on the stage as they were in life—fell short of achieving the necessary fluidity and elegance in the comedic elements of his otherwise grand and intricate compositions. Among Congreve’s immediate predecessors, those who aspired to construct a distinctively English comedy on French foundations, Wycherley’s work proved too coarse in quality, while Etherege’s was too insubstantial to rival his. Beyond these, the field offered little of value, with the dominant alternative being the unpolished and forceful output of Shadwell, whose talent, though vigorous, lacked refinement.

To adapt Walter Savage Landor’s metaphor, one could assert that a fragment of Molière’s genius would have sufficed to create a Congreve, while a fragment of Congreve’s talent would have sufficed to produce a Sheridan. The broad, energetic humor of Vanbrugh’s excellent comedies secures him a significant position among the masters of the form.

The lasting fame of our greatest comic playwright rests almost entirely on three of his five plays. His first attempt at comedy was little more than a dazzling imitation of earlier works, which he quickly outshone. When he turned to tragedy, it came across as overly made-up and artificial, like Lady Wishfort with her heavy makeup and powdered face. But those three brilliant comedies are more than enough to keep his name alive as long as the English language endures. Without them, we’d have no true example of comedy at its purest and finest.

Ben Jonson, who tried to bring this form of comedy to the stage by reforming the mix of tragedy and comedy common in his time, couldn’t quite manage to give his plays the light touch and natural flow that true comedy requires. Among Congreve’s forerunners, some aimed to build a fresh, straightforward English comedy inspired by French models. Yet Wycherley lacked refinement, and Etherege’s work was too insubstantial to compare with Congreve’s. Beyond these two, there wasn’t much else of note—just the rough and heavy-handed humor of Shadwell, whose talent, while forceful, lacked polish.

Using a comparison from Landor, we might say that a single part of Molière’s genius would have been enough to create a Congreve, and a part of Congreve’s brilliance could have made a Sheridan. As for Vanbrugh, the broad, hearty humor in his excellent plays earns him a place among the greats.

When he turned to tragedy, it came across as overly made-up and artificial, like Lady Wishfort with her heavy makeup and powdered face. But those three brilliant comedies are more than enough to keep his name alive as long as the English language endures. Without them, we’d have no true example of comedy at its purest and finest.

Ben Jonson, who tried to bring this form of comedy to the stage by reforming the mix of tragedy and comedy common in his time, couldn’t quite manage to give his plays the light touch and natural flow that true comedy requires. Among Congreve’s forerunners, some aimed to build a fresh, straightforward English comedy inspired by French models. Yet Wycherley lacked refinement, and Etherege’s work was too insubstantial to compare with Congreve’s. Beyond these two, there wasn’t much else of note—just the rough and heavy-handed humor of Shadwell, whose talent, while forceful, lacked polish.

Using a comparison from Landor, we might say that a single part of Molière’s genius would have been enough to create a Congreve, and a part of Congreve’s brilliance could have made a Sheridan. As for Vanbrugh, the broad, hearty humor in his excellent plays earns him a place among the greats.
