The enduring renown of our foremost comic dramatist rests almost entirely upon but three of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy, though undeniably brilliant, amounted to little more than a luminous exercise in mimicry, fashioned after models that his own nascent genius would soon outshine. Under his pen, tragedy appears as an aging coquette, her faded features obscured by the artificial embellishments of rouge and powder, much like the painted absurdities of Lady Wishfort. Yet these deficiencies are amply redeemed by his trio of masterful comedies, which alone suffice to anchor his legacy in perpetuity, as steadfast as the English tongue itself. Without these exemplars, we would be bereft of comedy in its most unalloyed and exalted form.

Ben Jonson, that lone crusader striving to impose a purer order upon the dramaturgical chaos of his era—when tragedy and comedy were as hopelessly entangled on the stage as they are in the tangled skein of human affairs—fell short in imbuing his intricate and monumental creations with the requisite fluidity and the essential vivacity that define the comic spirit. Turning to Congreve’s immediate precursors, we find that their collective efforts to erect an authentically English edifice of unadulterated comedy upon French foundations were marked by intrinsic limitations: Wycherley’s craftsmanship was alloyed with baser substances, and Etherege’s, though more refined, proved insubstantial—a coinage too light to carry weight against Congreve’s more substantial minting. Beyond these, the comedic landscape offered little but the unpolished ore of Shadwell’s coarse and muscular talent.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, the creative anatomy of Molière contains more than sufficient material to have fashioned a Congreve, while the equivalent proportion from Congreve’s corpus would, in turn, have sufficed to sculpt a Sheridan. Meanwhile, the robust and expansive humor that animates the comedies of Vanbrugh secures for him a seat—if not at the head, then not far removed—within the pantheon of comedic masters. Thus, within this lineage of theatrical endeavor, the contours of Congreve's achievement emerge as singularly luminous, a pinnacle unmatched by predecessors or successors alike.

The blazing renown of our mightiest comic playwright rests almost entirely—no, unshakably—on just three of his five creations. His debut comedy? A dazzling echo, a shimmering imitation of the very models it would soon outshine. As for tragedy, under his pen, it totters forth like Lady Wishfort herself—painted, powdered, and trembling beneath the weight of its own artifice. But those three comedies, oh, they tower like unyielding pillars, more than enough to bear the weight of a reputation as eternal as the language in which they were spun. Without them, we would stand bereft, clutching at shadows, with no pure and perfect comedy to hold aloft as our own.

Ben Jonson, titan though he was, strode into the fray with hopes of reforming the stage—a place where comedy and tragedy tangled together like lovers or wrestlers, mimicking the messy chaos of life itself. Yet, for all his grandeur and labor, he could not infuse his intricate, majestic works with the unforced grace, the nimble ease, that the finest comic worlds demand. And those who came just before Congreve, striving to raise an English comedy on French foundations? They stumbled. Wycherley’s substance was coarse, his metal too base. Etherege, by contrast, was all weightless glitter, a coin too light to carry value. Beyond them, the stage offered little but the crude, unrefined brawn of Shadwell, whose talent, though burly and bold, lacked the finer edge.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, we might say this: a single limb of Molière could have shaped a Congreve, and one limb of Congreve could have wrought a Sheridan. Such was the ladder of genius, each rung ascending to its own bright height. And yet, in the robust, hearty humor of Vanbrugh’s splendid comedies, we find a voice that earns its place at the master’s table—a voice that, while broad and brash, stands tall among the echoes of the great.

The illustrious reputation of our preeminent comic dramatist is primarily anchored in a mere triad of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy resembles a dazzling imitation, an homage to predecessors whose luminescence has been eclipsed by this nascent effort of their acolyte. In contrast, his engagements with tragedy reveal a visage artificially adorned, akin to the rouged and wrinkled countenance of Lady Wishfort, a grotesque caricature of emotional depth. Yet, the three monumental comedies—those paragon creations—suffice to fortify a legacy as enduring as the language that enshrines them. Absent these masterpieces, we would possess no exemplars of comedy in its most unadulterated and exalted essence.

Ben Jonson, a solitary figure who endeavored to engender reform within the theatrical milieu of his epoch—an era where the boundaries between comedy and tragedy were as inextricable as the dualities of human existence—ultimately faltered in infusing his elaborate and resplendent oeuvre with the requisite levity and essential grace characteristic of the comic spirit. Among Congreve's immediate forerunners, those who sought to erect a new edifice of English comedy upon the foundations laid by French traditions, Wycherley emerged as too base in his artistic currency, while Etherege, lacking in substantive weight, proved insufficient for comparison. Beyond these figures, the only alternative available was the crude and unrefined ore of Shadwell's brutish and vigorous talent.

Employing an analogy drawn from Walter Savage Landor, one might contend that the corporeal essence of Molière could have fashioned a Congreve, while a mere fragment of Congreve's artistry would suffice to engender a Sheridan. The robust and expansive humor encapsulated within the commendable comedies of John Vanbrugh secures him a rightful position within the pantheon of masters, elevating his contributions to the discourse on comedic excellence.

The reputation of our foremost comic dramatist is primarily based on three of his five plays. His initial comedy serves mainly as a refined imitation of earlier models that were surpassed by this first work of his. In his tragic works, he presents characters that appear artificial, as exemplified by the character of Lady Wishfort. However, his three major comedies are sufficient to maintain a lasting reputation that is as enduring as our language itself. In their absence, we would lack examples of comedy in its purest and most elevated form. Ben Jonson, who made an attempt to reform comedy amid a theatrical landscape where comedy and tragedy were intricately intertwined, did not successfully impart the necessary fluidity and essential grace characteristic of comic life and movement to the action and emotion in his complex and impressive productions. Among Congreve's immediate predecessors, those who sought to establish a new English form of straightforward and unblended comedy based on French influences, Wycherley lacked the requisite quality, and Etherege did not possess sufficient substance to be considered a worthy comparison. Additionally, the only other significant contributions to comedy during that period came from Shadwell, whose talents were coarse and heavy. To borrow a phrase from Walter Savage Landor, one might assert that a limb of Molière would have sufficed to create a Congreve, and a limb of Congreve would have been adequate to produce a Sheridan. The robust humor present in John Vanbrugh's commendable comedies earns him a place among the masters of the genre.

Ben Jonson, who tried to reform the mix of comedy and tragedy common in his time, did not quite capture the natural charm and grace that real comic life needs. His plays, while grand and elaborate, missed the lightness that brings laughter. Among those who came before Congreve, Wycherley and Etherege aimed to build a new style of simple comedy based on French ideas, but Wycherley was too crude, and Etherege was too light to stand up to him. Besides them, the only other options were the rough and heavy-handed works of Shadwell.

To borrow a metaphor from Landor, we could say that just a piece of Molière would have been enough to create a Congreve, and a piece of Congreve would have been enough to create a Sheridan. The strong and lively humor in Vanbrugh's wonderful comedies earns him a place among the greats.

The illustrious reputation of our preeminent comic dramatist is primarily anchored in a mere triad of his five theatrical compositions. His inaugural foray into comedy resembles a dazzling imitation, an homage to predecessors whose luminescence has been eclipsed by this nascent effort of their acolyte. In contrast, his engagements with tragedy reveal a visage artificially adorned, akin to the rouged and wrinkled countenance of Lady Wishfort, a grotesque caricature of emotional depth. Yet, the three monumental comedies—those paragon creations—suffice to fortify a legacy as enduring as the language that enshrines them. Absent these masterpieces, we would possess no exemplars of comedy in its most unadulterated and exalted essence.

Ben Jonson, a solitary figure who endeavored to engender reform within the theatrical milieu of his epoch—an era where the boundaries between comedy and tragedy were as inextricable as the dualities of human existence—ultimately faltered in infusing his elaborate and resplendent oeuvre with the requisite levity and essential grace characteristic of the comic spirit. Among Congreve's immediate forerunners, those who sought to erect a new edifice of English comedy upon the foundations laid by French traditions, Wycherley emerged as too base in his artistic currency, while Etherege, lacking in substantive weight, proved insufficient for comparison. Beyond these figures, the only alternative available was the crude and unrefined ore of Shadwell's brutish and vigorous talent.

Employing an analogy drawn from Walter Savage Landor, one might contend that the corporeal essence of Molière could have fashioned a Congreve, while a mere fragment of Congreve's artistry would suffice to engender a Sheridan. The robust and expansive humor encapsulated within the commendable comedies of John Vanbrugh secures him a rightful position within the pantheon of masters, elevating his contributions to the discourse on comedic excellence.

The towering fame of our greatest comic dramatist stands on the slender shoulders of just three out of five plays, like a magnificent edifice built on a shaky foundation. His inaugural comedy, a dazzling imitation of old masters, shines like a brilliant firework yet pales in comparison to the dazzling sky of his later works. Tragedy, in his grasp, emerges all rouged and wrinkled, a gaudy figure adorned with the make-up of Lady Wishfort, desperately trying to mask its age. But ah! Those three grand comedies! They are the shimmering jewels that could illuminate a reputation as eternal as the very language we speak. Without them, we would be left in the dark, void of pure comedy's essence, starved of its highest expression.

Consider Ben Jonson, the lone knight who dared to wield his quill in an attempt to reform the chaotic blend of comedy and tragedy that ruled the stage, as tangled as life itself. Yet even he could not capture that effortless grace, that intoxicating rhythm of laughter and life that dances through the heart of true comedy. His grand ambitions, though magnificent, fell short of the mark.

Now, cast your gaze upon Congreve’s immediate predecessors, those who sought to construct a new edifice of comedy upon the shaky French foundations. Wycherley, alas, was forged from base metal, while Etherege was too light, a whisper in the wind, failing to weigh against Congreve's brilliance. And what of Shadwell, that crude smith of a burly talent? He fashioned nothing but the coarsest ore, unrefined and lacking in finesse.

Let us borrow a sparkling metaphor from Landor: a limb of Molière would have sufficed to create a Congreve, while a mere limb of Congreve could be twisted into the form of a Sheridan. In this vibrant lineage, we can’t overlook the broad and robust humor of Vanbrugh, whose admirable comedies carve out a rightful place in the annals of comedy’s greats, standing tall beside the masters, a testament to the enduring vitality of laughter.

We call it a singular fact, because we Yankees are thought to be fond of the spread-eagle style, and nothing can be more remote from that than his. We are reckoned a practical folks, who would rather hear about a new air-tight stove than about [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet our favorite teacher's practicality is not in the least of the Poor Richard variety. If he have any Buncombe constituency, it is that unrealized commonwealth of philosophers which Plotinus proposed to establish; and if he were to make an almanac, his directions to farmers would be something like this: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to get in your early Vedas." What, then, is his secret? Is it not that he out-Yankees us all? that his range includes us all? that he is equally at home with the potato-disease and original sin, with pegging shoes and the Over-soul? that, as we try all trades, so has he tried all cultures? and above all, that his mysticism gives us a counterpoise to our super-practicality?

We designate this a remarkable paradox, for it stands in stark contrast to the stereotype of the Yankee temperament, often caricatured as enamored of grandiloquent rhetoric and ostentatious display. His manner, however, is the antithesis of such posturing. We New Englanders pride ourselves on our utilitarian ethos, preferring discourse on innovations like hermetically sealed stoves over lofty meditations on [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet the practicality of our emblematic thinker bears no resemblance to the pragmatic proverbs of Poor Richard. If he commands any constituency of ideological adherents, it is likely the unmanifested republic of sages envisioned by Plotinus, a realm of pure contemplation unmoored from material concerns. Should he turn his hand to authoring an almanac, its agricultural advisories might read: “October: *Indian Summer;* the season for harvesting your primordial wisdom from the Vedas.”

What, then, accounts for his resonance? Is it not that he encapsulates the Yankee essence more comprehensively than any among us? That his intellectual orbit embraces the mundane and the metaphysical alike—potato blight and original sin, cobbler’s workbench and the Over-soul? That his polymathic engagement mirrors our own versatility, yet his mysticism counterbalances the gravitational pull of our unyielding pragmatism?

A strange irony, isn’t it? We Yankees, with our reputation for chest-thumping bombast, have crowned as our favorite teacher a man whose style is as far from grandiose as the moon is from the muddy fields. We’re a people who’d sooner crowd around to hear about the latest marvel of air-tight stove engineering than entertain the lofty musings of Plato—yet here we are, clasping hands with a thinker whose pragmatism is worlds removed from the homespun wisdom of Poor Richard. If he has any constituency at all, it’s not the thrifty farmers or penny-pinching merchants, but some spectral republic of philosophers, the kind Plotinus dreamed of founding. Imagine his almanac: “October: *Indian Summer;* time to harvest your early Vedas.” What’s the trick, then? How does he hold us? It’s simple—he out-Yankees us, surpasses us, engulfs us. His reach stretches from potato blight to original sin, from cobbling shoes to the shimmering vastness of the Over-soul. He has tried all trades, tasted every culture, and his mysticism stands like a balancing pole in the hands of a tightrope walker, keeping us steady as we teeter over the abyss of hyper-practicality.

If he has any constituency at all, it’s not the thrifty farmers or penny-pinching merchants, but some spectral republic of philosophers, the kind Plotinus dreamed of founding. Imagine his almanac: “October: *Indian Summer;* time to harvest your early Vedas.” What’s the trick, then? How does he hold us? It’s simple—he out-Yankees us, surpasses us, engulfs us. His reach stretches from potato blight to original sin, from cobbling shoes to the shimmering vastness of the Over-soul. He has tried all trades, tasted every culture, and his mysticism stands like a balancing pole in the hands of a tightrope walker, keeping us steady as we teeter over the abyss of hyper-practicality.

We call it a remarkable thing, because we Yankees are often seen as loving bold and flashy talk, and his way couldn’t be further from that. Folks think of us as practical, more likely to care about a new kind of air-tight stove than about what Plato had to say. But our favorite teacher’s kind of practicality is nothing like the down-to-earth advice you’d find in *Poor Richard’s Almanack.* If he has any group of admirers, it’s more like that ideal community of philosophers that Plotinus dreamed of. And if he ever wrote an almanac, his advice to farmers might sound like this: “October: *Indian Summer;* now’s the time to harvest your early Vedas.”

So, what’s his secret? Isn’t it that he outdoes us all in the best Yankee way? That he can connect with everything we care about? He’s just as comfortable talking about potato blight as he is about original sin, as familiar with making shoes as with the Over-soul. Just like we try our hand at all sorts of trades, he’s explored all kinds of knowledge. And maybe most important of all, his mysticism gives us something to balance out how overly practical we tend to be.

This observation is notable because individuals from New England are often characterized as favoring a rhetorical style marked by grandiosity, which is entirely absent in his approach. We are commonly described as pragmatic people who would prefer discussions about innovations such as an air-tight stove over philosophical topics like those explored by Plato. However, the practicality exhibited by our preferred teacher does not align with the conventional, utilitarian wisdom typified by "Poor Richard's Almanack." If he has any audience inclined toward rhetorical excess, it would be the hypothetical community of philosophers envisioned by Plotinus rather than a pragmatic constituency. Were he to create an almanac, the guidance he might offer to farmers would likely be unconventional, such as: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to harvest your early Vedas."

What accounts for his appeal? Is it not that he surpasses even our own tendencies, encompassing all aspects of our identity? He is equally adept at addressing subjects as varied as agricultural blight and theological doctrine, mundane trades like shoemaking and profound concepts such as the Over-soul. His breadth of engagement mirrors our cultural diversity, and his mysticism provides a necessary balance to our pronounced focus on practicality.

Imagine his almanac: “October: *Indian Summer;* time to harvest your early Vedas.” What’s the trick, then? How does he hold us? It’s simple—he out-Yankees us, surpasses us, engulfs us. His reach stretches from potato blight to original sin, from cobbling shoes to the shimmering vastness of the Over-soul. He has tried all trades, tasted every culture, and his mysticism stands like a balancing pole in the hands of a tightrope walker, keeping us steady as we teeter over the abyss of hyper-practicality.

A strange irony, isn’t it? We Yankees, with our reputation for chest-thumping bombast, have crowned as our favorite teacher a man whose style is as far from grandiose as the moon is from the muddy fields. We’re a people who’d sooner crowd around to hear about the latest marvel of air-tight stove engineering than entertain the lofty musings of Plato—yet here we are, clasping hands with a thinker whose pragmatism is worlds removed from the homespun wisdom of Poor Richard. If he has any constituency at all, it’s not the thrifty farmers or penny-pinching merchants, but some spectral republic of philosophers, the kind Plotinus dreamed of founding. Imagine his almanac: “October: *Indian Summer;* time to harvest your early Vedas.” What’s the trick, then? How does he hold us? It’s simple—he out-Yankees us, surpasses us, engulfs us. His reach stretches from potato blight to original sin, from cobbling shoes to the shimmering vastness of the Over-soul. He has tried all trades, tasted every culture, and his mysticism stands like a balancing pole in the hands of a tightrope walker, keeping us steady as we teeter over the abyss of hyper-practicality.

We call it a singular fact, because we Yankees are thought to be fond of the spread-eagle style, and nothing can be more remote from that than his. We are reckoned a practical folks, who would rather hear about a new air-tight stove than about [Plato](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Author:Plato); yet our favorite teacher's practicality is not in the least of the Poor Richard variety. If he have any Buncombe constituency, it is that unrealized commonwealth of philosophers which Plotinus proposed to establish; and if he were to make an almanac, his directions to farmers would be something like this: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to get in your early Vedas." What, then, is his secret? Is it not that he out-Yankees us all? that his range includes us all? that he is equally at home with the potato-disease and original sin, with pegging shoes and the Over-soul? that, as we try all trades, so has he tried all cultures? and above all, that his mysticism gives us a counterpoise to our super-practicality?

We designate it a singular fact, for we Yankees are often perceived as devotees of the ostentatious rhetoric, and nothing could be more antithetical to that than his approach. We are considered pragmatists, inclined to prefer discourse on the latest innovations, such as a new air-tight stove, over the musings of Plato; yet, the practicality of our esteemed educator diverges profoundly from the utilitarianism of Poor Richard. Should he possess any constituency of Buncombe, it would be that aspirational commonwealth of philosophers envisioned by Plotinus. Were he to compose an almanac, his agricultural counsel might resemble: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the moment to harvest your early Vedas." What, then, constitutes his esoteric secret? Is it not the case that he transcends our quintessential Yankee identity? That his intellectual purview encompasses the entirety of our existence? He navigates the realms of potato blight and original sin, of cobbling shoes and the concept of the Over-soul, as we dabble in various trades while he immerses himself in diverse cultures? Above all, his mysticism serves as a counterbalance to our hyper-practical dispositions.

We label it a singular truth, for we Yankees are often painted with the broad brush of bombast, and nothing drifts further from that than his essence. We’re seen as the practical sort, eager to chew on the mechanics of an air-tight stove rather than ponder the musings of Plato; yet, our cherished teacher's practicality dances far from the pedestrian Poor Richard mold. If he were to claim a constituency, it would be that shimmering dream of philosophers Plotinus envisioned—a kaleidoscope of thought. Imagine his almanac: "October: *Indian Summer;* seize this moment to gather your early Vedas." What, then, is the alchemy of his genius? Is it not that he out-Yankees us all, weaving through our fabric like a master tailor? His canvas stretches wide—embracing the blight of potatoes and the weight of original sin, the leather of pegged shoes and the whisper of the Over-soul. As we dabble in every trade, he has dipped into every culture, and above all, his mysticism cradles our super-practicality, offering a balance to our relentless grind.

We call it a singular fact because we Yankees often like to show off. Yet, nothing could be further from that than his style. We are seen as practical folks who prefer hearing about a new air-tight stove rather than deep thoughts from philosophers like Plato. But our favorite teacher’s practicality is nothing like Poor Richard’s. If he has any followers, it’s the dreamers and thinkers like Plotinus imagined. If he were to make a calendar, his advice for farmers might say, "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to read your early Vedas." So, what’s his secret? Isn’t it that he out-Yankees us all? His thinking includes us all. He’s comfortable talking about everything from potato blight to original sin, from making shoes to the idea of the Over-soul. Just as we try our hand at many jobs, he explores all kinds of cultures. Most importantly, his mystical side offers a balance to our overly practical ways.

We refer to this as a singular fact because we, as Americans, are perceived as favoring an ostentatious style, which is in stark contrast to his approach. We are considered practical individuals who would prefer discussions about a new airtight stove rather than philosophical topics. However, our preferred educator's practicality does not resemble that of Poor Richard. If he were to have any constituency, it would be the abstract community of philosophers that Plotinus proposed to create. If he were to compose an almanac, his advice to farmers might resemble: "October: *Indian Summer;* now is the time to gather your early Vedas." What, then, is the essence of his appeal? Is it not that he embodies a more profound American ethos? His range encompasses all aspects of our existence, as he is equally conversant with both agricultural blights and the concept of original sin, as well as practical tasks like shoemaking and the notion of the Over-soul? Just as we engage in various professions, he has explored diverse cultures. Most importantly, his mysticism serves as a counterbalance to our excessive pragmatism.

The ancient proverb which declares that love is blind is only a half truth: love is only blind of one eye. The expression "blind hate" also contains only a half truth: hate has one powerful eye. When either of these passions is present, it insatiably sets about discovering new objects to feast upon. The lover collects and broods upon the graces of his beloved from the rising of the lark to the going down of the evening star. The husband who thoroughly loses his temper with his wife at the breakfast table busies himself all day long recollecting and amassing all the grievances that he has felt against her during the last ten years; and he returns at night in a sullen rage and with an almost superhuman insight into the deficiencies of his helpmate. So men of letters set out with gusto and find a relish in every wayside berry; or they set out on the satirical foot with a bad taste in their mouths, and for a whole generation occupy themselves with malicious caricatures of their mistress, Life.

The venerable aphorism that proclaims love’s blindness is, upon inspection, a partial truth at best: love is blind in but one eye, leaving the other to rove and revel. Likewise, the phrase "blind hate" is similarly deceptive, for hatred possesses a singular but piercing vision. These twin forces—love and hate—are insatiable seekers, endlessly questing for nourishment to sustain their fervor. The lover, entranced by the object of his affection, spends his hours tracing the contours of her virtues, his thoughts flitting from dawn’s lark to the twilight’s fading star, assembling a treasury of her charms. Conversely, the enraged husband, having quarreled with his wife over morning coffee, dedicates his day to summoning forth a litany of past grievances, constructing from the shards of ten years’ worth of dissatisfaction a monument to his wrath. By nightfall, he returns home, armed not merely with anger but with an almost preternatural acuity for cataloging her every flaw. And so, too, the writer embarks—whether enraptured or embittered—on the pathways of observation: one moment savoring the delicate sweetness of life’s incidental graces, the next consumed by a bitter appetite, producing caustic caricatures of existence, as though Life herself were a mistress deserving of scorn rather than devotion.

The proverb asserting that love is blind is only partially accurate; love is blind in only one eye. Similarly, the phrase "blind hate" is equally incomplete, as hate possesses one exceptionally keen eye. When either passion arises, it engages with relentless intensity in seeking new objects to consume. The lover devotes himself to contemplating the virtues of the beloved, from dawn to dusk. Conversely, a husband who loses his temper with his wife during breakfast may spend the entire day reflecting on and compiling grievances accumulated over the past decade. By evening, he returns home filled with resentment and an almost extraordinary ability to perceive his wife’s flaws. Similarly, literary individuals approach life with enthusiasm, finding enjoyment in every minor detail along their path. Alternatively, they may adopt a satirical stance, harboring distaste, and dedicate decades to producing spiteful distortions of their subject, which in this case is Life itself.

The old saying that love is blind isn’t completely true—love is only blind in one eye. Similarly, the phrase “blind hate” isn’t entirely accurate either—hate sees sharply with one strong eye. When someone is caught up in either feeling, they can’t help but hunt for more things to focus on. A lover spends the whole day noticing and cherishing every little charm of the person they adore, from the first light of morning to the setting of the sun. On the other hand, a husband who gets angry with his wife at breakfast might spend the entire day dwelling on every grievance he’s had against her over the past decade. By the time he returns home, his mood is bitter, and his mind seems unnaturally sharp at spotting every flaw in her. Writers, too, can go out into the world full of excitement, savoring every small detail they come across. Or they might start off with a sour spirit and spend years crafting biting, exaggerated portraits of life, their inspiration fueled by bitterness rather than joy.

When someone is caught up in either feeling, they can’t help but hunt for more things to focus on. A lover spends the whole day noticing and cherishing every little charm of the person they adore, from the first light of morning to the setting of the sun. On the other hand, a husband who gets angry with his wife at breakfast might spend the entire day dwelling on every grievance he’s had against her over the past decade. By the time he returns home, his mood is bitter, and his mind seems unnaturally sharp at spotting every flaw in her. Writers, too, can go out into the world full of excitement, savoring every small detail they come across. Or they might start off with a sour spirit and spend years crafting biting, exaggerated portraits of life, their inspiration fueled by bitterness rather than joy.

The venerable aphorism that proclaims love’s blindness is, upon inspection, a partial truth at best: love is blind in but one eye, leaving the other to rove and revel. Likewise, the phrase "blind hate" is similarly deceptive, for hatred possesses a singular but piercing vision. These twin forces—love and hate—are insatiable seekers, endlessly questing for nourishment to sustain their fervor. The lover, entranced by the object of his affection, spends his hours tracing the contours of her virtues, his thoughts flitting from dawn’s lark to the twilight’s fading star, assembling a treasury of her charms. Conversely, the enraged husband, having quarreled with his wife over morning coffee, dedicates his day to summoning forth a litany of past grievances, constructing from the shards of ten years’ worth of dissatisfaction a monument to his wrath. By nightfall, he returns home, armed not merely with anger but with an almost preternatural acuity for cataloging her every flaw. And so, too, the writer embarks—whether enraptured or embittered—on the pathways of observation: one moment savoring the delicate sweetness of life’s incidental graces, the next consumed by a bitter appetite, producing caustic caricatures of existence, as though Life herself were a mistress deserving of scorn rather than devotion.

Love, they say, is blind, but it stumbles only half-shrouded in the dark, one eye wide open, fixed on its quarry. And hate, oh, hate—the single blazing eye of it sees too much, too sharply, too cruelly. These twin tyrants, love and hate, are ravenous hunters, prowling always for fresh prey to feed their hunger. The lover? He’s a collector, his thoughts a magpie’s hoard of his beloved’s charms, polished and adored from the lark’s first song to the soft sigh of the evening star. The husband scalded by anger at breakfast? He spends his day dredging up the ghosts of quarrels past, grievances long buried but now risen and marching. By nightfall, he storms home, a thundercloud, clutching a decade’s worth of her flaws, each one gleaming with the cruel clarity of rage.

And those who wield the pen—the men of letters—are no different. When enamored of Life, they pluck every roadside berry, their words sweet and sun-warmed, a hymn to existence. But let bitterness take root, and they become bitter cartographers, sketching Life in caricature, their ink venomous, their pages steeped in bile. A generation may pass, and still they labor, their mistress maligned, their malice unyielding, their passion turned inside out.

The ancient proverb which declares that love is blind is only a half truth: love is only blind of one eye. The expression "blind hate" also contains only a half truth: hate has one powerful eye. When either of these passions is present, it insatiably sets about discovering new objects to feast upon. The lover collects and broods upon the graces of his beloved from the rising of the lark to the going down of the evening star. The husband who thoroughly loses his temper with his wife at the breakfast table busies himself all day long recollecting and amassing all the grievances that he has felt against her during the last ten years; and he returns at night in a sullen rage and with an almost superhuman insight into the deficiencies of his helpmate. So men of letters set out with gusto and find a relish in every wayside berry; or they set out on the satirical foot with a bad taste in their mouths, and for a whole generation occupy themselves with malicious caricatures of their mistress, Life.

The ancient adage proclaiming that love is blind reveals itself as but a partial truth; indeed, love is merely sightless in one eye. Similarly, the phrase "blind hate" encapsulates an incomplete reality: hate possesses a singular, potent vision. When these fervent passions arise, they embark on an insatiable quest to uncover new objects upon which to lavish their fervor. The lover, enraptured, meticulously collects and contemplates the virtues of the beloved from the dawn's first light to the dusk's final glow. Conversely, the husband, consumed by fury at the breakfast table, devotes the entire day to the recollection and accumulation of grievances amassed over a decade; he returns home, cloaked in a brooding rage, wielding an almost superhuman clarity concerning the shortcomings of his partner. Likewise, the literary mind ventures forth with enthusiasm, savoring each roadside berry, or, embittered, embarks on a satirical journey, gnawing on the bitterness of existence, thus devoting an entire generation to crafting malicious caricatures of their mistress, Life itself.

The ancient proverb stating that love is blind reflects only a partial truth: love is blind in one regard. Similarly, the term "blind hate" conveys only a partial truth; hate possesses one potent aspect. When either of these emotions is present, it actively seeks new subjects to engage with. The lover accumulates and reflects upon the qualities of the beloved from dawn until dusk. A husband who loses his temper with his wife at breakfast spends the remainder of the day recalling and cataloging grievances he has harbored against her over the past decade. He returns home in a state of anger, possessing an almost exaggerated awareness of his wife's shortcomings. Likewise, writers embark on their endeavors with enthusiasm, finding enjoyment in every detail they encounter, or they approach their tasks with a negative perspective, spending an extended period producing critical portrayals of their experience, which they refer to as Life.

The old saying that love is blind tells only part of the story. Love can’t see out of one eye. The phrase "blind hate" is also only partly true because hate has a strong vision. When either of these feelings takes hold, they eagerly look for new things to grab onto. A lover thinks about all the lovely traits of their partner from sunrise to sunset. On the other hand, a husband who loses his temper with his wife at breakfast spends his whole day remembering every little annoyance he has felt over the past decade. He comes home at night filled with anger and a sharp focus on all his wife's shortcomings. Writers, too, find joy in exploring the world around them. They enjoy every little thing they discover, or they might approach it with a bitter taste, spending years creating harsh images of their relationship with life itself.

Ah, the ancient wisdom that love is blind—yet it’s only half a truth, a winking jest! Love stumbles through the dark with one eye closed, peering into the shadows of adoration. And hate? Oh, it strides forth with a single, piercing eye that sees all! When these fierce passions take the stage, they become insatiable hunters, sniffing out fresh prey to consume. The lover, enraptured, gathers the sweet petals of his beloved’s charms from dawn’s first blush to the twilight’s gentle sigh. But beware the husband, fuming at the breakfast table, who, with gritted teeth, spends his day weaving a tapestry of grievances, each thread glistening with resentment from a decade of discontent. He returns home, a storm cloud of fury, wielding a keen eye for every flaw that flickers in the shadow of his partner. Meanwhile, the wordsmiths embark on their quests, savoring each roadside berry with gusto, or they march forth with a bitter taste on their tongues, crafting wicked caricatures of their mistress, Life, for an entire generation to behold.

There was a still more pregnant reason for my desertion of the camp of musical criticism. I seemed always to be about ten years ahead of most of the other critics and the orchestral conductors who make out programs. I missed the reviling of Wagner in New York, but I have watched the pundits of the press revile, in turn, Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, only, in the end, after their ears, through repeated hearings, had grown accustomed to the new clang-tints, to accept these composers as part of the sacred hierarchy. My orchestral education was carried on under Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Now Thomas was not a great conductor, although he always gave honest musical readings of his scores, but he had one great virtue: he believed that new music should be heard. He performed, therefore, every important European composition as soon as possible after it had been performed abroad. As a result, when I arrived in New York in 1906, having listened to and appraised nearly all the major works of Strauss and Debussy composed up to that date, I was amazed to discover that the New York critics were still fighting about these composers, first underpraising them and a little later overpraising them, for Strauss, at any rate, to my mind, has never been a whit superior as a composer to such a man as Liszt. In Paris, in 1913, I heard Stravinsky's [Sacre du Printemps](https://en.wikisource.org/w/index.php?title=Sacre_du_Printemps&action=edit&redlink=1) and a good deal more of this Russian's music. I wrote a panegyric about Le Sacre du Printemps in my first book,[[1]](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Red#cite_note-1) published in 1915. In 1924, Le Sacre du Printemps has at last been performed in New York and the critics have at last accepted Stravinsky. So, indeed, apparently, have the conductors who, until the past four years, hardly ever permitted his name to be emblazoned on their programs. The job of music critic in New York, therefore, is certainly not an ideal occupation for a man with imagination and foresight.

A more compelling reason for my departure from the field of musical criticism was my persistent sense of being significantly ahead of my peers, including both critics and the orchestral conductors responsible for programming. While I was not present in New York during the period when Wagner was widely criticized, I have observed a recurring pattern among critics: the initial rejection of figures like Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, followed by eventual acceptance once repeated exposure made their innovations more palatable. Over time, these composers came to be regarded as integral to the musical canon.

My foundational exposure to orchestral music took place under the direction of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. While Thomas may not have been an exceptional conductor, he consistently provided faithful interpretations of musical scores. His principal merit lay in his conviction that new compositions deserved to be heard. Consequently, he ensured that significant European works were performed domestically as soon as possible after their premieres abroad. By the time I moved to New York in 1906, I had already encountered and evaluated most of the major works by Strauss and Debussy available at that time. I was surprised to find that New York critics were still embroiled in debates about these composers, often oscillating between underappreciation and excessive praise. In my view, Strauss, for instance, has never surpassed Franz Liszt as a composer.

In 1913, while in Paris, I attended a performance of Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps* and became acquainted with several other works by the same composer. I expressed my admiration for *Le Sacre du Printemps* in my first book, published in 1915. Yet it was not until 1924 that this piece was performed in New York, where critics and conductors alike finally began to acknowledge Stravinsky’s significance. Prior to the past few years, his name had rarely appeared on concert programs. For someone possessing imagination and foresight, the role of music critic in New York is decidedly uninspiring.

In my view, Strauss, for instance, has never surpassed Franz Liszt as a composer.

In 1913, while in Paris, I attended a performance of Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps* and became acquainted with several other works by the same composer. I expressed my admiration for *Le Sacre du Printemps* in my first book, published in 1915. Yet it was not until 1924 that this piece was performed in New York, where critics and conductors alike finally began to acknowledge Stravinsky’s significance. Prior to the past few years, his name had rarely appeared on concert programs. For someone possessing imagination and foresight, the role of music critic in New York is decidedly uninspiring.

A still more profound impetus catalyzed my departure from the domain of musical criticism. It became evident that my aesthetic sensibilities operated on a temporal wavelength perpetually ahead of the prevailing judgments of both critics and the orchestral architects who curate the concert repertoire. My encounters with the cyclical vilification and subsequent canonization of composers unfolded like a ritualistic dance of delayed comprehension. Though I bypassed the era of Wagner’s denunciation in New York, I bore witness to the critical establishment’s successive repudiations of Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky—each initially dismissed, only to be sanctified once repeated exposure had acclimated ears to the unfamiliar tonal architectures. 

My own education in orchestral music was cultivated under the stewardship of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. While Thomas himself was not a conductor of transcendent brilliance, his interpretative integrity and a singular philosophical commitment elevated his legacy: he espoused the conviction that the unfamiliar and the avant-garde deserved their moment in the acoustic arena. Thus, under his baton, every seminal European composition found its way into performance shortly after its continental debut, ensuring that Chicago resonated with the echoes of progress. Consequently, when I arrived in New York in 1906, armed with firsthand familiarity with the principal works of Strauss and Debussy, I was startled to discover that the city’s critics were embroiled in puerile skirmishes over these composers—alternately undervaluing and overexalting them. Strauss, for instance, struck me as no more accomplished than Liszt, despite the hyperbolic fluctuations of critical regard.

By 1913, in Paris, I experienced Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps*—a seismic eruption of musical innovation—and immersed myself in the broader corpus of his creations. My inaugural book, published in 1915, extolled *Le Sacre* as a watershed moment. Yet it was not until 1924 that New York audiences heard this masterpiece, and not until then did the city’s critics and conductors reluctantly align their judgments with the inevitable. The cultural inertia of New York’s musical criticism, its sluggish reconciliation with the future, renders it an inhospitable vocation for one possessed of vision and audacity.

There was an even stronger reason why I gave up being a music critic. I always seemed to be about ten years ahead of most other critics and the conductors who chose what to put on concert programs. I wasn’t around to see the backlash against Wagner in New York, but I did watch the critics take turns attacking Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky. Yet, in time, as they heard the new sounds over and over, they got used to them. Eventually, they accepted these composers as part of the musical elite. 

My own exposure to orchestral music came from Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Thomas wasn’t a great conductor, though he always gave honest performances of the works he conducted. But he had one admirable quality: he believed in giving new music a chance. He made sure to perform every major European piece as soon as he could after its debut abroad. Thanks to him, by the time I arrived in New York in 1906, I had already heard and formed opinions on nearly all of the important works by Strauss and Debussy up to that point. I was shocked to find that New York critics were still arguing about these composers. At first, they didn’t give them enough credit, and then they went too far in the other direction. Personally, I’ve never thought Strauss was any better as a composer than someone like Liszt.

In Paris, in 1913, I had the chance to hear Stravinsky’s *The Rite of Spring* and more of his music. I praised *The Rite of Spring* in my first book, published in 1915. But it wasn’t until 1924 that *The Rite of Spring* finally made it to a New York stage, and only then did the critics and conductors start to embrace Stravinsky. Before that, conductors hardly ever included his work in their programs.

With all this in mind, it’s clear to me that being a music critic in New York is not the ideal job for someone who has imagination and can see what’s coming.

A more compelling reason for my departure from the field of musical criticism was my persistent sense of being significantly ahead of my peers, including both critics and the orchestral conductors responsible for programming. While I was not present in New York during the period when Wagner was widely criticized, I have observed a recurring pattern among critics: the initial rejection of figures like Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, followed by eventual acceptance once repeated exposure made their innovations more palatable. Over time, these composers came to be regarded as integral to the musical canon.

My foundational exposure to orchestral music took place under the direction of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. While Thomas may not have been an exceptional conductor, he consistently provided faithful interpretations of musical scores. His principal merit lay in his conviction that new compositions deserved to be heard. Consequently, he ensured that significant European works were performed domestically as soon as possible after their premieres abroad. There was, lurking in the shadows of my departure from the critic’s chair, a reason more potent than all others, an ember that burned hotter than mere dissatisfaction or fatigue. I seemed forever to stride ten years ahead of the pack—the critics, the maestros, the steely-eyed architects of concert programs—always ten steps forward, always alone. I missed the venomous uproar that Wagner provoked in New York, but I stood witness to the press’s ritual savaging of Richard Strauss, then Debussy, then Stravinsky, as if they were wild beasts to be tamed. And yet, as the years wore on, as the ears of these learned men grew calloused by the battering waves of new tonal colors, each of these composers was duly anointed, their crowns polished, their names carved into the sacred marble of musical canon.

My own apprenticeship in orchestral music unfolded under the banner of Theodore Thomas in Chicago—a man whose baton, though not touched by greatness, was guided by honesty. He read scores faithfully, but what set him apart was his fierce conviction that the new deserved its day in the sun. If some brilliant work had just unfurled its wings in Europe, Thomas would see to it that it landed swiftly on American soil. Thanks to him, by the time I set foot in New York in 1906, my ears had already drunk deeply from the wells of Strauss and Debussy, their major works laid bare before me. Yet to my astonishment, I found New York’s critics still embroiled in petty skirmishes over these composers—first dismissing them with sneers, then, in a dizzy swing of the pendulum, showering them with excess praise. Strauss, I dare say, has never risen above the level of Liszt in my eyes.

In Paris, 1913, I heard Stravinsky’s *Le Sacre du Printemps*, a feral, electrifying tempest of sound that shook me to my marrow. I sang its praises in my first book, published two years later. And now, in 1924, New York has finally caught up—*Le Sacre* has been performed, the critics have folded their arms in begrudging approval, and the conductors, once timid, now dare to write Stravinsky’s name in bold upon their programs. But for a man with imagination and the gift of foresight, the critic’s desk in New York is a poor perch indeed.

There was a still more pregnant reason for my desertion of the camp of musical criticism. I seemed always to be about ten years ahead of most of the other critics and the orchestral conductors who make out programs. I missed the reviling of Wagner in New York, but I have watched the pundits of the press revile, in turn, Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, only, in the end, after their ears, through repeated hearings, had grown accustomed to the new clang-tints, to accept these composers as part of the sacred hierarchy. My orchestral education was carried on under Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Now Thomas was not a great conductor, although he always gave honest musical readings of his scores, but he had one great virtue: he believed that new music should be heard. He performed, therefore, every important European composition as soon as possible after it had been performed abroad. As a result, when I arrived in New York in 1906, having listened to and appraised nearly all the major works of Strauss and Debussy composed up to that date, I was amazed to discover that the New York critics were still fighting about these composers, first underpraising them and a little later overpraising them, for Strauss, at any rate, to my mind, has never been a whit superior as a composer to such a man as Liszt. In Paris, in 1913, I heard Stravinsky's [Sacre du Printemps](https://en.wikisource.org/w/index.php?title=Sacre_du_Printemps&action=edit&redlink=1) and a good deal more of this Russian's music. I wrote a panegyric about Le Sacre du Printemps in my first book,[[1]](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Red#cite_note-1) published in 1915. In 1924, Le Sacre du Printemps has at last been performed in New York and the critics have at last accepted Stravinsky. So, indeed, apparently, have the conductors who, until the past four years, hardly ever permitted his name to be emblazoned on their programs. The job of music critic in New York, therefore, is certainly not an ideal occupation for a man with imagination and foresight.

In my view, Strauss has not demonstrated superior compositional ability compared to Liszt. In 1913, while in Paris, I listened to Stravinsky's *Sacre du Printemps* and several other works by this Russian composer. I praised *Le Sacre du Printemps* in my first book, published in 1915. By 1924, *Le Sacre du Printemps* was finally performed in New York, and the critics had, at last, accepted Stravinsky. Similarly, conductors, who had largely omitted his name from their programs until the previous four years, seemed to have acknowledged him as well. Therefore, the role of music critic in New York is not well suited for an individual possessing imagination and foresight.

A significant reason for my departure from the field of musical criticism was my consistent temporal disjunction from the prevailing views of other critics and orchestral conductors responsible for program selections. I did not witness the derision directed at Wagner in New York; however, I observed that the critics systematically disparaged Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky. Eventually, after repeated exposure, they adjusted their perceptions and accepted these composers into the established canon. My formal orchestral education occurred under Theodore Thomas in Chicago. Although Thomas was not an exceptional conductor, he provided authentic interpretations of the scores he conducted. His notable principle was the belief that new music deserved to be performed. Consequently, he introduced every significant European composition shortly after its premiere abroad. When I arrived in New York in 1906, I had already listened to and critically evaluated nearly all the major works of Strauss and Debussy composed up to that point. I was surprised to find that New York critics were still engaged in debates regarding these composers, initially undervaluing them and subsequently overvaluing them. In my view, Strauss has not demonstrated superior compositional ability compared to Liszt. Similarly, conductors, who had largely omitted his name from their programs until the previous four years, seemed to have acknowledged him as well. Therefore, the role of music critic in New York is not well suited for an individual possessing imagination and foresight.

A more profound rationale underpinned my exodus from the realm of musical criticism. It appeared that I perpetually inhabited a temporal plane at least a decade ahead of my contemporaries—the critics and orchestral conductors who orchestrate concert programs. While I missed the cacophony of disdain directed at Wagner in New York, I have observed, with a sort of detached curiosity, the critical establishment revile Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky in turn, only to ultimately embrace these composers as integral components of the revered pantheon, once their sonic innovations had sufficiently permeated the collective auditory palate. My formative orchestral education unfolded under the tutelage of Theodore Thomas in Chicago. His singular virtue lay in his conviction that new music merited an audience; thus, he endeavored to present every significant European composition shortly after its debut abroad. Consequently, upon my arrival in New York in 1906, armed with a comprehensive understanding of the major works of Strauss and Debussy, I found myself incredulous at the ongoing skirmishes among New York critics—first relegating these composers to the shadows of neglect, only to later elevate them to the heights of excessive adulation. In Paris, in the year 1913, I was privileged to experience Stravinsky's *Sacre du Printemps* along with a considerable array of the Russian’s oeuvre. I extolled the virtues of *Le Sacre du Printemps* in my inaugural book, published in 1915. By 1924, the piece had finally graced the New York stage, and the critics, at long last, appeared to acknowledge Stravinsky's significance. This newfound acceptance extended, apparently, to the conductors who, until the preceding four years, scarcely permitted his name to adorn their concert programs. Thus, the role of a music critic in New York emerges as decidedly ill-suited for one endowed with imagination and foresight.

There was another important reason I left the camp of music critics. I often felt I was about ten years ahead of most critics and orchestra conductors who put together concert programs. I missed the criticism of Wagner in New York, but I have seen the press attack Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky. He performed every important European work soon after it debuted abroad. So, when I arrived in New York in 1906, I was surprised to find the critics still arguing about Strauss and Debussy. They would praise them too little at first and then later praise them too much. I believed Strauss had never been better than a composer like Liszt.

It seemed that the conductors had too, as they had rarely included his name in their programs until the last few years. Because of all this, being a music critic in New York was not the best job for someone with imagination and vision.

A deeper, more visceral reason gnawed at my soul, urging me to abandon the camp of musical criticism. I felt as if I were always ten years ahead of the pack—those critics and orchestral conductors fumbling about like blindfolded children. Ah, how I missed the cacophony of Wagner’s vilification echoing through New York’s streets! Yet, I witnessed the same fate befall Richard Strauss, Debussy, and Stravinsky, as the press pundits hurled their barbs. But give it time, like a fine wine steeping in darkness, and the public's ears would soften, embracing these composers as if they were sacred relics. 

My musical education bloomed under the watchful eye of Theodore Thomas in the vibrant heart of Chicago. Now, let’s be clear: Thomas wasn’t a titan of the podium, but he wielded one glorious truth—new music deserved an audience. He brought forth every vital European composition, fresh from its birth abroad, into the eager hands of our own hungry ears. So, when I landed in New York in 1906, my heart racing with anticipation, I was stunned to find the critics still engaged in a bitter duel over these composers—first casting them into the shadows, then dragging them into the spotlight, only to let Strauss linger in an uneasy limbo, a mere whisper of greatness alongside Liszt.

In Paris, 1913, I found myself entranced by Stravinsky's *Sacre du Printemps*, enveloped in the wild, untamed beauty of his music. My pen flowed with praise for the piece in my debut book, published in 1915—a heartfelt ode to a masterpiece! But lo and behold, it wasn’t until 1924 that *Le Sacre du Printemps* finally graced the New York stage, and oh, how the critics, like reluctant moths, began to embrace Stravinsky’s genius. Conductors, too, had finally donned the armor of acceptance, after years of shunning his name like a dark secret. Thus, the role of a music critic in New York, a place where imagination and foresight are often cast adrift, was hardly a sanctuary for a visionary spirit.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute at the county-seat; and there distinguished guests of the superintendent taught the teachers fractions and spelling and other mysteries,—white teachers in the morning, Negroes at night. A picnic now and then, and a supper, and the rough world was softened by laughter and song. I remember how— But I wander. There came a day when all the teachers left the Institute and began the hunt for schools. I learn from hearsay (for my mother was mortally afraid of fire-arms) that the hunting of ducks and bears and men is wonderfully interesting, but I am sure that the man who has never hunted a country school has something to learn of the pleasures of the chase. I see now the white, hot roads lazily rise and fall and wind before me under the burning July sun; I feel the deep weariness of heart and limb as ten, eight, six miles stretch relentlessly ahead; I feel my heart sink heavily as I hear again and again, "Got a teacher? Yes." So I walked on and on—horses were too expensive—until I had wandered beyond railways, beyond stage lines, to a land of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the coming of a stranger was an event, and men lived and died in the shadow of one blue hill.

At the outset, there was a Teachers' Institute held at the county seat. During this event, distinguished guests invited by the superintendent provided instruction to teachers on subjects such as fractions, spelling, and other academic topics. White teachers attended these sessions in the morning, while African American teachers participated in the evening. Occasionally, a picnic or a supper was organized, introducing moments of levity where the harsh realities of life were temporarily softened by laughter and music. I recall vividly—though I digress. Eventually, the time arrived when all the teachers departed from the Institute to begin the search for schools. While I lack personal experience with hunting (as my mother had a pronounced fear of firearms), I have heard it said that pursuing ducks, bears, or even other humans can be an enthralling activity. However, I am convinced that an individual who has never sought out a rural school has yet to experience a particular kind of challenge. I can still picture the dusty, sun-scorched roads rising, falling, and twisting endlessly under the oppressive heat of July. I can recall the profound exhaustion that gripped both my body and spirit as mile after mile stretched ahead—ten, eight, six—unrelenting. Time and again, my spirits would sink as I was met with the same response: "Do you already have a teacher? Yes." Thus, I continued my journey on foot, as horses were beyond my financial means, wandering farther and farther—beyond the reach of railroads and stage lines—into a remote region inhabited by wildlife and rattlesnakes. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a notable occurrence, and life and death unfolded quietly under the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

While I lack personal experience with hunting (as my mother had a pronounced fear of firearms), I have heard it said that pursuing ducks, bears, or even other humans can be an enthralling activity. However, I am convinced that an individual who has never sought out a rural school has yet to experience a particular kind of challenge. I can still picture the dusty, sun-scorched roads rising, falling, and twisting endlessly under the oppressive heat of July. I can recall the profound exhaustion that gripped both my body and spirit as mile after mile stretched ahead—ten, eight, six—unrelenting. Time and again, my spirits would sink as I was met with the same response: "Do you already have a teacher? Yes." Thus, I continued my journey on foot, as horses were beyond my financial means, wandering farther and farther—beyond the reach of railroads and stage lines—into a remote region inhabited by wildlife and rattlesnakes. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a notable occurrence, and life and death unfolded quietly under the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

At the outset, there was a Teachers' Institute held at the county seat. During this event, distinguished guests invited by the superintendent provided instruction to teachers on subjects such as fractions, spelling, and other academic topics. White teachers attended these sessions in the morning, while African American teachers participated in the evening. Occasionally, a picnic or a supper was organized, introducing moments of levity where the harsh realities of life were temporarily softened by laughter and music. I recall vividly—though I digress. Eventually, the time arrived when all the teachers departed from the Institute to begin the search for schools. However, I am convinced that an individual who has never sought out a rural school has yet to experience a particular kind of challenge. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a notable occurrence, and life and death unfolded quietly under the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

First, there came the Teachers' Institute, perched proudly at the county-seat like a rooster on its roost. There, in the superintendent’s court of knowledge, his chosen sages unraveled the tangled skeins of fractions and spelling, those cryptic arts—white teachers in the light of morning, Negro teachers cloaked in night’s velvet. A picnic here, a supper there, laughter and song weaving a balm over the day’s sharp edges. I could tell you more—oh, how I could—but my thoughts slip away like a kite torn loose.  

And then, like a great migration, the teachers scattered, loosed from the Institute, hunting their schools as if they were quarry. My mother, who harbored a terror of firearms, had no tales of duck hunts or bear stalks, but I swear there’s no sport keener than chasing a country school. I can still see it: the roads, pale as bone, writhing under July’s cruel sun, rising, falling, twisting like something alive. The slog of it—mile after mile, ten then eight then six, each step heavier than the last. And always that crushing refrain, “Got a teacher? Yes.”  

Onward I trudged, horses being luxuries of another world, until the tracks and stage lines vanished behind me, and I entered the wilds. Here, where the sky leaned down to kiss a solitary blue hill, strangers were rare as comets. Here, in a land of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, life seemed to hover, suspended, between the sharp snap of birth and the quiet rustle of death.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute held at the county seat. The superintendent invited special guests who taught the teachers about fractions, spelling, and other subjects—white teachers attended in the morning, while Black teachers came at night. Sometimes there were picnics and suppers, moments where the hard edges of life softened with laughter and song. I remember how—well, never mind. Eventually, the Institute ended, and the teachers set out to find schools to teach. People say hunting ducks, bears, or even men can be exciting (though my mother was too scared of guns to talk much about it), but I believe those who've never hunted for a school in the countryside have missed one of life's odd adventures.

I can still picture the roads stretching ahead, dusty and white, twisting up and down under the blistering July sun. I can still feel the heavy weariness in my body and the ache in my heart as mile after mile passed. And I can hear the disappointment of the repeated answer: "Got a teacher? Yes." So I kept walking—horses cost too much—and wandered far beyond the railways and stagecoach routes. I ended up in a place full of varmints and rattlesnakes, where strangers were rare, and people lived and died in the shadow of a single blue hill.

Initially, there unfolded the Teachers' Institute at the county seat—a convening wherein the superintendent’s esteemed colleagues imparted their pedagogical wisdom to the assembled educators, unraveling the enigmatic intricacies of fractions, orthography, and other scholastic arcana. The morning belonged to white instructors, while the evening turned its mantle toward their Black counterparts. Interspersed throughout were picnics and communal suppers, occasions where the jagged contours of life’s hardships were momentarily smoothed by the balm of mirth and melody. I am tempted to linger on personal recollections—yet I digress. Soon, the Institute dispersed, and its participants embarked upon a singular pursuit: the quest for schools. 

Though I am told by those more venturesome than my fire-arm-fearing mother that the hunting of ducks, bears, and men bears an exhilarating allure, I hold steadfast in my conviction that the search for a rural school possesses its own peculiar savor of the chase. I recall, as though etched in the marrow of memory, the undulating contours of sun-scorched roads, their languid rise and fall under the oppressive glare of July. Each mile—ten, eight, six—stretched onward with merciless indifference, amplifying the ache in spirit and sinew alike. Rejections reverberated in my ears, each blunt “Got a teacher? Yes,” a weight upon my sinking heart. Step after laborious step, I trudged forward—horses being an unattainable luxury—pressing beyond railways and stagecoach routes into territories remote and untamed. Here lay a realm of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of a stranger constituted an epochal disturbance, and human lives unfolded, and concluded, under the eternal silhouette of a solitary blue hill.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute at the county-seat; and there distinguished guests of the superintendent taught the teachers fractions and spelling and other mysteries,—white teachers in the morning, Negroes at night. A picnic now and then, and a supper, and the rough world was softened by laughter and song. I remember how— But I wander. There came a day when all the teachers left the Institute and began the hunt for schools. I learn from hearsay (for my mother was mortally afraid of fire-arms) that the hunting of ducks and bears and men is wonderfully interesting, but I am sure that the man who has never hunted a country school has something to learn of the pleasures of the chase. I see now the white, hot roads lazily rise and fall and wind before me under the burning July sun; I feel the deep weariness of heart and limb as ten, eight, six miles stretch relentlessly ahead; I feel my heart sink heavily as I hear again and again, "Got a teacher? Yes." So I walked on and on—horses were too expensive—until I had wandered beyond railways, beyond stage lines, to a land of "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the coming of a stranger was an event, and men lived and died in the shadow of one blue hill.

Initially, there was a Teachers' Institute located at the county seat, where notable guests invited by the superintendent instructed teachers in subjects such as fractions and spelling. White educators taught in the morning, while African American educators provided instruction in the evening. Occasionally, there were social gatherings like picnics and suppers, which temporarily alleviated the hardships of daily life through shared laughter and music. However, I digress. 

A significant transition occurred when all the teachers departed from the Institute to seek employment in schools. According to hearsay—since my mother had a strong aversion to firearms—the pursuit of hunting ducks, bears, and even men is said to be quite engaging. Nevertheless, I assert that an individual who has never sought a position at a rural school has much to discover regarding the enjoyment of such an endeavor. 

I now visualize the sun-baked roads, which rise and fall gently while meandering before me under the intense heat of July. I experience the profound fatigue in both spirit and body as I traverse distances of ten, eight, and six miles that seem to stretch endlessly ahead. My heart grows heavy each time I hear the repeated inquiry, "Got a teacher? Yes." Consequently, I continued my journey on foot—since horses were prohibitively expensive—until I found myself beyond railroads and stage routes, in a region populated by various wildlife and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of an outsider was a notable occurrence, and where individuals lived and perished in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

First, there was a grand Teachers' Institute, a buzzing hive at the county-seat, where the air shimmered with knowledge, and distinguished guests, like wise wizards, imparted their secrets to the eager teachers—fractions and spelling, those enchanting spells. By day, white teachers conjured enlightenment; by night, the magic shifted, as Negroes took the stage, weaving their own tales of wisdom. Picnics blossomed like wildflowers, laughter danced on the breeze, and song wrapped the rough world in a soft embrace. Ah, but I digress. 

Then came the day when all the teachers, restless as the wind, left the Institute, embarking on a quest for schools. I learned through whispers, for my mother, wrapped in a cloak of fear of fire-arms, kept me at bay from such tales, that hunting ducks, bears, and men holds a thrill, but oh, to chase a country school! That’s where true adventure lies! I can feel the white-hot roads unfurling like a ribbon of molten silver beneath the relentless July sun, rising and falling, twisting like a serpent. My heart, a heavy stone, sinks as I trudge through ten, eight, six miles of endless yearning. “Got a teacher?” echoes in my ears, always the same response—“Yes.” So I walked on and on, for horses were luxuries I could not afford, wandering beyond the railways, past the stage lines, into a wild frontier of “varmints” and rattlesnakes, where strangers were a spectacle, and life unfolded in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute in the county seat. There, important guests from the superintendent taught the teachers about fractions, spelling, and other subjects—white teachers in the morning, Black teachers at night. Occasionally, there were picnics and suppers, bringing laughter and song to soften the rough world. I remember those days, but I get sidetracked. 

One day, all the teachers left the Institute to search for schools. I heard from others, since my mother was very scared of guns, that hunting for ducks, bears, and even men is quite exciting. But I believe that anyone who has never hunted for a country school is missing out on a special kind of adventure. 

I can picture the bright, hot roads that rise and fall under the burning July sun. I remember the deep tiredness in my heart and body as I walked ten, eight, or six miles with no end in sight. My heart sank every time I heard, "Got a teacher? Yes." So, I kept walking—horses were too costly for me—until I found myself far beyond railways and stage lines, in a land filled with "varmints" and rattlesnakes. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a big event, and people lived and died in the shadow of one blue hill.

I learned through whispers, for my mother, wrapped in a cloak of fear of fire-arms, kept me at bay from such tales, that hunting ducks, bears, and men holds a thrill, but oh, to chase a country school! That’s where true adventure lies! I can feel the white-hot roads unfurling like a ribbon of molten silver beneath the relentless July sun, rising and falling, twisting like a serpent. My heart, a heavy stone, sinks as I trudge through ten, eight, six miles of endless yearning. “Got a teacher?” echoes in my ears, always the same response—“Yes.” So I walked on and on, for horses were luxuries I could not afford, wandering beyond the railways, past the stage lines, into a wild frontier of “varmints” and rattlesnakes, where strangers were a spectacle, and life unfolded in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

Initially, there was a Teachers' Institute located at the county seat, where notable guests invited by the superintendent instructed teachers in subjects such as fractions and spelling. White educators taught in the morning, while African American educators provided instruction in the evening. Occasionally, there were social gatherings like picnics and suppers, which temporarily alleviated the hardships of daily life through shared laughter and music. However, I digress. 

A significant transition occurred when all the teachers departed from the Institute to seek employment in schools. According to hearsay—since my mother had a strong aversion to firearms—the pursuit of hunting ducks, bears, and even men is said to be quite engaging. Nevertheless, I assert that an individual who has never sought a position at a rural school has much to discover regarding the enjoyment of such an endeavor. 

I now visualize the sun-baked roads, which rise and fall gently while meandering before me under the intense heat of July. I experience the profound fatigue in both spirit and body as I traverse distances of ten, eight, and six miles that seem to stretch endlessly ahead. My heart grows heavy each time I hear the repeated inquiry, "Got a teacher? Yes." Consequently, I continued my journey on foot—since horses were prohibitively expensive—until I found myself beyond railroads and stage routes, in a region populated by various wildlife and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of an outsider was a notable occurrence, and where individuals lived and perished in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

First, there was a Teachers' Institute in the county seat. There, important guests from the superintendent taught the teachers about fractions, spelling, and other subjects—white teachers in the morning, Black teachers at night. Occasionally, there were picnics and suppers, bringing laughter and song to soften the rough world. I remember those days, but I get sidetracked. 

One day, all the teachers left the Institute to search for schools. I heard from others, since my mother was very scared of guns, that hunting for ducks, bears, and even men is quite exciting. But I believe that anyone who has never hunted for a country school is missing out on a special kind of adventure. 

I can picture the bright, hot roads that rise and fall under the burning July sun. I remember the deep tiredness in my heart and body as I walked ten, eight, or six miles with no end in sight. My heart sank every time I heard, "Got a teacher? Yes." So, I kept walking—horses were too costly for me—until I found myself far beyond railways and stage lines, in a land filled with "varmints" and rattlesnakes. Here, the arrival of a stranger was a big event, and people lived and died in the shadow of one blue hill.
