In the nascent days of pedagogical exploration, the county seat hosted a Teachers' Institute, a crucible of enlightenment where esteemed guests, summoned by the superintendent, imparted knowledge on the complexities of fractions, spelling, and other esoteric doctrines—white educators graced the morning hours while their Black counterparts filled the nocturnal void. Interludes of jovial picnics and communal suppers punctuated this intellectual endeavor, weaving laughter and song into the fabric of an otherwise harsh existence. Yet, I find myself digressing. A pivotal moment arrived when the collective of educators departed the Institute, embarking on the arduous quest for schools. From fragments of hearsay—my mother, gripped by an abiding fear of firearms, seldom ventured into such realms—I glean that the pursuit of ducks, bears, and even men can be exhilarating; yet, I contend that one who has never engaged in the hunt for a rural school is deprived of profound insights into the joys of the chase. I now envision the searing, undulating roads, shimmering beneath the oppressive July sun, each rise and fall echoing the deep fatigue that envelops both heart and body as ten, eight, six miles stretch interminably before me. With each repeated inquiry, "Got a teacher? Yes," my spirit sinks further, compelling me to traverse on foot—horses deemed too extravagant—until I wander beyond railways and stage lines, into a realm inhabited by "varmints" and rattlesnakes, where the arrival of an outsider marks a significant occurrence, and existence unfolds in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

First, there was a grand Teachers' Institute, a buzzing hive at the county-seat, where the air shimmered with knowledge, and distinguished guests, like wise wizards, imparted their secrets to the eager teachers—fractions and spelling, those enchanting spells. By day, white teachers conjured enlightenment; by night, the magic shifted, as Negroes took the stage, weaving their own tales of wisdom. Picnics blossomed like wildflowers, laughter danced on the breeze, and song wrapped the rough world in a soft embrace. Ah, but I digress. 

Then came the day when all the teachers, restless as the wind, left the Institute, embarking on a quest for schools. I learned through whispers, for my mother, wrapped in a cloak of fear of fire-arms, kept me at bay from such tales, that hunting ducks, bears, and men holds a thrill, but oh, to chase a country school! That’s where true adventure lies! I can feel the white-hot roads unfurling like a ribbon of molten silver beneath the relentless July sun, rising and falling, twisting like a serpent. My heart, a heavy stone, sinks as I trudge through ten, eight, six miles of endless yearning. “Got a teacher?” echoes in my ears, always the same response—“Yes.” So I walked on and on, for horses were luxuries I could not afford, wandering beyond the railways, past the stage lines, into a wild frontier of “varmints” and rattlesnakes, where strangers were a spectacle, and life unfolded in the shadow of a solitary blue hill.

Sprinkled over hill and dale lay cabins and farmhouses, shut out from the world by the forests and the rolling hills toward the east. There I found at last a little school. Josie told me of it; she was a thin, homely girl of twenty, with a dark-brown face and thick, hard hair. I had crossed the stream at Watertown, and rested under the great willows; then I had gone to the little cabin in the lot where Josie was resting on her way to town. The gaunt farmer made me welcome, and Josie, hearing my errand, told me anxiously that they wanted a school over the hill; that but once since the war had a teacher been there; that she herself longed to learn,—and thus she ran on, talking fast and loud, with much earnestness and energy.

Scattered like tossed seeds across the hills and hollows, the cabins and farmhouses crouched in their solitude, hemmed in by the brooding forests and the undulating eastern slopes. It was there, at last, that I stumbled upon a tiny school. Josie had spoken of it—a wiry, plain girl of twenty, her face dark as rich earth, her hair coarse and thick as bramble. I had crossed the stream at Watertown, pausing beneath the sprawling arms of the great willows, their shade cool and heavy. From there, I wandered to the little cabin perched in a rough patch of land, where Josie sat, catching her breath on her way to town. The lanky farmer welcomed me with a nod, and Josie, her eyes alive with purpose, leaned forward to share her tale. They needed a school over the hill, she said, her words tumbling out fast and fiery. Only once since the war had a teacher come. She herself ached to learn, her voice rising with a raw, urgent hunger as she spoke.

Scattered across the hills and valleys were cabins and farmhouses, tucked away from the world by the forests and the rolling eastern hills. It was there that I finally came upon a small school. Josie had told me about it. She was a thin, plain young woman, about twenty years old, with a dark-brown face and thick, coarse hair. I had crossed the stream at Watertown and stopped to rest under the big willow trees. Afterward, I made my way to a little cabin in a field, where Josie was taking a break on her way to town. The lean farmer welcomed me, and when Josie heard why I had come, she eagerly shared that they needed a school on the other side of the hill. She said there had been a teacher there only once since the war. She spoke quickly and passionately, saying how much she herself wanted to learn.

Scattered across the hills and valleys were cabins and farmhouses, isolated from broader society by the surrounding forests and the undulating terrain to the east. It was in this area that I eventually came upon a small school. Josie, a slender, unassuming young woman of twenty with a dark-brown complexion and coarse, thick hair, had informed me about it. Prior to this, I had crossed the stream at Watertown and rested beneath the large willow trees. Afterward, I proceeded to a modest cabin situated in a clearing, where I encountered Josie as she paused on her way into town. The lean farmer there extended his hospitality, and upon learning of my purpose, Josie spoke with noticeable urgency. She explained that there was a need for a school beyond the hill, as only one teacher had served the area since the war. She expressed her own strong desire for education and continued speaking rapidly, with evident determination and vigor.

Scattered across the undulating landscape of hills and hollows, cabins and farmhouses reclined in solitary isolation, veiled from the larger world by the dense embrace of forests and the undulating eastern ridges. Amidst this seclusion, I eventually encountered a modest schoolhouse, the existence of which Josie had revealed to me. Josie, a slender and unassuming young woman of twenty, bore a dark-brown visage and hair coarse as unyielding rope. My journey had taken me across the Watertown stream, where I paused beneath the expansive willows, and then onwards to a humble cabin nestled in a small clearing, where Josie was temporarily resting before continuing her errand into town. The lanky farmer greeted me with a rough-hewn hospitality, and upon hearing my purpose, Josie spoke with fervent urgency. She described a yearning for education in the community beyond the hill, lamenting that, since the war, a teacher had come but once. Her own hunger for knowledge burned brightly as she poured out her words, swift and impassioned, her earnestness carrying the weight of her hopes.

Scattered like tossed seeds across the hills and hollows, the cabins and farmhouses crouched in their solitude, hemmed in by the brooding forests and the undulating eastern slopes. It was there, at last, that I stumbled upon a tiny school. Josie had spoken of it—a wiry, plain girl of twenty, her face dark as rich earth, her hair coarse and thick as bramble. I had crossed the stream at Watertown, pausing beneath the sprawling arms of the great willows, their shade cool and heavy. From there, I wandered to the little cabin perched in a rough patch of land, where Josie sat, catching her breath on her way to town. The lanky farmer welcomed me with a nod, and Josie, her eyes alive with purpose, leaned forward to share her tale. They needed a school over the hill, she said, her words tumbling out fast and fiery. Only once since the war had a teacher come. She herself ached to learn, her voice rising with a raw, urgent hunger as she spoke.

Sprinkled over hill and dale lay cabins and farmhouses, shut out from the world by the forests and the rolling hills toward the east. There I found at last a little school. Josie told me of it; she was a thin, homely girl of twenty, with a dark-brown face and thick, hard hair. I had crossed the stream at Watertown, and rested under the great willows; then I had gone to the little cabin in the lot where Josie was resting on her way to town. The gaunt farmer made me welcome, and Josie, hearing my errand, told me anxiously that they wanted a school over the hill; that but once since the war had a teacher been there; that she herself longed to learn,—and thus she ran on, talking fast and loud, with much earnestness and energy.

Cabins and farmhouses, like scattered jewels, nestled in the embrace of rolling hills and dense forests, stood sentinel against the world beyond. It was here, in this hidden paradise, that I stumbled upon a little school. Josie, a wiry, unassuming girl of twenty, with a face the color of rich, dark earth and hair as unruly as a wild thicket, became my guide. I had just crossed the bubbling stream at Watertown and sought refuge beneath the sprawling willows, their branches dancing in the wind. Venturing to a modest cabin where Josie rested on her way to town, I was greeted by a gaunt farmer who welcomed me with open arms. But it was Josie’s voice that captured my attention—urgent and vibrant, she spilled out her heart. They needed a teacher over the hill, she said, and since the war, only once had a teacher graced their land. Her yearning to learn poured forth, a torrent of hope and energy.

Scattered across the undulating landscape, a constellation of cabins and farmhouses finds itself ensconced within the embrace of dense forests and the undulating hills that stretch towards the east. In this remote haven, I ultimately stumbled upon a modest educational institution. It was Josie, a slender and unassuming twenty-year-old with a visage of deep brown and resilient hair, who illuminated my path. After traversing the stream at Watertown, I took refuge beneath the sprawling willows, subsequently making my way to the diminutive cabin where Josie rested en route to the town. The gaunt farmer extended his hospitality, and upon learning of my purpose, Josie, with palpable urgency, conveyed the community's yearning for a school situated over the hill. She lamented that only once since the tumult of war had a teacher graced their presence, and her own fervent desire to learn bubbled forth, her words cascading in a torrent of earnestness and vitality.

While we have been learning calculus and economics; the theories of versification and genetics; conversational French and Ibsen, it is helpful to be reminded that the home-folks have not been altogether standing still—that Frank Chumbley is developing social finesse and religious fervor in circulating the ice cream, that Jessie Siegler is cultivating a love for the beautiful through the embroidery club, and that McKinley Miller is taking an elementary course in home economics by correspondence and by an occasional private lesson from the girl at Odin.

There is no agency like the home paper for developing a broad human interest and for keeping us in touch with the friendships and the ideals of our youth.

While we have been studying subjects such as calculus, economics, versification, genetics, conversational French, and the works of Ibsen, it is worthwhile to acknowledge that those back home have also been progressing in their own ways. For instance, Frank Chumbley is enhancing his social skills and deepening his religious commitment while distributing ice cream. Similarly, Jessie Siegler is fostering an appreciation for aesthetic beauty through her involvement in the embroidery club. Meanwhile, McKinley Miller is acquiring foundational knowledge in home economics through a correspondence course supplemented by occasional private instruction from a young woman in Odin.

The local newspaper serves as an unparalleled resource for fostering broad human engagement and maintaining connections with the friendships and values that shaped our formative years.

Amid our intellectual pursuits—grappling with the intricacies of calculus and the subtleties of economics, unraveling the mechanics of poetic meter and genetic inheritance, immersing ourselves in conversational French and the dramaturgy of Ibsen—it serves us well to recall that the rhythms of life in our hometown have not idled in stagnation. Frank Chumbley refines his social acumen and religious zeal through the ritual of ice cream circulation; Jessie Siegler nurtures an aesthetic sensibility within the quiet confines of the embroidery club; McKinley Miller, meanwhile, embarks on foundational lessons in domestic management, mediated both by correspondence courses and sporadic instruction from the Odin girl.

No instrument rivals the home newspaper in its capacity to foster expansive human curiosity, bridging the distance between our present intellectual endeavors and the enduring friendships and aspirations that shaped our formative years.

While we’ve been wrestling with calculus and economics, deciphering the mysteries of genetic codes and poetic meter, stumbling through conversational French and the shadowed dramas of Ibsen, it’s good—essential, even—to remember that the old hometown hasn’t been frozen in amber. Frank Chumbley, for instance, is honing his knack for charm and devotion while ferrying ice cream like a preacher with a frosted gospel. Jessie Siegler, needle in hand, is stitching her way to a deeper reverence for beauty through the embroidery club. And McKinley Miller? He’s cracking open the basics of home economics, swapping correspondence lessons for whispered tips from Odin’s girl.

No force rivals the hometown paper for kindling a generous spirit, for tethering us to the friendships and dreams spun in the soft light of our youth.

My bands of silk and miniver Momently grew heavier; The black gauze was beggarly thin; The ermine muffled mouth and chin; I could not suck the moonlight in. Harlequin in lozenges Of love and hate, I walked in these Striped and ragged rigmaroles; Along the pavement my footsoles Trod warily on living coals. Shouldering the thoughts I loathed, In their corrupt disguises clothed, Morality I could not tear From my ribs, to leave them bare Ivory in silver air. There I walked, and there I raged; The spiritual savage caged Within my skeleton, raged afresh To feel, behind a carnal mesh, The clean bones crying in the flesh.

My garments of silk and fur became progressively burdensome with time; the black gauze offered scant protection, its thinness inadequate. The ermine concealed my mouth and chin, preventing me from drawing in the moonlight. Dressed as a harlequin, adorned with alternating patterns of love and hate, I moved through the streets in these striped and tattered ensembles. My footsteps cautiously navigated the pavement, as though treading upon smoldering embers. I bore the weight of thoughts I despised, cloaked in their deceitful appearances, unable to strip away the morality embedded within me, leaving only exposed ribs like ivory against the silvered air. Thus, I walked, filled with fury—the untamed spirit confined within my skeletal frame raging anew, tormented by the sensation of pure bones yearning beneath the constraints of flesh.

I wore my silks and furs, but they felt heavier with each moment. The thin black veil did little to shield me, and the thick fur wrapped my face so tightly I couldn’t breathe in the cool moonlight. Like a jester caught between love and hate, I moved through the streets in my tattered, mismatched clothes. Each step felt like walking on burning embers. I carried thoughts I despised, dressed up in lies I couldn’t peel away. Morality clung to me, bound tight to my chest, impossible to strip off and let my ribs gleam bare in the open air. I walked on, angry and restless, a wild spirit trapped inside my bones. I could feel the clean, aching skeleton beneath my flesh crying out for freedom.

My ribbons of silk and pale miniver tightened, their weight a slow-growing ache; the gauze, flimsy as charred paper, offered no comfort. The ermine swallowed my face whole—no moonlight could seep past its suffocating clasp. I stumbled, a harlequin caught in jagged diamonds of love and loathing, wrapped in these tattered riddles, these striped ruins of thought. Each step, cautious, pressed my soles against coals that hissed and lived. Laden with the vile masquerade of ideas I detested, I could not peel morality from my ribs, could not bare the ivory truth to the silver-breathing sky. I strode, I fumed—a feral spirit throttled inside its bone cage, thrashing to escape the sinew’s prison, to free the white cry of marrow muffled by hungering flesh.

The swathes of silk and miniver encasing me accrued an oppressive gravity, moment by moment; the frailty of black gauze betrayed its insufficiency, while the ermine smothered both breath and articulation, denying my lips the communion of moonlight. As a harlequin tessellated in the paradoxical geometry of love interwoven with hate, I traversed this labyrinth of striped, threadbare absurdities; my soles, cautious emissaries, negotiated a path over embers that seemed to pulse with sentience. Reluctantly, I bore the weight of thoughts I abhorred, shrouded in the grotesque masquerade of corrupted virtue—a moral integument I could not excise to expose the gleaming scaffolding of ribcage beneath, crystalline in the argent air. Thus I moved, seething with a primal fury as the feral spirit imprisoned within my anatomy rebelled anew, anguished by the dissonance of unblemished bone entombed in sullied flesh.

My layers of silk and fur gradually became more burdensome. The black fabric was insubstantial. The ermine concealed my mouth and chin, preventing me from absorbing the moonlight. I, a figure characterized by contrasting emotions, navigated through these disordered garments. My feet stepped cautiously on a path that felt like burning coals. I bore thoughts that I found repugnant, concealed in their deceptive appearances. I was unable to extract morality from my being, leaving me exposed in a pure and unadulterated state. As I moved, I felt intense frustration; the primal instinct within me was confined, longing to express itself through the physical body, where my true essence was obscured.

My bands of silk and fur felt heavier with each moment. The black fabric was so thin, while the warm fur covered my mouth and chin. I couldn’t breathe in the moonlight. Burdened by thoughts I detested, cloaked in their nefarious guises, the essence of morality clung to my being, an indelible weight upon my ribs, rendering them a stark ivory amidst the silvery ether. In that space, I walked and seethed, the primal spirit imprisoned within my skeletal frame, yearning to transcend the corporeal veil, as the pure bones lamented from within the flesh.

The silken and furred trappings I donned grew increasingly burdensome, as the diaphanous black veil rendered my existence threadbare. My silken bands and miniver, oh, how they weighed me down, a heavy cloak of twilight! The black gauze, a beggar's veil, was thin as whispers; the ermine wrapped my mouth and chin, stifling my breath, stealing the moonlight's kiss. A Harlequin, caught in love's lozenge, hate's sharp edge, I wandered through ragged stripes of garish rigmarole. My footfalls danced on smoldering coals, a wary step upon a living blaze. Thoughts I loathed clung to me, their corrupt disguises a shroud; Morality, an unyielding weight, anchored in my ribs, leaving my ivory soul gasping in silver air. There I strode, a tempest within, the caged savage raged anew, yearning to feel the bones, pristine and raw, crying out beneath the flesh’s imprisoning veil.

For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love, Or chide my palsy, or my gout, My five gray hairs, or ruined fortune flout, With wealth your state, your mind with arts improve, Take you a course, get you a place, Observe his honor, or his grace, Or the king's real, or his stamped face Contemplate; what you will, approve, So you will let me love. Alas, alas, who's injured by my love? What merchant's ships have my sighs drowned? Who says my tears have overflowed his ground? When did my colds a forward spring remove? When did the heats which my veins fill Add one more to the plaguy bill? Soldiers find wars, and lawyers find out still Litigious men, which quarrels move, Though she and I do love. Call us what you will, we are made such by love; Call her one, me another fly, We're tapers too, and at our own cost die,

For heaven’s sake, still your tongue—let me love! Or rail against my trembling hands, my gouty bones, My five tired hairs, my fortunes turned to dust. Build your state with gold, sharpen your mind with art, Chase ambition down its endless halls, Bow to honor’s gleaming mask, or trace the king’s True face upon his coin. Gaze at what you will, Approve what stirs your restless heart, So long as you let me love.  

Oh, tell me, who crumbles beneath my love’s weight? What merchant’s fleet have my sighs sent crashing? Whose fields have drowned beneath the flood of my tears? What frosts of mine have stunted spring’s bright bloom? When has my blood’s fever added to the world’s plague? Soldiers still wage wars, and lawyers forever find Men itching for quarrels; yet she and I love on.  

Call us what you wish—love has made us thus. Call her one flame, me another; we are candles, Burning down by our own sweet cost, our hearts alight.

Please, just let me love. Don’t scold me for my trembling hands or aching joints. Don’t mock my few gray hairs or my poor luck with money. Go ahead and focus on your own life—build up your wealth, sharpen your mind, chase your ambitions. Pay attention to honors, titles, or even the king’s image on a coin. Do whatever pleases you, but let me have this love.

Who’s really hurt by it, anyway? Has any merchant’s ship sunk because of my longing? Has my sadness flooded someone’s fields? When have my chills ever delayed spring’s arrival? Or has the heat in my veins made sickness worse for anyone? Soldiers still go to war. Lawyers still find quarrels to argue over. Life goes on, even though she and I love each other.

Say what you want about us—we are what love has made us. Maybe we’re like moths, or candles burning down by our own light.

For the sake of propriety, restrain your speech and permit me to love. If criticism is required, address my trembling hand, my gout, my few gray hairs, or mock the decline of my fortune. Direct your energies to enhancing your wealth, cultivating your intellect through the arts, pursuing a career, securing a position, or admiring the dignity of a nobleman or the king, whether in his person or as represented on coinage. Occupy yourself with these endeavors, provided you allow me the freedom to love. 

What harm, I ask, is caused by my affection? Have my sighs sunk any merchant’s ships? Has anyone claimed that my tears have flooded their land? Did the coldness of my disposition delay an early spring? Has the heat within my veins added to the toll of any pestilence? Soldiers continue to find wars, and lawyers continue to discover contentious individuals who fuel disputes. None of this is altered by the love shared between her and me. 

Call us whatever you wish; we are shaped by love. Label her as one creature and me as another. We are also like candles, expending ourselves at our own expense to sustain our light.

For the sake of all that is sacred, restrain your tongue, and grant me the liberty to love—or if you must speak, direct your reproaches to my trembling limbs, my aching joints, the sparse remnants of my youth’s hair, or the wreckage of my fortune. Exalt your opulence, refine your intellect through cultivated arts, chart your ambitions, secure your station, attend to titles and dignities, or muse upon the sovereign’s visage, whether embossed in coin or borne in flesh. Pursue what pleases you, commend what you will, but leave me to this quiet passion. Who suffers harm from my love? What fleets have foundered beneath the weight of my sighs? Whose lands have my tears submerged? Did my winter breath ever thwart a burgeoning spring? Have the fires coursing through my veins compounded the burden of pestilence? Soldiers still march to war, lawyers still uncover disputes, and quarrels remain the province of litigious minds—yet she and I persist, undisturbed, in our love. Call us what names you wish; love has wrought us thus. Say she is one creature and I another, that we are insects drawn to fleeting flames; we are candles that willingly consume ourselves in pursuit of light.

For heaven’s sake, hush your lips and let my heart soar! Critique my trembling limbs, my weary joints, my five silvered strands, or mock my tattered dreams. You, with your coffers full, your mind a garden of fine arts—take a path, secure a title, bow to his honor or his grace, gaze upon the king’s visage, stamped and proud. Approve of what you will, but grant me this: let me love! Oh, woe betide, who has been harmed by my affection? What merchant's vessel has sunk beneath my sighs? Who claims my tears have drenched his land? When did my chill send spring fleeing? When did the fever coursing through my veins add one more weight to the list of woes? Soldiers seek battles, lawyers find men to quarrel, while she and I simply love. Call us what you wish; love shapes us true. Label her one, me another moth—we are candles too, burning bright, consuming ourselves in this fiery dance.

Please, just be quiet and let me love. You can scold my shaky hands or my sore feet, my gray hair or my lost fortune. You can build your wealth or improve your mind with art. You can follow your path, find a job, and admire those in power or the king’s face. Think about whatever you want, as long as you let me love. Oh, who is hurt by my love? What ships have sunk because of my sighs? Who says my tears have soaked their land? When have my colds chased away the spring? When have the feelings in my heart added to anyone’s troubles? Soldiers find their wars, and lawyers find their arguments, but my love for her stays strong. Call us what you like; love makes us who we are. Call her one thing and me another, but we are like candles, burning brightly until we fade away.

Please refrain from speaking and allow me to love. Alternatively, criticize my physical ailments, such as my tremors or arthritis, my five gray hairs, or my diminished fortune. You may enhance your social status and intellectual pursuits, pursue a career, secure a position, or pay attention to his honor or grace, or the king's image, whether real or stamped. You may engage in any activity you prefer, provided that you permit me to love. I question who is harmed by my affection. What merchant vessels have been sunk by my sighs? Who claims that my tears have flooded their land? When have my ailments disrupted the onset of spring? When have the passions that fill my veins added to the burdens of illness? Soldiers engage in warfare, and lawyers continue to pursue contentious individuals who instigate disputes, even though she and I are in love. Label us as you wish; we are defined by our love. Whether you refer to her as one entity and me as another, we are also candles, burning at our own expense.

Please, just be quiet and let me love. You can scold my shaky hands or my sore feet, my gray hair or my lost fortune. You can build your wealth or improve your mind with art. You can follow your path, find a job, and admire those in power or the king’s face. Think about whatever you want, as long as you let me love. Oh, who is hurt by my love? What ships have sunk because of my sighs? Who says my tears have soaked their land? When have my colds chased away the spring? When have the feelings in my heart added to anyone’s troubles? Soldiers find their wars, and lawyers find their arguments, but my love for her stays strong. Call us what you like; love makes us who we are. Call her one thing and me another, but we are like candles, burning brightly until we fade away.

For the divine's sake, restrain your utterances and permit me the freedom to love, or to berate my tremors, or my afflictions, my five strands of gray, or the derision of my shattered fortunes. Elevate your station with riches, embellish your intellect with the arts, chart your course, secure your position, and pay homage to his dignity or his favor; reflect upon the sovereign's essence or his minted visage—whatever you choose to endorse, so long as you allow me my love. Oh, what injury arises from my affection? Which merchant vessel has succumbed to the tempest of my sighs? Who claims my weeping has inundated his domain? At what moment have my chills displaced a burgeoning spring? When has the fervor coursing through my veins added yet another wretched tally? Soldiers may seek out conflicts, and lawyers unearth the quarrels of the litigious, though she and I are entwined in love. Label us as you will; we are shaped by the very essence of love—designate her one entity and me another, yet we are but candles, extinguished at our own expense.

For heaven’s sake, hush your lips and let my heart soar! Critique my trembling limbs, my weary joints, my five silvered strands, or mock my tattered dreams. You, with your coffers full, your mind a garden of fine arts—take a path, secure a title, bow to his honor or his grace, gaze upon the king’s visage, stamped and proud. Approve of what you will, but grant me this: let me love! Oh, woe betide, who has been harmed by my affection? What merchant's vessel has sunk beneath my sighs? Who claims my tears have drenched his land? When did my chill send spring fleeing? When did the fever coursing through my veins add one more weight to the list of woes? Soldiers seek battles, lawyers find men to quarrel, while she and I simply love. Call us what you wish; love shapes us true. Label her one, me another moth—we are candles too, burning bright, consuming ourselves in this fiery dance.

Shall I compare thee to a summers day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, And summers lease hath all too short a date; Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines, And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; And every fair from fair sometime declines, By chance or natures changing course untrimm'd; But thy eternal summer shall not fade, Nor lose possession of that fair thou owst; Nor shall death brag thou wanderst in his shade, When in eternal lines to time thou growst: So long as men can breathe or eyes can see, So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

Should I liken thee to a day of summer’s grace? Thou surpassest its beauty and steadiness: Tempestuous gusts batter May’s tender blossoms, And summer’s tenure proves pitifully brief; At times, the celestial eye burns with excessive fervor, And oft its gilded radiance wanes to obscurity; All that is fair succumbs, in time, to decline— By fortune’s whim or nature’s relentless flux undone; Yet thy perennial summer shall evade such decay, Nor forfeit the splendor that is thy rightful due; Nor shall mortality boast dominion o’er thy steps, As thou art enshrined within lines that defy the ravages of time: So long as breath endures and sight prevails, So long lives this verse, and it bequeaths life unto thee.

Should I compare you to a summer’s day? You are lovelier and more gentle. Summer’s winds can be rough, shaking the tender buds of May, and summer itself is far too short. Sometimes the sun blazes too hot, and other times its golden light is dimmed. All beauty fades eventually, whether by chance or the natural passage of time. But your beauty won’t fade; it will stay with you always. Death won’t boast of you being lost in its shadow because your essence will live on in these lasting words. As long as people can breathe and see, this poem will endure, keeping your memory alive forever.

Could I liken you to a summer’s day?  
You outshine it, softer, steadier in your grace.  
May’s tender blossoms tremble in rough winds,  
And summer, brief tenant, soon packs its bags.  
The sun, that blazing tyrant, sometimes glares too hot,  
And other days his golden face is veiled in shadow.  
All beauty, sooner or later, slips away,  
Caught in the nets of chance or time’s relentless hand.  
But your summer, your brightness, will not wither;  
The treasure of your fairness shall not fade.  
Death won’t dare to boast you roam his darkened halls,  
For in these undying lines, you’ll walk with time.  
As long as breath is drawn and eyes can see,  
These words will live—and give you life in me.

Shall I liken you to a summer's day? You are more exquisite and more temperate: Tempestuous winds rattle the cherished buds of May, and the summer's lease is fleetingly ephemeral; At times, the sun's gaze is excessively fervent, and often is its golden visage obscured; Moreover, all beauty inevitably succumbs to decline, whether through chance or the inexorable vicissitudes of nature. Yet, your eternal summer shall not wither, nor relinquish the beauty you possess; Nor shall death arrogate the claim that you dwell in his shadow, for in the infinite lines of time, you flourish: As long as humanity breathes or eyes perceive, this verse shall endure, and through it, you shall live on.

Stella is sick, and in that sick-bed lies Sweetness, that breathes and pants as oft as she; And grace, sick too, such fine conclusions tries That sickness brags itself best graced to be. Beauty is sick, but sick in so fair guise That in that paleness beautys white we see; And joy, which is inseparate from these eyes, Stella now learns (strange case!) to weep in thee. Love moves thy pain, and like a faithful page, As thy looks stir, runs up and down, to make All folks prest at thy will thy pain to assuage; Nature with care sweats for her darlings sake, Knowing worlds pass, ere she enough can find Of such heaven stuff, to clothe so heavenly mind.

Stella is ill, and upon her sick-bed rests a sweetness that exhales and labors as frequently as she does. Grace, afflicted as well, reaches such refined conclusions that even sickness boasts of being adorned with grace. Beauty, though unwell, presents itself in so elegant a form that its pallor reveals beauty’s pure essence. Joy, typically inseparable from Stella’s gaze, now manifests differently, as Stella learns—an unusual occurrence—to shed tears through you. Love is stirred by your suffering and acts like a devoted attendant, responding to your expressions and striving to mobilize everyone to alleviate your distress. Nature labors with diligence for her cherished creation, recognizing that countless ages would pass before she could gather sufficient material to adorn a mind of such celestial quality.

Stella lies in her sickness, a fevered throne where Sweetness gasps and trembles, echoing her every breath. Grace, frail and fever-struck, spins such delicate threads of thought that even illness boasts, crowned by her touch. Beauty, too, wears sickness like a veil, pallid yet radiant, a white so pure it blinds the eye. And Joy—Joy, that loyal companion to her gaze—has turned traitor; Stella learns the bitter art of weeping, her tears strange visitors in those eyes. Love, restless servant, dances in her pain, darting like a shadow to summon the world’s hands to soothe her suffering. Nature herself bends, sweating over her masterpiece, frantic to preserve her treasure, knowing millennia would fail to forge such celestial cloth for a soul so impossibly divine.

Stella is unwell, and lying there in her sickbed, even her sweetness seems to breathe and struggle along with her. Her grace, also afflicted, works so delicately that sickness almost feels proud to be touched by it. Her beauty, though pale and worn, still shines in such a way that even this whiteness holds its own charm. Joy, which is usually tied to her bright eyes, now takes a strange turn as Stella learns to cry. Love feels her pain and rushes about like a loyal servant, responding to every glance and stirring others to ease her suffering. Nature works tirelessly for her, sweating with care for this precious soul, knowing it would take ages to gather enough of heaven’s finest essence to match the spirit of one so heavenly.

Beneath the veil of Stella’s ailment lies a paradoxical tableau wherein sweetness, bound to her breath and frailty, mirrors her every gasp. Grace itself, succumbing to this shared affliction, fashions such exquisite paradoxes that sickness, adorned by its touch, proclaims itself ennobled. Beauty, too, falters, yet its decline assumes so luminous a guise that even pallor transforms into a revelation of beauty’s essence. Joy, an erstwhile constant in her gaze, now transmutes into sorrow’s emissary, for Stella—curious inversion—discovers the art of tears within herself. Love, an ever-dutiful attendant, animates her suffering, flitting ceaselessly in response to her glance, orchestrating the world to attend her solace. Nature, as if in anxious devotion to her chosen masterpiece, toils relentlessly, aware that eons might pass before such celestial substance could again vest so sublime a soul.

Grace, too, wears the cloak of illness, spinning delicate tales of beauty in the throes of despair, proclaiming that sickness wears its crown with pride. Stella is sick, lying in her bed. Sweetness is there with her, breathing heavily like she does. Grace is also unwell, trying to find a way to feel better, as if being sick makes it more special. Beauty is sick too, but looks lovely, showing her pale skin. Joy, which is usually always present in her eyes, now strangely learns to cry with her. Love feels your pain and, like a loyal friend, runs around to help ease it whenever you show your hurt. Nature works hard for her beloved one, knowing it takes time to find the right things to nurture such a beautiful spirit.

In the throes of ailment lies Stella, a vessel of Sweetness, whose breath mirrors the labored cadence of her own; and grace, ensnared in similar malaise, attempts to articulate its own paradoxical conclusions, wherein the very essence of affliction exalts itself as the most adorned state of existence. Beauty, though enfeebled, adorns itself in a guise so resplendent that within its pallor we discern the quintessence of whiteness; and joy, which is inextricably linked to these observant eyes, now reveals to Stella—an enigmatic predicament—the art of weeping through her. Love orchestrates your suffering, a devoted squire that, stirred by your countenance, scurries forth to compel all to heed your will and alleviate your distress; Nature, with fervent exertion, toils for her cherished, cognizant that epochs may elapse before she can procure the ethereal substance requisite to garment such a celestial intellect.

Thou art my lute, by thee I sing, My being is attuned to thee. Thou settest all my words a-wing, And meltest me to melody. Thou art my life, by thee I live, From thee proceed the joys I know; Sweetheart, thy hand has power to give The meed of lovethe cup of woe. Thou art my love, by thee I lead My soul the paths of light along, From vale to vale, from mead to mead, And home it in the hills of song. My song, my soul, my life, my all, Why need I pray or make my plea, Since my petition cannot fall; For Im already one with thee!

You are my lute, through you I sing, My existence resonates with you. You inspire my words to take flight, Transforming me into harmony. You are my life, through you I endure, From you arise the joys I experience. Beloved, your touch holds the ability To bestow love’s reward or sorrow’s cup. You are my love, through you I ascend, Guiding my spirit along paths of illumination, Traversing valleys and meadows, And finding its rest within the hills of song. My song, my soul, my life, my all— Why should I pray or make requests, When my supplication cannot fail? For I am already united with you.

You are my song, through you I sing, My heart is tuned to match your voice. You lift my words and let them fly, And turn my soul to sweetest sound. You are my life, through you I live, The joys I feel all come from you. Your touch can bring both love’s reward And sorrow’s cup for me to bear. You are my love, through you I find The way to walk in paths of light, From valley low to meadow green, And rest in hills where songs belong. My song, my soul, my life, my all, Why should I ask or make my case? My prayer is answered, for I’m yours, Already one with you always.

You are my love, through you I find The way to walk in paths of light, From valley low to meadow green, And rest in hills where songs belong. My song, my soul, my life, my all, Why should I ask or make my case? My prayer is answered, for I’m yours, Already one with you always.

You are my lute, through you I sing, My existence resonates with you. You inspire my words to take flight, Transforming me into harmony. You are my life, through you I endure, From you arise the joys I experience. Beloved, your touch holds the ability To bestow love’s reward or sorrow’s cup. You are my love, through you I ascend, Guiding my spirit along paths of illumination, Traversing valleys and meadows, And finding its rest within the hills of song. My song, my soul, my life, my all— Why should I pray or make requests, When my supplication cannot fail? For I am already united with you.

You are the lute through which my essence resonates, the conduit by which my spirit finds its voice. My existence vibrates in harmony with your being, and through you, my words take flight, transmuted into the currency of melody. You are the vital principle animating my life; all delights I apprehend issue from your touch. Beloved, your hand wields the alchemy to dispense both love’s rapture and sorrow’s chalice. You are the lodestar of my devotion, guiding my soul along luminous trajectories—from shadowed vales to verdant pastures, where it ascends to dwell in the peaks of song. My melody, my essence, my breath, my totality—what need have I for supplication or entreaty? My desires are needless petitions, for I am already indivisible from you.

You are my lute, and through your strings I sing; my spirit hums in harmony with yours. You lift my words like birds on trembling wings, and melt my heart to music's coursing shores. You are my life, the breath through which I grow, the wellspring of my joy, my pain, my fire; your touch contains the gift of love's warm glow, or bitter draughts that burn but never tire. You are my love, my guide along the way, my lantern through the meadows and the mist; from valley's hush to hilltop's golden play, you lead me home to songs that can't resist. My soul, my song, my all—what need have I to beg or plead? I'm woven through your sky.

Thou art my lute, by thee I sing, My being is attuned to thee. Thou settest all my words a-wing, And meltest me to melody. Thou art my life, by thee I live, From thee proceed the joys I know; Sweetheart, thy hand has power to give The meed of lovethe cup of woe. Thou art my love, by thee I lead My soul the paths of light along, From vale to vale, from mead to mead, And home it in the hills of song. My song, my soul, my life, my all, Why need I pray or make my plea, Since my petition cannot fall; For Im already one with thee!

You are my instrument; through you, I express myself. My existence is in harmony with you. You inspire my words, transforming them into music. You are my life; through you, I experience joy. Beloved, your touch has the ability to offer both love and suffering. You are my affection; through you, I guide my spirit along pathways of enlightenment, traversing from one valley to another and finding home in the uplands of song. My song, my spirit, my life, my entirety—what need is there for prayer or supplication? My request is unnecessary, for I am already united with you.

Perspective never withers from their eyes; They keep that docile edict of the Spring That blends March with August Antarctic skies: These are but cows that see no other thing Than grass and snow, and their own inner being Through the rich halo that they do not trouble Even to cast upon the seasons fleeting Though they should thin and die on last years stubble. And they are awkward, ponderous and uncoy . . . While we who press the cider mill, regarding them We, who with pledges taste the bright annoy Of friendships acid wine, retarding phlegm, Shifting reprisals (til who shall tell us when The jest is too sharp to be kindly?) boast Much of our store of faith in other men Who would, ourselves, stalk down the merriest ghost. Above them old Mizzentop, palatial white Hostelryfloor by floor to cinquefoil dormer Portholes the ceilings stack their stoic height. Long tiers of windows staring out toward former

Vision never falters in their placid gaze; they hold to Spring’s gentle decree, that seamless accord where March and August merge beneath Antarctic skies. They are but cows, blind to all but the grass, the snow, and the quiet expanse of their own souls, haloed in a golden calm they never bother to scatter over fleeting seasons—though seasons may starve them, thinning their forms upon last year’s brittle stubble. Awkward and lumbering, graceless as stones rolled by indifferent tides... And we, who grind cider from autumn’s bitter fruit, watching them—yes, we, who sip the tart sting of friendship’s biting brew, who stall and fumble through the sluggish mire of grudges traded, wondering (always wondering) when jest cuts too deep to heal—how proudly we parade our faith in others, others who, like us, would haunt the happiest ghost down to its marrow. Above them, Mizzentop looms, a stately white fortress, floor upon floor piling to cinquefoil dormers, its porthole windows a stoic stare, gazing outward toward what was.

The lens through which they apprehend the world remains unclouded, unbetrayed by time's attrition; they embody the tranquil decree of Spring, that seamless alchemy which weds March's thaw to August's polar constellations. These beings—mere cattle—discern no reality beyond the dual expanse of pasture and frost, nor beyond the quiet, unfathomed contours of their own essence, mediated through a luminous aura they neither question nor impose upon the transient rhythm of seasons, even as they languish, skeletal, upon the brittle remnants of last year's growth. They are ungainly, deliberate, devoid of guile or pretension.  

Yet we, engaged in the alchemy of cider's labor, gaze upon them from our station; we, whose friendships ferment into a tart elixir of pledges and reproach, whose repartees and retaliations spiral ceaselessly (until, perhaps, the blade of wit severs its kindness altogether), pride ourselves on a fragile faith in our fellow beings—though we, no less than they, would pursue with equal fervor the laughing specter of our undoing. And above it all looms Mizzentop, its pallid grandeur tiered in stoic symmetry, each cinquefoil dormer a sentinel, its strata of windows gazing impassively toward the vanishing echoes of what once was.

Their vision remains unclouded, consistently retaining the calm assurance of spring’s decree, which harmonizes the transitional days of March with the Antarctic skies of August. These creatures—merely cows—perceive nothing beyond the grass beneath them, the snow surrounding them, and the essence of their own existence. They view all of this through an untroubled radiance, unconcerned with casting that light upon the fleeting seasons, even as last year’s stubble grows sparse and their vitality diminishes. They are cumbersome and inelegant in their movements.  

Meanwhile, we, engaged in the labor of the cider mill, observe them. We, who partake in the bittersweet complexities of friendship—symbolized by the sharp tang of wine—are encumbered by shifting retaliations and unresolved tensions. Until the jest becomes too cutting to remain kind, we take pride in our confidence in others, even as we, ourselves, might pursue the remnants of joy like hunters chasing the faintest specter.  

Above them, the grand structure of Mizzentop rises—a white hostelry, floor upon floor, culminating in cinquefoil dormer windows. Its tiered rows of openings gaze outward, impassively, toward the memories of what once was.

Their view never fades; they hold to the simple rule of Spring that links March to August under icy skies. These are just cows, focused only on the grass beneath them, the snow around them, and the quiet world within. They don’t worry about the glow of life they carry, even as the seasons pass and last year’s dried stalks leave little to sustain them. They are clumsy and heavy, without charm or pretense.

Meanwhile, we, busy at the cider press, watch them from afar. We, who sip the sharpness of friendship’s bittersweet wine, pulling back against the irritations it brings. We trade jabs and uneasy jokes, never knowing when the humor becomes too cruel to be kind. We pride ourselves on trusting others, even as we chase down the ghosts of happiness with reckless intent.

Above it all rises old Mizzentop, the grand white inn. Its floors stack high, ending in dormer windows shaped like flowers. Row upon row of windows stare out, unchanging, toward what used to be.

Above it all rises old Mizzentop, the grand white inn. Its floors stack high, ending in dormer windows shaped like flowers. Row upon row of windows stare out, unchanging, toward what used to be.

The lens through which they apprehend the world remains unclouded, unbetrayed by time's attrition; they embody the tranquil decree of Spring, that seamless alchemy which weds March's thaw to August's polar constellations. These beings—mere cattle—discern no reality beyond the dual expanse of pasture and frost, nor beyond the quiet, unfathomed contours of their own essence, mediated through a luminous aura they neither question nor impose upon the transient rhythm of seasons, even as they languish, skeletal, upon the brittle remnants of last year's growth. They are ungainly, deliberate, devoid of guile or pretension.  

Yet we, engaged in the alchemy of cider's labor, gaze upon them from our station; we, whose friendships ferment into a tart elixir of pledges and reproach, whose repartees and retaliations spiral ceaselessly (until, perhaps, the blade of wit severs its kindness altogether), pride ourselves on a fragile faith in our fellow beings—though we, no less than they, would pursue with equal fervor the laughing specter of our undoing. And above it all looms Mizzentop, its pallid grandeur tiered in stoic symmetry, each cinquefoil dormer a sentinel, its strata of windows gazing impassively toward the vanishing echoes of what once was.

Vision never falters in their placid gaze; they hold to Spring’s gentle decree, that seamless accord where March and August merge beneath Antarctic skies. They are but cows, blind to all but the grass, the snow, and the quiet expanse of their own souls, haloed in a golden calm they never bother to scatter over fleeting seasons—though seasons may starve them, thinning their forms upon last year’s brittle stubble. Awkward and lumbering, graceless as stones rolled by indifferent tides... And we, who grind cider from autumn’s bitter fruit, watching them—yes, we, who sip the tart sting of friendship’s biting brew, who stall and fumble through the sluggish mire of grudges traded, wondering (always wondering) when jest cuts too deep to heal—how proudly we parade our faith in others, others who, like us, would haunt the happiest ghost down to its marrow. Above them, Mizzentop looms, a stately white fortress, floor upon floor piling to cinquefoil dormers, its porthole windows a stoic stare, gazing outward toward what was.

Their eyes, eternal sentinels, never fade; They cradle the tender decree of Spring, That weaves March with the icy embrace of August skies. Mere cattle, they gaze, oblivious to the world, Rooted in grass and snow, lost in the depths of their own souls, Wrapped in a golden glow they neither seek nor shed, Even as the seasons swirl and fade, while they linger, Thinning, fading upon last year’s brittle remains. Oh, how awkward, how heavy they roam, While we, the cider pressers, sip the sharp tang Of friendship’s bittersweet wine, staving off the dullness, Twisting in a dance of reprisal (who knows when The jest cuts too deep for kindness?) We boast of our faith in the goodness of men, Yet we ourselves would chase the most whimsical ghost. Above them looms Mizzentop, a grand white sentinel, Floors stacked like stories, five-petaled dormers peering Through long rows of windows, unblinking, gazing Out toward a past that whispers on the wind.

The lens through which they perceive reality remains untouched by the erosion of time; they adhere to a serene mandate of renewal that unites the transitional months of March with the frigid expanse of Antarctic August. These creatures, mere cattle, are ensnared in a singular existence, fixated solely on the dichotomy of verdant grass and the starkness of snow, their consciousness encased within a luminous sphere they neither question nor extend to encompass the ephemeral seasons, even as they wither upon the remnants of last year's harvest. Clumsy and unrefined, they are oblivious to the complexities surrounding them. In contrast, we, the architects of the cider mill, scrutinize their existence, partaking in the sharp, invigorating essence of camaraderie distilled into a tart elixir, stifling our own inertia. We engage in a delicate dance of retribution, pondering the limits of jest, while professing our belief in humanity, even as we ourselves pursue the most elusive specters of mirth. Towering above them, the venerable Mizzentop stands as a grand edifice, each floor meticulously ascending to meet the sky, its windows gazing out toward a bygone era.

Perspective remains unaltered in their view. They adhere to a passive acceptance of the season's cycle that merges March with the summer of August under Antarctic skies. These cattle perceive only grass and snow, alongside their own internal existence, through a rich aura they do not even bother to project onto the transitory seasons, despite the possibility that they may diminish and perish on last year's remnants. They appear clumsy, heavy, and unassuming. In contrast, we, who operate the cider press, observe them. We, who experience the sharpness of friendships akin to sour wine, engage in defensive actions, uncertain of when humor becomes too biting to be considered friendly. We boast of our considerable faith in others, even as we ourselves might pursue the most carefree specter. Above them stands the grand Mizzentop, a hotel with multiple floors, characterized by cinquefoil dormer windows, while the ceilings exhibit a steadfast height. Rows of windows gaze outward toward the past.

Their eyes never lose sight of what they see. They hold on to the gentle rules of Spring that mix the cool days of March with the warmth of August. They are just cows, focused only on grass and snow, and their own thoughts. They don’t even try to notice the changing seasons, even as they fade away on last year’s dry grass. They move slowly, heavy, and shy. Meanwhile, we work at the cider mill, watching them. We taste the sharpness of friendships, filled with laughter and a little annoyance. We joke and wonder when a joke might hurt too much. We talk a lot about trusting others, even as we chase after fleeting moments of joy. Above them stands the grand old Mizzentop, its white walls towering high. Each floor has dormer windows, and the tall ceilings give a sense of strength. Rows of windows look out at the past, holding stories of what once was.

I never knew the earth had so much gold The fields run over with it, and this hill Hoary and old, Is young with buoyant blooms that flame and thrill. Such golden fires, such yellowlo, how good This spendthrift world, and what a lavish God! This fringe of wood, Blazing with buttercup and goldenrod. You too, beloved, are changed. Again I see Your face grow mystical, as on that night You turned to me, And all the trembling worldand youwere white. Aye, you are touched; your singing lips grow dumb; The fields absorb you, color you entire . . . And you become A goddess standing in a world of fire!

I never guessed the earth could hold such gold—  
Fields spilling over, a molten flood, this hill,  
Ancient, cold,  
Now crowned with blooms that burn and pulse and thrill.  
Such wild infernos, yellow’s endless tide,  
This world, a spendthrift’s dream, God’s open hand!  
The woods abide,  
Their edges blazing—goldenrod, buttercup stand.  

And you, my love, have shifted, changed anew—  
Your face, aglow, returns to that strange light,  
As once it drew  
The trembling world—and you—into pure white.  
Yes, you are touched; your singing lips fall still,  
The fields consume you, drench you in their fire…  
Until you fill  
The air, a goddess wreathed in flame’s desire!

I was unaware that the earth contained such abundant gold. The fields are suffused with it, and this ancient hill appears rejuvenated, adorned with vibrant blossoms that radiate and stir. These golden hues, this richness of yellow—how generous is this world, and how bountiful its Creator. The bordering wood is alight with buttercups and goldenrod, creating a vivid spectacle. You too, dear one, are transformed. Once more, I witness your face take on a mystical quality, as it did that night when you turned to me, and everything—yourself and the trembling world—was bathed in white. Indeed, you are altered; your voice falls silent, and the fields envelop and imbue you completely. You are transfigured, appearing as a goddess amidst a realm consumed by fire.

I never realized the earth held so much gold. The fields are brimming with it, and this old hill, weathered and gray, feels young again with bright, dancing flowers. Such golden light, such shining yellow—it’s amazing how generous the world is, and how giving God must be! The edge of the woods glows with buttercups and goldenrod, blazing like a treasure.

You’ve changed too, my love. I see it again now—your face softens and glows, just like that night when you turned to me, and everything—everything—felt pure and bright. Yes, something has touched you; your voice quiets, and it’s like the fields pull you in, wrapping you in their colors. You’re transformed, standing there like a goddess in a world lit with fire.

Indeed, you are altered; your voice falls silent, and the fields envelop and imbue you completely. You are transfigured, appearing as a goddess amidst a realm consumed by fire.

I never realized the earth held so much gold. The fields are brimming with it, and this old hill, weathered and gray, feels young again with bright, dancing flowers. Such golden light, such shining yellow—it’s amazing how generous the world is, and how giving God must be! The edge of the woods glows with buttercups and goldenrod, blazing like a treasure.

You’ve changed too, my love. I see it again now—your face softens and glows, just like that night when you turned to me, and everything—everything—felt pure and bright. Yes, something has touched you; your voice quiets, and it’s like the fields pull you in, wrapping you in their colors. You’re transformed, standing there like a goddess in a world lit with fire.

I had not grasped the earth’s opulent reserves,  
its plains suffused with aureate excess, this rise,  
ancient in its contours,  
now rejuvenated, pulsing with blossoms that exult and blaze.  
Such prodigality of luminance, this golden effusion—  
how prodigious a cosmos, how munificent its architect!  
The bordering grove,  
a conflagration of buttercup and goldenrod, testifies.  

And you, too, have undergone transfiguration.  
Once more I discern that ineffable shift:  
your visage assumes the numinous cast it bore  
when, on that fateful eve,  
you turned toward me,  
and the shuddering cosmos, and you,  
were rendered pale as spectral light.  

Indeed, an unseen force has claimed you;  
your voice falters, silenced by reverence.  
The fields subsume your essence, suffuse your form with their hue,  
until you are transfigured—  
a deity poised amidst a confluent inferno of gold.

I never guessed the earth could hold such gold—  
Fields spilling over, a molten flood, this hill,  
Ancient, cold,  
Now crowned with blooms that burn and pulse and thrill.  
Such wild infernos, yellow’s endless tide,  
This world, a spendthrift’s dream, God’s open hand!  
The woods abide,  
Their edges blazing—goldenrod, buttercup stand.  

And you, my love, have shifted, changed anew—  
Your face, aglow, returns to that strange light,  
As once it drew  
The trembling world—and you—into pure white.  
Yes, you are touched; your singing lips fall still,  
The fields consume you, drench you in their fire…  
Until you fill  
The air, a goddess wreathed in flame’s desire!

I never knew the earth had so much gold The fields run over with it, and this hill Hoary and old, Is young with buoyant blooms that flame and thrill. Such golden fires, such yellowlo, how good This spendthrift world, and what a lavish God! This fringe of wood, Blazing with buttercup and goldenrod. You too, beloved, are changed. Again I see Your face grow mystical, as on that night You turned to me, And all the trembling worldand youwere white. Aye, you are touched; your singing lips grow dumb; The fields absorb you, color you entire . . . And you become A goddess standing in a world of fire!

I never imagined the earth cradled so much gold! Fields overflow, a shimmering cascade, while this ancient hill, weathered yet wise, bursts forth with blooms that dance and ignite. Oh, those golden flames, that glorious yellow glow—what an extravagant world we inhabit, and what a generous Creator! This woodland edge, ablaze with buttercups and goldenrod, sings its vibrant song. You too, my beloved, have transformed. I behold your face, now a tapestry of mystique, like that fateful night you turned to me, as the entire world shimmered in a cloak of white. Yes, you are touched; your once-singing lips now hush. The fields embrace you, enveloping you in their riot of colors... You become a goddess, radiant amid this world of fire!

I was previously unaware that the earth contained such abundant gold. The fields are filled with it, and this hill, ancient and weathered, appears rejuvenated with vibrant blooms that captivate. Such brilliant colors, such richness; this generous world reflects a benevolent deity. This border of trees is illuminated with buttercups and goldenrods. You, too, have transformed. I again observe your face becoming ethereal, reminiscent of that evening when you turned towards me, and the entire world appeared radiant and pale. Indeed, you are altered; your once expressive lips are now silent. The fields envelop you, saturating you completely, and you emerge as a goddess within a realm of brilliance.

I had not fathomed that the terrestrial sphere harbored such an abundance of golden essence. The undulating plains overflow with this lustrous wealth, while the venerable hill, though ancient in its stature, flourishes with exuberant blooms that ignite and enrapture. These resplendent conflagrations, this chromatic exuberance—what an extravagant cosmos we inhabit, and what a prodigal Creator presides over it! This verdant fringe, ablaze with buttercup and goldenrod, mirrors your own metamorphosis. Once more, I behold your visage, suffused with an ethereal glow, reminiscent of that fateful evening when you turned towards me, and the tremulous world, alongside you, was enveloped in a luminous pallor. Indeed, you are transformed; your once-vibrant voice now muted, as the fields envelop and transmute your essence entirely. Thus, you ascend, a goddess amid a realm aflame!

Care for thy soul as thing of greatest price, Made to the end to taste of power divine, Devoid of guilt, abhorring sin and vice, Apt by Gods grace to virtue to incline. Care for it so as by thy retchless train It be not brought to taste eternal pain. Care for thy corse, but chiefly for souls sake; Cut off excess, sustaining food is best; To vanquish pride but comely clothing take; Seek after skill, deep ignorance detest. Care so, I say, the flesh to feed and clothe That thou harm not thy soul and body both. Care for the world to do thy body right; Rack not thy wit to win thy wicked ways; Seek not to oppress the weak by wrongful might; To pay thy due do banish all delays. Care to dispend according to thy store, And in like sort be mindful of the poor. Care for thy soul, as for thy chiefest stay; Care for thy body for thy souls avail; Care for the world for bodys help alway; Care yet but so as virtue may prevail. Care in such sort that thou be sure of this: Care keep thee not from heaven and heavenly bliss.

Attend to thy soul as the jewel of utmost worth, fashioned for communion with the transcendent and attuned to the harmonies of divine potency. Let it remain untainted, abhorring the corrosive pull of vice, and by grace be drawn toward the luminous arc of virtue. Guard it with vigilance, lest through careless indulgence it be cast into the abyss of perpetual anguish.

Tend to thy corporeal frame, yet subordinate its care to the soul’s higher claim. Eschew excess; moderation sustains both flesh and spirit. Adorn thyself with humility, for clothing should serve propriety, not vanity. Seek knowledge, and let ignorance be thy foe, cultivating the mind without impoverishing the essence of thy being.

Engage with the world not as an end but as a means. Pursue no path of cunning that veers toward oppression or cruelty; wield thy strength not to subdue but to uphold justice, and discharge thy obligations without delay. Distribute thy wealth with discernment, remembering the plight of the impoverished as thou measurest thy own sufficiency.

In all thy cares, let virtue dictate thy measure. Care for the soul, the body, and the world as interconnected spheres, yet never permit thy attentions to these lesser domains to eclipse the celestial inheritance awaiting thee. Let thy care be the tether that secures thee to heaven and its eternal beatitude.

Guard thy soul, treasure beyond all worth,  
Forged to revel in the spark divine.  
Let no shadow stain its holy birth,  
Turn it from sin, let virtue’s stars align.  
Guard it so no reckless step or snare  
Should hurl it down to the abyss of despair.  

Tend thy flesh, yet for thy soul’s fair throne;  
Shun excess—plain bread will serve thee best.  
Dress to humble pride, not boast alone,  
Seek wisdom’s light, let ignorance be detest.  
Feed and clothe the body, but take great care—  
To harm thy soul would lay both to despair.  

Turn to the world, but only as is just;  
Twist not thy wit to carve out crooked gains.  
Spare the weak from the hammer of thy lust,  
And give what’s due, let not greed forge thy chains.  
Spend with thy means, yet still thy heart implore:  
Forget not the poor who linger at thy door.  

Guard thy soul—it holds thy life’s true stay.  
Guard thy flesh, but for thy soul’s delight.  
Turn to the world, but tread the narrow way,  
Let virtue guide and keep thy path aright.  
Guard all with care, and let this truth be plain:  
No care should bar thee from the heavenly plain.

Take care of your soul; it’s your greatest treasure,  
Meant to know the joy of God’s own power.  
Keep it clean, far from sin’s dark measure,  
With grace to guide it toward virtue’s hour.  
Be mindful always, lest careless ways  
Lead it to suffer in endless days.  

Take care of your body, but for your soul’s good;  
Avoid indulgence—simple food is best.  
Dress with modesty, as the humble should,  
And seek wisdom; let ignorance find no rest.  
Feed and clothe your flesh in a way that’s kind,  
But never harm both body and mind.  

Take care in the world to meet your needs,  
But don’t twist your thoughts to chase what’s wrong.  
Don’t crush the weak with selfish deeds;  
Pay what you owe—don’t delay too long.  
Spend what you have with a giving heart,  
And remember the poor, to do your part.  

Care for your soul—it’s your truest guide.  
Care for your body, but let your soul lead.  
Use the world’s help, but let virtue decide.  
In all your caring, take careful heed:  
Let nothing keep you from heaven’s door,  
Where joy and peace last evermore.

Attend to your soul, for it holds the utmost value, designed to partake in divine power. Keep it free from guilt, avoiding sin and vice, and, through the grace of God, let it incline toward virtue. Guard it diligently, ensuring that through neglect it is not subjected to eternal suffering.

Care for your body, but primarily for the welfare of your soul. Avoid excess; choose sustenance that is sufficient. Dress modestly to overcome pride, and pursue knowledge while rejecting ignorance. Provide for the needs of the flesh in such a manner that neither soul nor body suffers harm.

Engage with the world to fulfill the requirements of your physical existence, but do not strain your intellect in pursuit of immoral aims. Refrain from using unjust power to oppress the weak. Address your debts promptly and allocate your resources wisely, ensuring consideration for the poor.

Prioritize the care of your soul as your principal foundation. Care for your body only insofar as it serves the soul's purpose. Interact with the world to support the body, but only in ways that uphold virtue. Ensure that your efforts in these matters do not hinder your path to heaven and eternal bliss.

Attend to your soul, for it holds the utmost value, designed to partake in divine power. Keep it free from guilt, avoiding sin and vice, and, through the grace of God, let it incline toward virtue. Guard it diligently, ensuring that through neglect it is not subjected to eternal suffering.

Care for your body, but primarily for the welfare of your soul. Avoid excess; choose sustenance that is sufficient. Dress modestly to overcome pride, and pursue knowledge while rejecting ignorance. Provide for the needs of the flesh in such a manner that neither soul nor body suffers harm.

Engage with the world to fulfill the requirements of your physical existence, but do not strain your intellect in pursuit of immoral aims. Refrain from using unjust power to oppress the weak. Address your debts promptly and allocate your resources wisely, ensuring consideration for the poor.

Prioritize the care of your soul as your principal foundation. Care for your body only insofar as it serves the soul's purpose. Interact with the world to support the body, but only in ways that uphold virtue. Ensure that your efforts in these matters do not hinder your path to heaven and eternal bliss.

Take care of your soul; it’s your greatest treasure,  
Meant to know the joy of God’s own power.  
Keep it clean, far from sin’s dark measure,  
With grace to guide it toward virtue’s hour.  
Be mindful always, lest careless ways  
Lead it to suffer in endless days.  

Take care of your body, but for your soul’s good;  
Avoid indulgence—simple food is best.  
Dress with modesty, as the humble should,  
And seek wisdom; let ignorance find no rest.  
Feed and clothe your flesh in a way that’s kind,  
But never harm both body and mind.  

Take care in the world to meet your needs,  
But don’t twist your thoughts to chase what’s wrong.  
Don’t crush the weak with selfish deeds;  
Pay what you owe—don’t delay too long.  
Spend what you have with a giving heart,  
And remember the poor, to do your part.  

Care for your soul—it’s your truest guide.  
Care for your body, but let your soul lead.  
Use the world’s help, but let virtue decide.  
In all your caring, take careful heed:  
Let nothing keep you from heaven’s door,  
Where joy and peace last evermore.

Attend to thy soul as the jewel of utmost worth, fashioned for communion with the transcendent and attuned to the harmonies of divine potency. Let it remain untainted, abhorring the corrosive pull of vice, and by grace be drawn toward the luminous arc of virtue. Guard it with vigilance, lest through careless indulgence it be cast into the abyss of perpetual anguish.

Tend to thy corporeal frame, yet subordinate its care to the soul’s higher claim. Eschew excess; moderation sustains both flesh and spirit. Adorn thyself with humility, for clothing should serve propriety, not vanity. Seek knowledge, and let ignorance be thy foe, cultivating the mind without impoverishing the essence of thy being.

Engage with the world not as an end but as a means. Pursue no path of cunning that veers toward oppression or cruelty; wield thy strength not to subdue but to uphold justice, and discharge thy obligations without delay. Distribute thy wealth with discernment, remembering the plight of the impoverished as thou measurest thy own sufficiency.

In all thy cares, let virtue dictate thy measure. Care for the soul, the body, and the world as interconnected spheres, yet never permit thy attentions to these lesser domains to eclipse the celestial inheritance awaiting thee. Let thy care be the tether that secures thee to heaven and its eternal beatitude.

Guard thy soul, treasure beyond all worth,  
Forged to revel in the spark divine.  
Let no shadow stain its holy birth,  
Turn it from sin, let virtue’s stars align.  
Guard it so no reckless step or snare  
Should hurl it down to the abyss of despair.  

Tend thy flesh, yet for thy soul’s fair throne;  
Shun excess—plain bread will serve thee best.  
Dress to humble pride, not boast alone,  
Seek wisdom’s light, let ignorance be detest.  
Feed and clothe the body, but take great care—  
To harm thy soul would lay both to despair.  

Turn to the world, but only as is just;  
Twist not thy wit to carve out crooked gains.  
Spare the weak from the hammer of thy lust,  
And give what’s due, let not greed forge thy chains.  
Spend with thy means, yet still thy heart implore:  
Forget not the poor who linger at thy door.  

Guard thy soul—it holds thy life’s true stay.  
Guard thy flesh, but for thy soul’s delight.  
Turn to the world, but tread the narrow way,  
Let virtue guide and keep thy path aright.  
Guard all with care, and let this truth be plain:  
No care should bar thee from the heavenly plain.

Care for thy soul as thing of greatest price, Made to the end to taste of power divine, Devoid of guilt, abhorring sin and vice, Apt by Gods grace to virtue to incline. Care for it so as by thy retchless train It be not brought to taste eternal pain. Care for thy corse, but chiefly for souls sake; Cut off excess, sustaining food is best; To vanquish pride but comely clothing take; Seek after skill, deep ignorance detest. Care so, I say, the flesh to feed and clothe That thou harm not thy soul and body both. Care for the world to do thy body right; Rack not thy wit to win thy wicked ways; Seek not to oppress the weak by wrongful might; To pay thy due do banish all delays. Care to dispend according to thy store, And in like sort be mindful of the poor. Care for thy soul, as for thy chiefest stay; Care for thy body for thy souls avail; Care for the world for bodys help alway; Care yet but so as virtue may prevail. Care in such sort that thou be sure of this: Care keep thee not from heaven and heavenly bliss.

Nurture thy spirit as the most precious entity, crafted to ultimately partake in the divine essence, untainted by guilt and repulsed by moral decay, naturally inclined, through divine grace, toward virtue. Tend to it with such fervor that it is not condemned to endure eternal torment. Attend to thy corporeal vessel, yet prioritize the well-being of the soul; eliminate excess, for moderation sustains, and to conquer hubris, adorn thyself with modest raiment; pursue knowledge, while casting aside the shackles of ignorance. Attend to the nourishment and attire of the flesh in such a manner that neither soul nor body suffers detriment. Engage with the world to ensure the corporeal thrives; do not torment your intellect in pursuit of nefarious ends; refrain from subjugating the vulnerable through unjust power; expunge procrastination in fulfilling your obligations. Allocate resources judiciously, remaining ever mindful of the destitute. Care for your soul, as it is your paramount foundation; care for your body for the benefit of the soul; care for the world for the continual aid of the body; yet let your care be such that virtue remains supreme. Exercise care in this manner, ensuring that your attentiveness does not obstruct your path to celestial realms and the bliss of the divine.

Take good care of your soul. It is the most valuable part of you, made to connect with something divine. Keep it free from guilt and away from sin. With God’s help, it can grow in goodness. Look after it so that you don’t face endless suffering.

Also, care for your body, but remember your soul comes first. Cut back on excess; simple food is best. Avoid pride and wear nice but modest clothes. Strive to learn and steer clear of ignorance. Make sure to feed and dress your body without harming your soul.

Think about the world, too, to take care of your body. Don’t stress yourself chasing after bad paths. Do not take advantage of the weak. Pay what you owe and don’t delay in helping others. Spend wisely according to what you have, and remember to help those in need.

Take care of your soul as your most important support. Look after your body for your soul’s benefit. Care for the world to help your body always. Yet, do all this in a way that lets goodness shine through. Care in a way that ensures you don’t miss out on heaven and its joy.
