Time only cause of my unrest By whom I hopd once to bee blest How cruell art thou turned? That first gavst lyfe unto my love, And still a pleasure nott to move Or change though ever burned; Have I thee slackd, or left undun One loving rite, and soe have wunn Thy rage or bitter changing? That now noe minutes I shall see, Wherein I may least happy bee Thy favors soe estranging. Blame thy self, and nott my folly, Time gave time butt to bee holly; True love such ends best loveth Unworhty love doth seeke for ends A worthy love butt worth pretends Nor other thoughts itt proveth: Then stay thy swiftnes cruell time, And lett mee once more blessed clime To joy, that I may prayse thee: Lett mee pleasure sweetly tasting Joy in love, and faith nott wasting And on fames wings Ile rayse thee: Never shall thy glory dying Bee untill thine owne untying That time noe longer liveth; Tis a gaine such tyme to lend: Since soe thy fame shall never end Butt joy for what she giveth.

Time, the sole cause of my unrest,  
From whom I once hoped to be blessed,  
How mercilessly you have turned—  
You who first gave life to my love,  
Yet remain unmoved, unchanging,  
Though ever consuming in desire.  

Have I been negligent or failed  
In fulfilling love's proper rites,  
Thus provoking your anger and bitter change?  
Now no moment shall I witness  
In which I may be even slightly blessed,  
Your favor so distantly withdrawn.  

Do not hold my folly to account;  
The fault lies with you, not with me.  
Time was given to serve holiness,  
For true love pursues no selfish ends.  
It seeks only worth and sincerity,  
Testing no thoughts beyond these.  

Therefore, restrain your cruel speed,  
And grant me once more a blessed ascent  
To joy, that I may honor you.  
Let me savor pleasure without decay,  
Rejoice in steadfast love and faith,  
And through my praise, lift your renown.  

Your glory shall endure undiminished,  
Lasting until your own cessation,  
When time itself shall cease to be.  
Such time, freely lent, is no loss,  
For it ensures your fame eternal,  
And joy in what you bestow.

Therefore, restrain your cruel speed,  
And grant me once more a blessed ascent  
To joy, that I may honor you.  
Let me savor pleasure without decay,  
Rejoice in steadfast love and faith,  
And through my praise, lift your renown.  

Your glory shall endure undiminished,  
Lasting until your own cessation,  
When time itself shall cease to be.  
Such time, freely lent, is no loss,  
For it ensures your fame eternal,  
And joy in what you bestow.

Time, the sole cause of my unrest,  
From whom I once hoped to be blessed,  
How mercilessly you have turned—  
You who first gave life to my love,  
Yet remain unmoved, unchanging,  
Though ever consuming in desire.  

Have I been negligent or failed  
In fulfilling love's proper rites,  
Thus provoking your anger and bitter change?  
Now no moment shall I witness  
In which I may be even slightly blessed,  
Your favor so distantly withdrawn.  

Do not hold my folly to account;  
The fault lies with you, not with me.  
Time was given to serve holiness,  
For true love pursues no selfish ends.

Time, you were meant to bring me peace,  
A gift I once hoped would never cease.  
But how cruel you've become,  
You gave life to my love, it's true,  
Yet now you stand still, unmoved too,  
While my heart burns, undone.  

Did I neglect some sacred vow?  
Have I failed you somehow?  
Is that why you've turned so cold?  
No longer granting even a moment,  
Not a single instant of contentment,  
Your kindness withheld, untold.  

Don't blame me—look to yourself.  
Time exists not for selfish wealth.  
True love seeks no fleeting ends;  
It only shows what worth it holds,  
Not chasing gains or stories told,  
But proving what it defends.  

So slow your pace, relentless time,  
Let me climb again to joy’s high rhyme,  
To taste sweet pleasure once more.  
Let love and faith renew their place,  
And I’ll lift your name with endless praise,  
Your fame forever secure.  

Your glory shall never fade away,  
Not until your final day,  
When time itself is gone.  
Grant us moments rich and true,  
For lending time is gain for you,  
In joy, your name lives on.

Time, architect of my disquiet,  
Once the lodestar of my aspiring spirit—  
By what grim alchemy hast thou transfigured,  
Thou who first didst quicken the flame of my devotion,  
Yet now, immutable in thy austere pleasure,  
Dost watch unyielding as I smolder?  
Have I, through negligence or omission,  
Failed some sacred ordinance of affection,  
And thus provoked thy tempestuous caprice?  
For now, no fragment of thy measure permits me  
Even the faintest reprieve of felicity,  
Thy countenance grown so estranged.  

Accuse not my frailty but thine own essence,  
For thou wert allotted to sanctify moments, not to squander them.  
True love, uncorrupted, seeks no terminal purpose,  
But rests in the equilibrium of worth itself;  
Unworthy love, by contrast, craves utility,  
And thereby betrays its shallow constitution.  

Pause, then, thou merciless arbiter of flux,  
And grant me once more the summit of delight,  
That I might hymn thy virtues anew:  
Permit me the nectar of unblemished love,  
Faith undiminished, joy undiluted,  
And I shall lift thee upon fame’s eternal wings.  

Thy renown shall not fade while thou endurest,  
For only with thine own cessation shall it falter—  
Yet such a gift as love’s renewal, freely bestowed,  
Would render thy glory imperishable,  
And enshrine thy name in the annals of joy unending.

O Time, the restless hand that shakes my peace,  
Once I thought you’d grant me sweet release,  
But how sharp and strange you’ve grown!  
You breathed the spark into my heart’s first flame,  
Yet now you stand, unmoved, the same,  
A cold king on a stony throne.  

Did I falter? Did I fail some vow,  
A sacred rite I’ve broken somehow,  
To earn your cruel disdain?  
For now, each fleeting moment mocks,  
No joy unlocks, no love unshocks—  
Your favor turns to pain.  

Blame yourself, not my heart’s design;  
You gave the hours to grow divine,  
For love that seeks no gain.  
The shallow heart may chase an end,  
But true love holds, will not pretend,  
And walks a truer plane.  

So halt your rush, relentless Time,  
Let me climb, once more, the heights sublime,  
To taste joy’s sweetest kiss.  
Let love stand pure, let faith remain;  
Then I’ll sing your endless reign,  
And raise your name in bliss.  

Your glory shall not dim or sever,  
So long as love endures forever—  
A gift that Time redeems.

Time only cause of my unrest By whom I hopd once to bee blest How cruell art thou turned? That first gavst lyfe unto my love, And still a pleasure nott to move Or change though ever burned; Have I thee slackd, or left undun One loving rite, and soe have wunn Thy rage or bitter changing? That now noe minutes I shall see, Wherein I may least happy bee Thy favors soe estranging. Blame thy self, and nott my folly, Time gave time butt to bee holly; True love such ends best loveth Unworhty love doth seeke for ends A worthy love butt worth pretends Nor other thoughts itt proveth: Then stay thy swiftnes cruell time, And lett mee once more blessed clime To joy, that I may prayse thee: Lett mee pleasure sweetly tasting Joy in love, and faith nott wasting And on fames wings Ile rayse thee: Never shall thy glory dying Bee untill thine owne untying That time noe longer liveth; Tis a gaine such tyme to lend: Since soe thy fame shall never end Butt joy for what she giveth.

Time is the sole source of my unrest, by which I once hoped to be blessed. How cruel you have become, you who first gave life to my love and still remain a pleasure that does not change, despite the continual burning. Have I failed to complete any loving rite, thus inciting your rage or bitter transformation? Now, I do not experience any moment in which I may be least unhappy, as your favors have become estranging. Blame yourself, not my folly; time provided only to be holy. True love best appreciates such outcomes, while unworthy love seeks only for ends. A worthy love merely pretends worth and proves no other thoughts. Therefore, delay your cruel swiftness, time, and allow me once more to ascend to a state of joy, so that I may praise you. Let me savor pleasure sweetly, experiencing joy in love and maintaining faith, and on the wings of fame, I will elevate you. Your glory shall never perish until your own dissolution, for time shall no longer exist. It is a gain to offer such time; thus, your fame shall never cease, but will yield joy for what it provides.

Time is the reason for my restless heart. I once hoped it would bless me. How cruel you have become! You were the one who brought my love to life, yet you bring me no joy and only change. Have I not done everything right? Did I miss one loving act and now you are filled with anger? Now, every moment feels unhappy. Your kindness feels so far away.

Blame yourself, not my mistakes. Time was meant to be holy. True love finds joy in giving, while unworthy love only seeks selfish ends. A worthy love shows its truth. 

So please, slow down, cruel time. Let me feel joy once more, so I can sing your praises. Let me savor the sweetness of love, keeping my faith alive. On the wings of fame, I will lift you high. Your glory will never fade until you are no longer bound by time. It is a gift to have time. Because of that, your name will live on forever, bringing joy for all it gives.

Time is the reason for my restless heart. I once hoped it would bless me. How cruel you have become! You were the one who brought my love to life, yet you bring me no joy and only change. Have I not done everything right? Did I miss one loving act and now you are filled with anger? Now, every moment feels unhappy. Your kindness feels so far away.

Blame yourself, not my mistakes. Time was meant to be holy. True love finds joy in giving, while unworthy love only seeks selfish ends. A worthy love shows its truth. 

So please, slow down, cruel time. Let me feel joy once more, so I can sing your praises. Let me savor the sweetness of love, keeping my faith alive. On the wings of fame, I will lift you high. Your glory will never fade until you are no longer bound by time. It is a gift to have time. Because of that, your name will live on forever, bringing joy for all it gives.

Time, the sole architect of my disquiet, once promised the benediction of bliss. How cruelly have you metamorphosed! You, who first breathed life into my affection, now render pleasure immutable, even amidst the conflagration of longing. Have I, in a moment of negligence, neglected a single rite of devotion, thus inciting your wrath and capriciousness? Now, in every fleeting moment, I find no sanctuary from the depths of my discontent, as your favors recede into estrangement.

Direct your reproach inward, for it is not my folly that bears responsibility; time grants existence solely to the sacred. True love, in its essence, seeks not ulterior motives, while unworthy affection masquerades behind pretenses of merit. 

Thus, I implore you, halt your relentless march, cruel time, and permit me one final ascent to the realm of joy, that I may extol your virtues. Allow me to savor the sweetness of love’s embrace, to bask in unwavering faith, and upon the wings of renown, I shall elevate you. 

Your glory shall not perish until your own dissolution, for time, in its infinite cruelty, is a gift; thus, your legacy shall endure, rejoicing in the bounty it bestows.

Oh, Time, the restless architect of my turmoil, how cruel your metamorphosis! You, who once breathed life into my love, now wield a pleasure that burns yet refuses to budge or flicker. Have I, in my folly, neglected a single tender rite, and thus unleashed your wrath, your bitter transformation? Now, every fleeting moment slips through my fingers, painted with the hues of despair, for your gifts turn cold and estranging. 

Blame yourself, not my foolish heart; Time grants but the sacred space to be holy. True love delights in purest ends, while unworthy passion flails for empty goals, pretending worth like a broken compass. So, I implore you, halt your relentless march, cruel Time, and grant me one more ascent to joy, that I might sing your praises! 

Let me savor sweetness, wrapped in love, and faith not wasted, as I ride the wings of fame to elevate you. Your glory, never to fade, shall endure until your very unraveling, for in this borrowed time lies the treasure: joy for all that it bestows!

Sweet shades why doe you seeke to give delight To mee who deeme delight in this vilde place Butt torment, sorrow, and mine owne disgrace To taste of joy, or your vaine pleasing sight; Show them your pleasures who saw never night Of greife, wher joyings fauning, smiling face Appeers as day, wher griefe found never space Yett for a sigh, a grone, or envies spite; Butt O on mee a world of woes doe ly, Or els on mee all harmes strive to rely, And to attend like servants bound to mee, Heat in desire, while frosts of care I prove, Wanting my love, yett surfett doe with love Burne, and yett freeze, better in hell to bee.

Gentle shadows, why do you endeavor to bring pleasure  
To one who perceives joy in this wretched place  
As nothing but anguish, sorrow, and personal disgrace?  
To partake of happiness or your vain, pleasing appearance  
Should be reserved for those who have never encountered  
The darkness of grief, where joy's flattering, smiling visage  
Reigns as daylight, and where sorrow has never found room  
Even for a sigh, a groan, or the sting of envy.  
But upon me lies a vast burden of woes,  
Or perhaps all misfortunes conspire to rest upon me,  
Attending me as though bound in servitude:  
A burning desire paired with the chilling frost of care,  
Lacking my beloved, yet overwhelmed by love—  
Consuming and freezing simultaneously; better, perhaps, to reside in hell.

O shadowed calm, why cast your tender grace  
On one who sees in this grim, wretched space  
No hint of sweetness—only sorrow’s trace,  
A bitter torment clothed in soft embrace?  
Go, offer joy to those whose hearts know none  
Of grief’s deep night, where gladness, like the sun,  
Smiles full and bright, unbroken, never shunned,  
And sighs nor groans nor envy’s spite have run.  

But here—on me—a thousand sorrows weigh,  
As if all harm had marked me as its prey,  
Bound close, like servants chained to misery.  
I burn with want, yet freeze in care’s cruel bind,  
Starved of my love, yet drowning in love’s tide—  
Flames in my heart, frost in my soul—oh, why?  
Better to sink where hell’s raw fires reside.

Gentle shadows, why do you try to bring me joy,  
When all I find in this wretched place  
Is pain, sorrow, and my own disgrace?  
I cannot taste your joy or see your lovely view.  

Offer your comforts to those who’ve never known  
The dark weight of grief, where happiness  
Shines bright like day, untouched by sadness,  
Without even a sigh, a groan, or envy’s sting.  

But for me, a world of troubles piles high,  
As if all harm is bound to follow me,  
Clinging close like loyal servants.  

I burn with longing, yet freeze with care.  
I ache for love, yet am overwhelmed by it.  
To burn and freeze—better to be in hell itself.

O shadows, why do you labor to bestow solace upon one who discerns no solace within this desolate sphere, but only the burdens of anguish, shame, and the hollow echoes of disgrace? To savor joy or your alluring semblance is but a cruel jest. Reserve your gifts for those unacquainted with grief’s relentless dominion, where joy’s deceitful visage reigns unchallenged, and sorrow has never carved a refuge for sighs, groans, or the venom of envy. Yet upon me descends a tempest of woes, as though every ill conspires to anchor itself to my existence, attending me as bound vassals. Desire’s fire consumes, yet care’s frost encases me. Bereft of love, I am glutted by its absence, burning while frozen—an agony that makes infernal exile seem a kinder fate.

Why do you seek to bring pleasure to me in this wretched place, where I find only torment, sorrow, and my own disgrace? Show your joys to those who have never experienced the darkness of grief, where joy, smiling, and brightness prevail, and where grief has never found a moment to exist. However, upon me rests a multitude of woes, or else all harms seem to depend on me, serving my desires. I endure the heat of longing while experiencing the coldness of care. I lack my love, yet I am consumed by it, suffering as if I am in hell.

Oh, sweet shades, why do you flutter like butterflies, eager to sprinkle joy upon my weary soul, who finds delight here an illusion, a cruel trick of fate? In this wretched realm, where sorrow's shadows twist and turn, tasting joy feels like a bitter mirage, a teasing glimpse of light. Show your pleasures to those who’ve never known the night of grief, where laughter dances freely, and sorrow never dares to dwell. 

But alas, a mountain of woes looms over me, or perhaps I am the magnet for misfortune, my every desire a servant to my pain. I feel the heat of longing in my veins, while the cold grip of despair bites at my heart. Yearning for my love, yet drowning in a flood of longing, I burn and freeze, caught in this hellish tug-of-war. Better to be lost in the inferno than endure this frozen despair.

Oh sweet shades, why do you try to bring me joy in this awful place? To me, it feels like nothing but pain, sorrow, and my own shame. Let your beauty shine for those who have never known the dark nights of grief, where happiness and smiles are like daylight, and sorrow has no room to breathe. But oh, I carry a heavy load of troubles. It feels like all harm is tied to me, waiting on me like loyal servants. I feel the heat of desire while the cold of worry surrounds me. I long for my love, yet I am consumed by love and suffering. Sometimes, it feels better to be in hell than to endure this.

O ephemeral shadows, what compulsion drives you to extend your ephemeral joy to one who perceives only torment within this desolate realm? For me, this place is a crucible of sorrow and personal disgrace, rendering the prospect of delight as mere illusion. Bestow your fleeting pleasures upon those untouched by the nocturnal weight of grief, where joy's radiant countenance never casts its light—where sorrow is denied even a moment for a sigh or a groan, or the bitter pangs of envy. Yet, alas, a veritable universe of woes is anchored upon my being, as if all afflictions conspire to serve me, fervently igniting desire while I endure the frigid frost of care. Yearning for my beloved, I find myself consumed by a surfeit of love, burning yet freezing, for it would be preferable to dwell in the depths of hell than to endure this paradox.

O ephemeral shadows, what compulsion drives you to extend your ephemeral joy to one who perceives only torment within this desolate realm? For me, this place is a crucible of sorrow and personal disgrace, rendering the prospect of delight as mere illusion. Bestow your fleeting pleasures upon those untouched by the nocturnal weight of grief, where joy's radiant countenance never casts its light—where sorrow is denied even a moment for a sigh or a groan, or the bitter pangs of envy. Yet, alas, a veritable universe of woes is anchored upon my being, as if all afflictions conspire to serve me, fervently igniting desire while I endure the frigid frost of care. Yearning for my beloved, I find myself consumed by a surfeit of love, burning yet freezing, for it would be preferable to dwell in the depths of hell than to endure this paradox.

Oh, sweet shades, why do you flutter like butterflies, eager to sprinkle joy upon my weary soul, who finds delight here an illusion, a cruel trick of fate? In this wretched realm, where sorrow's shadows twist and turn, tasting joy feels like a bitter mirage, a teasing glimpse of light. Show your pleasures to those who’ve never known the night of grief, where laughter dances freely, and sorrow never dares to dwell. 

But alas, a mountain of woes looms over me, or perhaps I am the magnet for misfortune, my every desire a servant to my pain. I feel the heat of longing in my veins, while the cold grip of despair bites at my heart. Yearning for my love, yet drowning in a flood of longing, I burn and freeze, caught in this hellish tug-of-war. Better to be lost in the inferno than endure this frozen despair.

Whoso list to hunt, I know where is an hind, But as for me, helas, I may no more. The vain travail hath wearied me so sore, I am of them that farthest cometh behind. Yet may I by no means my wearied mind Draw from the deer, but as she fleeth afore Fainting I follow. I leave off therefore, Sithens in a net I seek to hold the wind. Who list her hunt, I put him out of doubt, As well as I may spend his time in vain. And graven with diamonds in letters plain There is written, her fair neck round about: Noli me tangere, for Caesar's I am, And wild for to hold, though I seem tame.

Whoever wishes to hunt, I am aware of a hind’s location; however, as for myself, alas, I can do so no longer. The futile effort has exhausted me to such a degree that I am among those who fall the farthest behind. Yet, despite my weariness, I am unable to detach my thoughts from the deer. As she runs ahead, I pursue her weakly, only to abandon the chase, for it is akin to attempting to trap the wind in a net. To any who wish to pursue her, I assure you that he will similarly waste his time. Around her fair neck, inscribed in plain lettering and adorned with diamonds, are the words: "Do not touch me, for I belong to Caesar, and though I may appear tame, I am untamed and wild."

Whoever burns to chase, I’ll tell you where she runs—  
a fleet and gleaming hind. But me? Ah, no, I’m done.  
The hopeless hunt has lashed me raw,  
left me gasping, lagging farther than the farthest claw.  
And still, my weary mind clings tight to her retreat,  
though she slips ahead, swift as the wind,  
and I, stumbling, falter in her heat.  

Enough! I let her go—what fool would try  
to snare the gale in a trembling net?  
Yet if any dare pursue, let him not forget:  
he’ll waste his hours, as I have done,  
for ’round her throat, where diamonds gleam,  
a message rests, cold and keen:  

"Touch me not, for Caesar’s claim I bear.  
Wild am I to hold, though I pretend to care."

For those inclined to pursue, I disclose the locus of a certain hind; yet, alas, such endeavors are forsworn to me. The fruitlessness of this toil has burdened me beyond endurance, leaving me among those who trail most distantly behind. And yet, no exhaustion, however profound, can sever my restless thoughts from the fleeting quarry. She evades, and I, enervated, give chase—but to persist is folly, for I attempt to enclose the ungraspable, to snare the wind within a net. Let any who would hunt her be assured: his labor shall prove no less futile than mine. Encircling her neck, inscribed in unyielding diamond script, one finds this decree: *Noli me tangere*—I belong to Caesar. Though she appears docile, she is untamable, an essence irreducibly wild.

If anyone wants to chase, I know where there’s a deer. But as for me, alas, I can hunt no more. The useless effort has worn me out so much, I’m always the one who trails far behind. Yet no matter how tired I am, I can’t pull my thoughts away from her. She runs ahead, and I stumble after, weak and faint. So I give up, knowing it’s like trying to catch the wind in a net. Anyone who wants to pursue her, I’ll be clear—he’ll waste his time just as I have. Around her lovely neck, in plain letters carved in diamonds, it says: “Do not touch me, for I belong to Caesar. I may seem tame, but I’m too wild to hold.”

Whoever wishes to hunt, I know where a deer can be found. However, I can pursue no longer. This futile effort has exhausted me greatly, and I find myself among those who lag behind. Nonetheless, I cannot divert my tired mind from the deer, as I weakly pursue her while she escapes. Therefore, I cease my efforts, since I attempt to capture something as elusive as the wind. To any who wish to hunt her, I must clarify that they will only waste their time. Inscribed in clear letters, around her graceful neck, are the words: "Do not touch me, for I belong to Caesar," indicating that while I may appear manageable, I am inherently untamable.

Whoever dares to hunt, I know where a hind hides, but alas, I can run no more. The fruitless chase has drained me to the bone; I am the weary soul trailing at the back. Yet my tired mind cannot escape the vision of the deer, as she dances away, elusive and enchanting. Fainting, I pursue her ghost. So I surrender, for in this tangled web, I grasp only at the wind. Whoever wishes to track her, I warn you now, you’ll only squander your time in futility. Inscribed in diamonds around her graceful neck are the words: Noli me tangere, for I belong to Caesar, wild at heart, though I wear the guise of the tame.

To any who wish to hunt her, I must clarify that they will only waste their time. To those who seek the elusive quarry, I possess knowledge of its whereabouts; yet, alas, I find myself incapable of further pursuit. The futile endeavor has exhausted my spirit to an extreme, placing me among the ranks of the most distant followers. Nevertheless, I cannot detach my weary consciousness from the figure of the hunted, as it escapes before me, compelling me to pursue in a state of exhaustion. Thus, I relinquish my efforts, for striving to capture the ethereal is akin to ensnaring the wind. To those who aspire to hunt her, I offer this certainty: their time is destined for futility. Inscribed in diamonds upon her graceful neck is a clear decree: "Noli me tangere, for I belong to Caesar, and though I may appear subdued, I remain wild at heart."

My true-love hath my heart and I have his, By just exchange one for the other given: I hold his dear, and mine he cannot miss; There never was a bargain better driven. His heart in me keeps me and him in one; My heart in him his thoughts and senses guides: He loves my heart, for once it was his own; I cherish his because in me it bides. His heart his wound received from my sight; My heart was wounded with his wounded heart; For as from me on him his hurt did light, So still, methought, in me his hurt did smart: Both equal hurt, in this change sought our bliss, My true love hath my heart and I have his.

My beloved possesses my heart as I possess his, an equitable transaction wherein each bestowed the other’s essence in perfect parity. His heart, cradled within me, renders us a singular entity, indivisible in purpose and being; mine, entrusted to him, directs his thoughts and animates his perceptions. He treasures my heart, recognizing its origin within his own chest; I honor his, for it resides in the sanctuary of my soul. His heart suffered its wound, inflicted by the mere radiance of my gaze; mine, in turn, was pierced by the echo of his pain. As his anguish emanated from me toward him, so it reverberated within me, a reciprocal torment binding us in shared vulnerability. This symmetry of sorrow forged the foundation of our felicity: my beloved holds my heart, and I hold his.

As his anguish emanated from me toward him, so it reverberated within me, a reciprocal torment binding us in shared vulnerability. This symmetry of sorrow forged the foundation of our felicity: my beloved holds my heart, and I hold his.

My true love’s heart beats in my chest, and mine in his—a perfect barter, sealed with breath and blood. I cradle his tender heart, and he holds mine fast, as if the world itself would crumble were it misplaced. No deal was ever struck with such divine precision. His heart nests within me, stitching us into one seamless thread; my own dwells in him, steering his thoughts like stars to their courses. He treasures my heart, for it once sang his name; I adore his, for it hums softly in my ribs. His heart was pierced, felled by the arrow of my gaze; mine shuddered, wounded by his pain. The hurt we bore was twin-born, a mirrored ache that bound us to our joy. My true love’s heart beats in mine—and mine in his.

My true-love hath my heart and I have his, By just exchange one for the other given: I hold his dear, and mine he cannot miss; There never was a bargain better driven. His heart in me keeps me and him in one; My heart in him his thoughts and senses guides: He loves my heart, for once it was his own; I cherish his because in me it bides. His heart his wound received from my sight; My heart was wounded with his wounded heart; For as from me on him his hurt did light, So still, methought, in me his hurt did smart: Both equal hurt, in this change sought our bliss, My true love hath my heart and I have his.

My beloved possesses the very essence of my heart, while I, in turn, hold his in reciprocal exchange; a transaction of souls that transcends mere possession. I treasure his heart, an entity of profound significance, and he cannot be bereft of mine; there exists no more favorable accord than this. His heart within me forges an indissoluble union, while mine in him orchestrates the symphony of his thoughts and perceptions. He cherishes my heart, once a fragment of his own, while I revere his, for it resides within my being. His heart bore the scars inflicted by my gaze; my heart, too, bears the imprint of his affliction. As his pain was cast upon me, I perceived the mirrored anguish within. Both our hearts, entwined in equal suffering, sought solace in this transformative exchange; my beloved possesses my heart, and I possess his.

My true love holds my heart, and I clasp his in return, a splendid exchange of souls, woven tight. I treasure his, a jewel of affection, while his spirit dances within mine, a seamless embrace. There’s never been a deal more divine, a celestial trade! His heart, alive in me, stitches us together; mine, a compass guiding his thoughts, his senses ablaze. He adores my heart, for it once beat in his chest; I cradle his, for it nestles safely within. His heart bears a wound, birthed from my gaze; mine aches, stitched with the very same thread. As his pain flickered from me like a star, I felt its echo rippling through my core. Both hearts, wounded yet yearning, sought the balm of bliss. My true love holds my heart, and I hold his.

Walking the fields a wantcatcher I spied, To him I went, desirous of his game: Sir, have you taken wants? Yes, he replied, Here are a dozen, which were lately taen. Then you have left no more. No more? quoth he. Sir I can show you more: the more the worse; And to his work he went, but 'twould not be, For all the wants were crept into my purse. Farewell friend wantcatcher, since 'twill not be, Thou cannot catch the wants, but they catch me.

While walking through the fields, I noticed a wantcatcher. Approaching him, I inquired about his efforts: "Sir, have you caught any wants?" "Yes," he answered, "Here are a dozen I have recently captured." I then remarked, "So there are no more left?" "No more?" he replied. "I can show you more, though having more is worse." He resumed his task, but it proved futile, as all the wants had already found their way into my possession. "Farewell, wantcatcher," I said, "since it cannot be. You cannot catch the wants; instead, they ensnare me."

Traversing the meadows, I discerned a wantcatcher at his craft. Approaching with curiosity for his peculiar endeavor, I inquired: Pray, sir, have you ensnared desires? Indeed, he affirmed, presenting twelve freshly captured. Then, I ventured, have you left none behind? None? he retorted. Nay, I can reveal more, though abundance breeds affliction. Resuming his labor, he found it futile, for the wants had migrated stealthily into my coffer. Farewell, diligent wantcatcher; your net avails you not, for the wants elude your grasp only to ensnare me instead.

Walking through the fields, I saw a man called a wantcatcher. Curious, I went up to him and asked, "Sir, have you caught any wants?" "Yes," he said, "I’ve caught a dozen just recently." "Then there are no more left?" I asked. He smiled and said, "No more? I can show you more, but the more there are, the worse it gets." He turned back to his work, but it didn’t help—somehow, all the wants had slipped into my own pocket. "Goodbye, friend wantcatcher," I said. "You can’t seem to catch the wants; they’ve already caught me."

Indeed, he affirmed, presenting twelve freshly captured. Then, I ventured, have you left none behind? None? he retorted. Nay, I can reveal more, though abundance breeds affliction. Resuming his labor, he found it futile, for the wants had migrated stealthily into my coffer. Farewell, diligent wantcatcher; your net avails you not, for the wants elude your grasp only to ensnare me instead.

Roaming the fields, I glimpsed a wantcatcher bent to his craft,  
And to him I strode, hungry for his haul.  
"Good sir," I asked, "have you snared some wants?"  
"Aye," said he, "a dozen, freshly seized."  
"Then none remain?" I pressed.  
"None? Oh, there are more," he laughed,  
"But more's a curse, not a prize."  
Back he turned to his snaring, yet all was in vain—  
For sly wants had slithered into my purse instead.  
"Farewell, hunter," I sighed, "your nets are undone;  
The wants chase me now, and I cannot outrun."

Walking the fields a wantcatcher I spied, To him I went, desirous of his game: Sir, have you taken wants? Yes, he replied, Here are a dozen, which were lately taen. Then you have left no more. No more? quoth he. Sir I can show you more: the more the worse; And to his work he went, but 'twould not be, For all the wants were crept into my purse. Farewell friend wantcatcher, since 'twill not be, Thou cannot catch the wants, but they catch me.

While walking through the fields, I observed a wantcatcher. I approached him, eager to know about his offerings. I asked, "Have you captured any wants?" He affirmed, stating, "I have a dozen that were recently acquired." I inquired whether he had any remaining. He responded, "No more?" I insisted, but he assured me he could demonstrate additional wants, though he warned that more would only worsen my situation. He began his work, yet it proved ineffective, as all the wants had already entered my possession. I concluded my conversation, acknowledging that he could not capture wants; rather, they captured me.

In my perambulations across the verdant expanse, I encountered a purveyor of yearnings, a wantcatcher. I approached him, fueled by an insatiable desire for his elusive quarry. "Good sir, have you captured any wants?" I inquired. "Indeed," he replied, revealing a dozen recently ensnared. "Yet you possess no more?" I challenged. "No more?" he echoed, "I can unveil further, yet more begets worse." As he endeavored to manifest his wares, I found that all the wants had clandestinely infiltrated my own purse. Thus, I bid adieu to the wantcatcher, for in this dynamic, it is not he who captures the wants, but rather, they ensnare me.

Strolling through the sun-drenched fields, I stumbled upon a wantcatcher, his presence a shimmering mirage. Eagerly, I approached, heart racing for his bounty. “Sir, have you snared any wants?” “Indeed,” he answered, eyes twinkling, “Here lie a dozen, freshly captured.” “But surely, there are no more?” “No more?” he echoed, a glint of mischief in his gaze. “I can unveil a trove, but beware—the more you seek, the worse it becomes.” Yet, as he toiled, my purse grew heavy, devouring every wish. “Farewell, dear wantcatcher, this dance is futile; you cannot ensnare my desires, for they ensnare me.”

Now in the West the slender moon lies low, And now Orion glimmers through the trees, Clearing the earth with even pace and slow, And now the stately-moving Pleiades, In that soft infinite darkness overhead Hang jewel-wise upon a silver thread. And all the lonelier stars that have their place, Calm lamps within the distant southern sky, And planet-dust upon the edge of space, Look down upon the fretful world, and I Look up to outer vastness unafraid And see the stars which sang when earth was made.

The crescent moon, slender as a whisper, sinks into the western hush, while Orion flickers between branches, his stride steady, deliberate, sweeping the earth clean. Above, the Pleiades drift like queens, their brilliance strung on threads of silver spun through the endless velvet dark. Farther still, the southern stars, solitary and serene, burn like distant lanterns, while faint planet-dust fringes the edges of infinity. They gaze upon this restless, troubled world, and I, unshaken, lift my eyes to the cosmic vastness, to the ancient choir of stars that sang when the earth was born.

The Pleiades, with their majestic choreography, adorn the ethereal canvas, suspended like jewels upon a gossamer filament. Amidst the tranquil, limitless darkness, the solitary stars, steadfast sentinels of the southern firmament, cast their luminous gaze upon the tumultuous sphere below. I, in turn, elevate my vision towards the boundless cosmos, unperturbed, and behold the celestial harmonies that resonated at the inception of our world.

All around, the lonely stars, calm sentinels of the southern sky, glimmer like distant lanterns. Potuia, potuia White grave goddess, Pity my sadness, O silence of Paros. I am not of these about thy feet, These garments and decorum; I am thy brother, Thy lover of aforetime crying to thee, And thou hearest me not. Potuia, Potuia,  
White goddess of the grave,  
Extend pity to my sorrow,  
O stillness of Paros.  

I am not among those near your feet,  
Arrayed in garments and decorum;  
I am your brother,  
Your lover from a past time, calling to you,  
Yet you do not hear me.  

I have spoken to you in your solitude  
Of our shared love in Phrygia,  
Of the distant rapture beneath blazing noons,  
When delicate pipes  
Fell silent within the cypress shade,  
And the shepherd’s brown fingers  
Touched slender shoulders,  
While only the cicada sang.  

I have described to you the hills,  
The soft sound of reeds,  
And sunlight upon your breasts,  
Yet you do not hear me,  
Potuia, Potuia,  
You do not hear me.

Potuia, Potuia,  
Pale goddess of the silent tomb,  
Bend to my sorrow, O breathless isle of Paros.  
I am no stranger trailing the hems of propriety,  
No bound disciple cloaked in ritual.  
I am your kin, your long-lost flame,  
A voice from the furnace of forgotten time, crying out,  
And yet you do not hear.  

I have murmured to you in the hollow of your aloneness,  
Of our fevered days in Phrygia,  
Those molten hours when the sun burned high,  
The pipes fell silent beneath cypress shadows,  
And the shepherd’s hands, dark as earth,  
Brushed against narrow shoulders—  
Only the cicada sang.  

I have spoken of hills that whispered,  
Reeds that sighed,  
The sun that kissed your skin like prayer,  
Yet you hear me not, Potuia, Potuia,  
You do not hear.

Potuia, Potuia, White goddess of the grave, Please hear my sorrow, O quietness of Paros. I don’t belong with those gathered near you, With their robes and solemn ways. I am your brother, Once your lover, calling out to you, But you do not answer. I’ve reminded you of the hills, The rustle of reeds, And the sun warming your skin. But still, you do not hear me. Potuia, Potuia, You do not hear me.

Potuia, Potuia,  
Pale arbiter of sepulchral repose,  
Extend thy mercy to this desolation,  
O voiceless Parian sanctity.  
I am estranged from those who kneel at thy hem,  
Draped in their ceremonials and proprieties;  
For I am not mere supplicant,  
But thy kin,  
Thy erstwhile paramour, imploring thee across the abyss,  
And yet thou remainest deaf to my lament.  
In the cloisters of thy solitude, I have murmured  
Of our Phrygian raptures,  
The incandescent fervor of unrelenting noontides,  
When the delicate flutes fell silent  
Beneath the cypress canopy,  
And a shepherd’s sun-burnished hands  
Glided over yielding contours;  
Only the cicada’s endless dirge persisted.  
I spoke of the undulating hills,  
The reed-song’s gentle cadence,  
And the aureate light upon thy form;  
Yet thou remainest unmoved,  
Potuia, Potuia,  
Thou remainest unmoved.

O Potuia, O Potuia, solemn deity of the ashen sepulcher, extend thy compassion to my desolation, encapsulated within the tranquil void of Paros. I do not belong to this throng encircling thy feet, clad in their fineries and conventions; rather, I stand as thy kin, thy erstwhile paramour, lamenting in a voice that reaches thee in vain. In the sanctity of thy solitude, I have recounted our shared passions in Phrygia, the distant rapture of ardent afternoons, when the delicate melodies of the pipes surrendered to the embrace of cypress shadows, as the earthy hands of the shepherd traversed the contours of slender forms, leaving naught but the cicada’s solitary refrain. I have unveiled the undulating hills, the murmurs of the reeds, and the sun’s caress upon thy form, yet still, O Potuia, thou dost not hear me.

O Potuia, O Potuia, solemn deity of the ashen sepulcher, extend thy compassion to my desolation, encapsulated within the tranquil void of Paros. I do not belong to this throng encircling thy feet, clad in their fineries and conventions; rather, I stand as thy kin, thy erstwhile paramour, lamenting in a voice that reaches thee in vain. In the sanctity of thy solitude, I have recounted our shared passions in Phrygia, the distant rapture of ardent afternoons, when the delicate melodies of the pipes surrendered to the embrace of cypress shadows, as the earthy hands of the shepherd traversed the contours of slender forms, leaving naught but the cicada’s solitary refrain. I have unveiled the undulating hills, the murmurs of the reeds, and the sun’s caress upon thy form, yet still, O Potuia, thou dost not hear me.

Potuia, potuia, O pale specter of sorrow! Cradle my grief, hush of Paros. I do not belong to this throng at your feet, draped in their finery and false airs; I am your kin, your once-cherished, calling out to you, and you do not hear my lament. In the hush of your solitude, I have whispered tales of our love in Phrygia, those fiery afternoons where time melted under the sun's embrace, and the tender pipes fell silent in the cypress shade, as brown hands traced the curves of slender shoulders, with only the cicada's song for company. I’ve shared the secrets of the hills, the soft murmur of reeds, the sunlight dancing upon your breasts, yet still you do not hear me, Potuia, potuia, still you do not hear.
